**Summary.** The researchers successfully realized a Z3 discrete time crystal using a chain of 15 superconducting qutrits. By utilizing native chiral interactions, they were able to stabilize period-tripling oscillations and prevent the destabilizing effects of domain wall degeneracies. This work highlights the potential of qudit-based processors for exploring complex non-equilibrium phases.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates how engineering specific interaction symmetries (chirality) and utilizing higher-dimensional qudit (qutrit) hardware can access and stabilize exotic non-equilibrium phases of matter that are otherwise unstable in qubit-based systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of stabilizing higher discrete symmetry (Z3) discrete time crystals (DTCs) due to domain wall degeneracies that prevent the formation of stable, long-range order.

**Main result.** Demonstration of a robust Z3 DTC in a 15-qutrit superconducting chain, where native chiral interactions are shown to stabilize the period-tripling phase and prevent initial state dependence.

**Method.** Implementation of a Floquet chiral clock model using a 20-transmon processor with tunable couplers, utilizing microwave-driven gates, flux-pulsing for interactions, and many-body localization (MBL) to prevent heating.

**Model / system.** A 1D chain of 15 superconducting qutrits (spin-1) implemented on a 2D grid of 20 flux-tunable transmons and tunable couplers, following a Floquet chiral clock model with Z3 symmetry.

**Key observables.** Spin-1 magnetization, Fourier transform of magnetization (to detect period tripling), autocorrelators, and the Edwards-Anderson order parameter (chi_EA).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Kicking strength (g), chiral angle (theta), coupling strength (J), and system size (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system relies on many-body localization to prevent Floquet heating and assumes that the experimental decay is primarily limited by intrinsic system decoherence rather than gate errors.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the processor layout, coherence statistics, single-qutrit gate calibration workflow, magnetization spectroscopy (FFT), and the scaling of the Edwards-Anderson order parameter across different chain lengths.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of Z3 DTC stability, describes the superconducting qutrit hardware and chiral clock model implementation, details the calibration and control methods, presents the experimental observation of period tripling and the role of chirality, and concludes with phase transition analysis and scaling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Periodically driven quantum many-body systems can spontaneously break discrete time-translation symmetry, realizing discrete time crystals. To date, both experimental and theoretical efforts have largely focused on the simplest case of spontaneous period-doubling in $\mathbb{Z}_2$ discrete time crystals realized with qubits. This owes, in part, to the challenge of stabilizing eigenstate order in higher discrete symmetry ($\mathbb{Z}_n$) time crystals, due to the presence of richer domain wall physics. Here, we demonstrate the realization of a $\mathbb{Z}_3$ discrete time crystal by implementing a Floquet chiral clock model in a chain of 15 superconducting qutrits. Unlike the conventional Ising setting, our system features a tunable chiral angle that governs domain-wall dynamics, spectral degeneracies, and crucially, the stability of time-crystalline order. Using disordered nearest-neighbor chiral interactions, we observe robust subharmonic period tripling that persists across a wide range of drive strengths and is independent of initial state. Finally, we highlight the special role that chirality plays in our $\mathbb{Z}_3$ discrete time crystal -- in its absence, the system's Floquet dynamics exhibit a marked initial state dependence governed by domain wall degeneracies. Our results establish native qudit hardware as a powerful platform to access a broader landscape of non-equilibrium phases.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14250"></a>
### [Time Crystals in Coupled Exciton-Polariton Condensates](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14250v1)

**Authors:** Xuan Ye, Hong-Jin Xiong, Alexey Kavokin, Sanjib Ghosh  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14250v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `driven-dissipative phase transition` **5/5** · 🔥 `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14250_figures/2605.14250_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Schematic of the coupled microcavity setup. Two microcavity exciton-polariton condensates are coupled to a common exciton reservoir. Each condensate undergoes local linear gain and dissipation at rates g and κ, respectively, alongside nonlinear loss at rate ηI. The shared reservoir mediates correlated-incoherent gain and dissipation with rates JIg and JIl, while coherent quantum tunneling between the cavities occurs at rate JR.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14250_figures/2605.14250_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Time-crystalline and steady states of the system. (a) In the time-crystalline phase, the particle numbers oscillate persistently in the long-time regime for a representative parameter set P/κ0 = (2, 3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 1). (b) Steady-state behavior outside the time- crystalline region. For parameter set P/κ0 = (2, 3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 0.6), both particle num- bers converge to a common value, representing a symmetric steady state. In contrast, for P/κ0 = (0.5, 3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 1), the populations converge to different values. (c) Fixed points determined by Eqs. (5) for P/κ0 = (4, 3×10−4, 3.2×10−4, 2, 1). The initial conditions Ri = 10−3</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14250_figures/2605.14250_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: The phase diagram. (a) The green shaded region in the (γ, β) plane identifies the stable time-crystalline phase, with its boundary defined by the intersection of the instability thresholds for the in-phase symmetric (black), anti-phase symmetric (blue), and asymmetric(red) fixed points. (b) Bifurcation diagram versus γ for (p, ηR, ηI, JR)/κ0 = (2, 3×10−4, 10−4, 1), with JI/κ0 ∈[0.5, 2.0] along the horizontal purple line in (a). (c) Bifurcation diagram versus β for (ηR, ηI, JR, JI)/κ0 = (3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 1), with p/κ0 ∈[0.2, 4] along the vertical purple line in (a).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14250_figures/2605.14250_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Robustness against quantum fluctuations. (a) Green region: time-crystalline phase determined from the mean-field equations. Red dots: parameter sets yielding stable quantum oscillations. Blue squares: parameter sets for which the quantum fluctuations grow exponentially, indicating the breakdown of perturbation theory. (b) Stable quantum oscillations for (ηR, ηI, JR, ω, gIg, gIl, g, κ)/κ0 = (3 × 10−4, 10−4, 1, 104, 3, 1, 3, 1). (c) Exponentially growing quantum fluctuations for (ηR, ηI, JR, ω, gIg, gIl, g, κ)/κ0 = (3×10−4, 10−4, 10, 104, 30, 10, 30, 10).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper shows that time crystals can spontaneously emerge in coupled exciton-polariton condensates using only the inherent gain and loss of the microcavity. It provides a theoretical threshold for this phase based on nonlinearity ratios and proves that the resulting oscillations are robust against quantum noise. This discovery offers a new way to realize time-crystalline behavior without the need for complex external driving.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it identifies a novel mechanism for spontaneous time-translation symmetry breaking driven by intrinsic dissipation rather than external periodic driving.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether a time crystal phase can emerge in coupled exciton-polariton condensates without periodic external driving and determining its robustness against quantum fluctuations.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that time crystals can emerge via time-independent gain and dissipation channels, provided the ratio of Kerr nonlinearity to nonlinear dissipation exceeds sqrt(5/4), and show that this phase is robust against leading-order quantum corrections.

**Method.** The study uses a Lindblad master equation approach, mean-field analysis via coupled Stuart-Landau equations, Jacobian matrix stability analysis, and Bogoliubov perturbation theory to evaluate quantum fluctuations.

**Model / system.** The system consists of coupled exciton-polariton condensates in semiconductor microcavities, modeled by a Lindblad master equation including local linear gain, dissipation, nonlinear dissipation, and correlated gain/dissipating channels via a shared reservoir.

**Key observables.** Particle numbers (N_i) and phase difference (phi).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nonlinearity ratio (alpha = eta_R/eta_I), gain/pumping parameter (beta = p/J_R), coupling parameter (gamma = J_I/J_R), and the regime of gain-dominated systems (p > 0, J_I > 0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a mean-field approximation for the underlying dynamics and relies on the validity of Bogoliubov perturbation theory where quantum corrections remain small.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the microcavity setup; Figure 2 illustrates periodic oscillations and steady-state convergence; Figure 3 presents the phase diagram in the (gamma, beta) plane; Figure 4 compares stable versus divergent quantum fluctuations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of driving-free time crystals, describes the microcavity model and master equation, presents mean-field stability analysis and phase diagrams, evaluates quantum corrections using Bogoliubov theory, and concludes with a discussion on the robustness of the phase.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this paper, we show that time crystals can emerge in coupled exciton-polariton condensates without periodic external driving, enabled instead by incoherent gain and dissipation channels inherent to semiconductor microcavities. We present a full quantum description of these processes that recovers the established effective theory at the mean-field level. We analytically determine the mean-field phase diagram for the time-crystalline phase and find that its emergence requires the ratio of Kerr nonlinearity to nonlinear dissipation to exceed $\sqrt{5/4}$. Within this regime, the periodic oscillation of the particle numbers forms an attractor that is insensitive to the initial conditions. Numerical bifurcation diagrams reveal transitions between the time-crystalline phase and various steady phases, in excellent agreement with the analytical results. Using Bogoliubov perturbation theory, we evaluate the leading-order quantum corrections and find that, over a wide parameter range, these corrections remain periodic and much smaller than the mean-field background, thereby establishing the robustness of the time crystal.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper presents a method to improve the detection efficiency of ultrafast quantum light by optimizing the temporal weights used in homodyne detection. By accounting for detector response and electronic noise, the authors significantly enhance the observable squeezing and non-Gaussianity of pulsed quantum states.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonic quantum information as it provides a practical method to recover high-fidelity quantum states from noisy, high-speed experimental measurements.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Efficient homodyne detection of ultrafast continuous-variable quantum states is limited by slow detector response and electronic noise, which degrades the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and obscures nonclassical features.

**Main result.** By optimizing a temporal weight function using a generalized Rayleigh quotient approach, the authors achieved a 135% increase in SNR compared to constant weighting, leading to enhanced observation of squeezing and Wigner-function negativity.

**Method.** The authors formulate the optimization of the temporal weight as a generalized Rayleigh quotient problem to maximize SNR, utilizing a truncated Fourier-basis to regularize against sampling errors.

**Model / system.** An experimental ultrafast homodyne detection setup using a Ti:sapphire laser, a spontaneously pumped optical parametric oscillator (SPOPO) for squeezed vacuum, and a transimpedlamperance amplifier-based detector.

**Key observables.** Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), detection efficiency, squeezing and anti-squeezing levels, Wigner-function negativity, and noise statistics (skewness and kurtosis).

**Important parameters / regimes.** 80 MHz repetition rate, 75 fs pulse duration, 800 nm wavelength, 10 GSa/s sampling rate, and 13-18 dB SNR range.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Electronic noise is assumed to be stationary; the detector response is modeled via a circuit-based impulse response.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Detection scheme; Fig 2: Enhancement of nonclassical features vs SNR; Fig 3: Experimental setup; Fig 4: Detector characterization (power spectra and pulse response); Fig 5: Autocorrelation matrices; Fig 6: Performance comparison of weight vectors; Fig 7: Histograms of quadrature outcomes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of ultrafast detection, presents a mathematical optimization framework for temporal weighting, details the experimental implementation and detector characterization, and concludes with a performance comparison of different weighting strategies and noise statistics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ultrafast continuous-variable quantum states offer new opportunities for advanced quantum technologies, but efficient homodyne detection of these states remains challenging. Here, we present a method for efficient ultrafast homodyne detection by exploiting temporal correlations in detector signals. By optimizing the temporal weight used to extract quadrature outcomes, we achieve a substantial increase in the signal-to-noise ratio of ultrafast homodyne detection, thereby improving the detection efficiency. We analyze the autocorrelations of shot noise and electronic noise and determine the optimal weight by solving a generalized Rayleigh quotient problem. The optimal weight enhances the squeezing and anti-squeezing levels observed experimentally. These results highlight the importance of optimized signal processing for efficient quantum measurements.

</details>

**Authors:** Zhisheng Xu, Mengxue Li, Chunfang Sun, Gangcheng Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14394v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

For the spinning cavity, when the driving field is clockwise (counterclock- wise), a positive (negative) frequency shift ∆F &gt; 0 (&lt; 0) is generated via the Sagnac effect. </figure>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to create directional (nonreciprocal) entanglement between two magnon modes in a spinning cavity system. By leveraging the Sagnac effect from cavity rotation and the Kerr nonlinearity of magnons, the researchers show that entanglement can be precisely controlled and remains stable even at relatively high temperatures.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a theoretical framework for using mechanical rotation (Sagnac effect) and nonlinearities to engineer directional quantum correlations, which is highly relevant for developing nonreciprocal quantum networks and devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The generation and control of nonreciprocal magnon-magnon entanglement in a hybrid cavity-magnon system using the Sagnac and Kerr effects.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that magnon-magnon entanglement can be nonreciprocally enhanced or suppressed by tuning the Sagnac-Fizeau shift and the magnon Kerr nonlinearity, with entanglement remaining robust up to temperatures of 100 mK.

**Method.** The authors use Quantum Langevin Equations and a linearized effective Hamiltonian, applying a joint unitary squeezing transformation and solving the Lyapunov equation to quantify entanglement via logarithmic negativity.

**Model / system.** A hybrid system consisting of two Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) spheres coupled to a spinning whispering-gallery-mode (WGM) cavity, where the cavity rotation induces a Sagnac-Fizeau shift and the magnons exhibit Kerr nonlinearity.

**Key observables.** Magnon-magnon entanglement (logarithmic negativity), average photon number, and average magnon numbers.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Sagnac-Fizeau frequency shift (Delta_F), magnon Kerr coefficient (K_j), coupling strength (g_mj,a), decay rates (kappa), and bath temperature (T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is in a strong driving regime allowing for linearization, the Markovian approximation is used for input noise, and the relativistic dispersion term in the Sagnac effect is neglected.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the system schematic and coupling configuration; Fig 3-5 show entanglement dependence on Sagnac/Kerr shifts and detuning; Fig 6-8 show the effects of coupling strength and decay rates; Fig 9 shows the survival of entanglement with temperature.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the hybrid system, defines the Hamiltonian and linearized dynamics, derives the resonance condition via a squeezing transformation, analyzes the impact of various physical parameters on entanglement, and concludes with the robustness of the system against thermal noise.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Cavity-magnon systems, combining magnons and photons, offer a versatile platform for studying quantum entanglement and advancing quantum information science. In this work, we propose a scheme for generating nonreciprocal magnon-magnon entanglement in a hybrid system consisting of two yttrium iron garnet spheres coupled to a spinning whispering-gallery-mode cavity. By leveraging the magnon Kerr nonlinearity and the Sagnac effect arising from the cavity rotation, we show that the entanglement can be substantially enhanced, and the resulting entanglement exhibits pronounced nonreciprocal characteristics. Furthermore, our scheme demonstrates that the entanglement remains robust against thermal noise and persists at bath temperatures up to 100 mK. This work underscores the potential of spinning cavity-magnon systems as a versatile platform for realizing nonreciprocal quantum devices and facilitating the development of quantum technologies.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proves that the dynamical phase transition in spin squeezing is universal across different lattice geometries and dimensions. It identifies a new scaling regime for short-range interactions and shows that the transition can be controlled by tuning interlayer couplings, offering a way to engineer entanglement in quantum simulators.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a versatile route for controlling entanglement generation through interaction engineering and uncovers new scaling physics in the short-range regime, which is highly relevant for designing quantum sensors in Rydberg and trapped-ion arrays.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the universality of a dynamical spin-squeezing phase transition in power-law interacting bilayer spin models across different lattice geometries, dimensions, and coupling strengths.

**Main result.** The authors establish that the dynamical squeezing transition is universal across various 1D and 2D geometries and identify a new sub-linear scaling regime for the critical aspect ratio in the short-range interaction regime.

**Method.** The research combines Bogoliubov instability analysis using Holstein-Primakoff transformations with numerical simulations using the discrete Truncated Wigner Approximation (dTWA).

**Model / system.** The system consists of power-law interacting spin-1/2 bilayer XXZ-type models, including 1D ladders and 2D square, triangular, and honeycomb lattices, with interactions decaying as r^-alpha.

**Key observables.** Spin squeezing (variance of mixed quadratures), phase sensitivity, and the scaling exponent p of the minimal variance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Power-law exponent (alpha), interlayer coupling ratio (lambda), and the aspect ratio (aZ/L).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Bogoliubov analysis is a quadratic-order approximation that may systematically underestimate the critical aspect ratio due to the omission of non-linear corrections.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare Bogoliubov and dTWA predictions for phase boundaries, show scaling collapses for different lattice geometries and coupling ratios, and illustrate momentum-dependent dispersion relations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the dynamical transition, presents Bogoliubov-based analytical scaling laws, demonstrates universality via numerical scaling collapses across geometries and couplings, and discusses experimental implications for Rydberg and trapped-ion platforms.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent work has identified a dynamical squeezing phase transition in power-law interacting bilayer XXZ spin models, separating a fully collective phase with Heisenberg-limited squeezing from a partially-collective phase with universal critical scaling. Here we test and establish the universality of this transition along two qualitatively different microscopic axes: lattice geometry, by studying square, triangular, and honeycomb $2\mathrm{D}$ bilayers as well as $1\mathrm{D}$ ladders, and a symmetry-preserving rescaling $λ$ of the interlayer couplings relative to the intralayer ones. Combining a Bogoliubov instability analysis with discrete truncated Wigner simulations, we find that the transition persists across all four lattice geometries and over a wide range of $λ$ with critical exponents consistent within error, providing strong evidence for a genuine non-equilibrium universality class. The Bogoliubov theory recovers the previously identified scaling $a_Z^* \propto L$ in the long-range interacting regime $α< d+2$, and yields an analytical scaling $a_Z^* \propto L^{2/(α-d)}$ for the critical aspect ratio with system size for $α>d+2$, with $α$ the power-law exponent in dimension $d$. This uncovers a previously unrecognized sub-linear regime for short-range interactions. By tuning $λ$ we vary the interlayer coupling strength at fixed layer spacing, demonstrating that the dynamical transition can be driven purely through interaction engineering without modifying the underlying geometry. These findings provide a versatile route toward controlling entanglement generation in Rydberg-array, polar molecule, and trapped-ion platforms with applications in quantum sensing and simulation.

</details>

### [Effective Hamiltonians in Cavity and Waveguide QED from Transition-Operator Diagrammatic Perturbation Theory](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14100v1)

**Authors:** Mohamed Meguebel, Maxime Federico, Louis Garbe, Nadia Belabas, Nicolas Fabre  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14100v1>

Schematic illustration of how a JLM diagram (of con- stitutive JLM transition operators indicated in black) and its Hermitian branch (of constitutive JLM transition operators indicated in grey) combine to form a closed loop that returns the system to its initial state. For a given JLM transition op- erator, the Hermitian conjugate carries the opposite detuning. Here, we highlight this for ˆξ and ˆξ† and the same reasoning applies to all other JLM transition operators.</figcaption>

The first kind corresponds to terms of the form ˆξ† i ˆξi and ˆξi ˆξ† i , repre- senting either Stark or Bloch-Sieger shifts. There are exactly M/2 JLM diagrams associated with this first class. **Summary.** This paper introduces a new diagrammatic perturbation theory based on light-matter transition operators to derive effective Hamiltonians in the dispersive regime. It provides a systematic way to handle multi-photon processes and multi-qubit systems in both cavity and waveguide QED. The method is shown to be more computationally efficient than previous techniques for high-order expansions.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful, systematic tool for researchers in quantum optics and circuit QED to analytically derive complex multi-photon interactions and energy shifts in multi-level systems without the computational explosion of standard state-centric approaches.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for a systematic, higher-order perturbative framework to derive effective Hamiltonians in the dispersive regime of Cavity and Waveguide QED, specifically for multi-level/multi-qubit systems and continuous frequency spectra.

**Main result.** The development of a transition-centric diagrammatic perturbation theory (JLM diagrams) that provides a scalable 'toolbox' for constructing higher-order effective Hamiltonians and handles multi-photon processes more efficiently than existing methods.

**Method.** A transition-operator-based diagrammatic expansion using a resolvent method in Laplace space, combined with adiabatic elimination via controlled projections onto transition subspaces.

**Model / system.** Hybrid light-matter systems including Cavity QED (discrete modes) and Waveguide QED (continuous modes), involving N-level atoms or N-qubit systems interacting with quantized electromagnetic fields.

**Key observables.** Effective higher-order Hamiltonians, AC Stark shifts, effective qubit-quibit couplings, and multi-photon transition amplitudes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dispersive regime (large detunings), coupling strengths, frequency continua, and regularization parameters for handling divergences.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Hamiltonian is assumed to be time-independent, and the expansion is primarily focused on the dispersive regime where coupling-to-detuning ratios are small.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the schematic of dispersive expansion and adiabatic elimination; Figure 2 presents the building blocks of JLM diagrams; Figure 3 illustrates 1st and 2nd-order JLM diagrams; Figures 13-16 show the mathematical proof of complexity bounds and a comparison of efficiency against James' method.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the JLM transition operator framework, establishes the diagrammatic rules and perturbative expansion, develops the adiabatic elimination procedure, provides a combinatorial analysis of diagram complexity, and concludes with a comparison of computational efficiency against traditional methods.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose an adiabatic-elimination formalism in the dispersive regime based on a transition-centric perturbation theory. The perturbative expansion is recast into a diagrammatic framework, while adiabatic elimination is implemented through controlled projections onto transition subspaces. Our approach applies systematically at arbitrary perturbation order, and is suited to multilevel systems and multiple qubits in both cavity and waveguide quantum electrodynamics. It ultimately enables the explicit construction of effective higher-order Hamiltonians while bypassing important limitations of existing techniques, thereby providing a practical toolbox for multiphoton processes in the dispersive regime.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.14582"></a>
### [Quantum battery optimized by parametric amplification](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14582v1)

**Authors:** Fang-Mei Yang, Jun-Hong An, Fu-Quan Dou  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14582v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `Tavis-Cummings & cavity-many-emitter` **4/5** · `Dicke superradiance` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `correlated cavity matter` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

A flux-pumped superconducting quantum interference device, formed by two Josephson junc- tions each with energy EJ and capacitance CJ, is threaded by an external magnetic flux Φext and embedded in the res- onator loop to provide a tunable nonlinear inductive element. (b- d) The ground-state Wigner functions at different squeezing parameters r/δc = 1.1, 1.3, 1.5. Other parameters are δq/δc = 1, and g/δc = 0.05.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to optimize quantum batteries using two-photon parametric amplification in superconducting circuits. By using a squeezed cavity mode, the researchers show that charging power can be exponentially enhanced and the battery can be made more robust against environmental noise. This provides a scalable platform for high-power, stable quantum energy storage.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates how parametric control and squeezed light can be used to engineer light-matter interactions to achieve exponential gains in power and enhanced protection against decoherence in many-body quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Optimizing the performance of quantum batteries (QBs) by balancing the trade-off between high charging power and environmental decoherence/energy leakage.

**Main result.** Two-photon parametric driving exponentially enhances the effective cavity-qubit coupling, significantly increasing maximum charging power while providing stability against noise through squeezed cavity modes.

**Method.** The authors use a Lagrangian approach and unitary transformations (squeezing and rotating frames) to derive an effective generalized Dicke model, then solve the Lindblad master equation for open-system dynamics.

**Model / system.** A superconducting circuit system consisting of a two-photon-driven LC resonator (the charger) coupled to an array of transmon qubits (the battery), modeled via a generalized Dicke Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Stored energy (E), average charging power (P), maximum charging power (Pmax), steady-state energy, and Wigner functions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Squeezing parameter (r), coupling strength (g), cavity/qubit detunings, dissipation rates (kappa, gamma), and number of qubits (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Transmon qubits are treated as two-level systems; the Lagrangian is truncated to the fourth order; the system is analyzed in the context of strong and ultrastrong coupling regimes.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the circuit schematic; Fig 2 displays energy spectra and Wigner functions; Fig 3 shows energy/power evolution and scaling in closed systems; Fig 4 shows energy/power evolution and scaling in open systems under dissipation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the quantum battery problem, describes the superconducting circuit model and Hamiltonian derivation, analyzes the closed-system dynamics and scaling, investigates the open-system stability and dissipation effects, and concludes with the robustness of the scheme.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The parametric amplification enabled by two-photon driving constitutes a versatile platform for advanced quantum technologies. We present an optimized scheme for implementing quantum batteries (QBs) based on a superconducting circuit system, where a two-photon-driven LC resonator serves as the charger and an array of transmon qubits functions as the battery. Our results show that two-photon parametric driving exponentially enhances the effective cavity-qubit coupling, which in turn gives rise to near-degenerate energy-level structures and highly entangled quantum states. This significantly enhances the charging power and enables rapid energy transfer from the charger to the battery. Moreover, the engineered squeezed cavity mode and the associated quantum correlations effectively suppress environmentally induced decoherence, thereby delaying energy leakage and facilitating stable energy storage. The proposed scheme remains robust against practical experimental imperfections, such as parameter disorder and environmental noise, preserving its performance advantages. The work provides a feasible platform for realizing high-power, high-stability QBs and highlights the potential of parametric control in quantum energy technologies.

</details>

### [Matrix-Product Belief Propagation for continuous-state-space variables](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14522v1)

**Authors:** Federico Florio, Alfredo Braunstein  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14522v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `methods for driven-dissipative` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Panel a shows the original interaction network, panel b shows the dual representation of the factor graph (see [10], fig. 3). </figure>

Top: magnetizations of all nodes at all times (Monte-Carlo vs MPBP with Fourier decomposition). Bottom: Time evolution of the “time-shifted energy” of the system.</figcaption>
</figure>

Top: magnetizations of all nodes at all times (Monte-Carlo vs MPBP with Fourier decomposition). Bottom: Time evolution of the “time-shifted energy” of the system.</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: Average magnetization (left) and autocorrelation (right) on an infinite random-regular graph with degree 8 and with β = 0.3, Jk,i ∼Uniform[−1, +1], h = 0, m0 = 0.6.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The authors introduce Basis-MPBP, a numerical method that extends Matrix-Product Belief Propagation to systems with continuous state variables. By using a function basis expansion and MPS-based truncation, the method efficiently computes complex dynamical observables and large deviations in large-scale disordered networks. It serves as a computationally efficient alternative to Monte-Carlo simulations for studying rare events in continuous-state stochastic processes.

**Why it may be interesting.** The method provides a powerful tensor-network-inspired tool for simulating the non-equilibrium dynamics of continuous-variable systems, which is highly relevant for studying open quantum systems and many-body dynamics in the presence of disorder.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Extending the Matrix-Product Belief Propagation (MPBP) method to handle stochastic dynamics involving continuous or mixed continuous/discrete state-space variables.

**Main result.** The development of Basis-MPBP, a method that achieves linear computational complexity in network size and time horizon while providing a controlled approximation for continuous variables via basis expansion.

**Method.** A generalization of MPBP using a Matrix-Product State (MPS) ansatz combined with a tunable expansion in a Hilbert function basis (e.g., Fourier basis) and SVD-based truncation to manage bond dimension growth.

**Model / system.** Kinetic Ising dynamics on large sparse networks (including tree, random-regular, and Erdős-Rényi graphs) with real-valued random couplings.

**Key observables.** Marginal distributions, time auto-correlations, time evolution of energy and magnetization, and large deviation functions of magnetization.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Bond size (matrix dimension), basis size (number of expansion elements), time horizon, and network degree.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the accuracy of the basis expansion and SVD truncation; it is exact on acyclic graphs only in the limit of infinite bond and basis size.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the redesign of a factor graph for high-degree nodes; Figures 5-7 display magnetization trajectories, autocorrelations, and the large deviation law on various graph topologies.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of continuous-state dynamics, presents the Basis-MPBP formalism and its technical implementation via MPS and SVD, demonstrates its application to the Kinetic Ising model, and validates results against Monte-Carlo simulations and population dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Computation of observables in discrete stochastic, possibly conditioned, dynamics over large sparse networks is at the basis of a myriad of applications. The Matrix-Product Belief Propagation method allows a semi-analytical estimation of such observables with a controlled error that depends on the size of the employed matrices, called bond size. Its computational cost is linear in the time horizon and the network size for a large family of models with discrete degrees of freedom. Here, a generalization of this method to models with continuous or mixed continuous/discrete degrees of freedom is presented, using a tunable expansion in a Hilbert function basis. The computational cost of the method is linear in the network size with a prefactor that depends on the basis size and the bond size. The method's efficacy is demonstrated by employing a Fourier basis for a mixed continuous/discrete representation of the Kinetic Ising dynamics with real-valued random couplings, where intermediate ``local fields'' are treated as continuous. The accuracy of the method is verified via comparison with Monte-Carlo simulations. For this model, we calculate time auto-correlations, time evolution of energy and magnetization, and finally we estimate the large deviation function of the magnetization at a given future time.

</details>

### [QOuLiPo: What a quantum computer sees when it reads a book](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14188v1)

**Authors:** Christophe Jurczak

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: End-to-end pipeline on Giambullari’s 1544 treatise: (1) original page photograph, (2) fine-tuned vision-language-model OCR (VLM-OCR) at 2.48% character error rate (CER) [33], (3) k-NN text graph, (4) 2D QPU and 3D emulator registers, (5) bitstring histograms.</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: The corpus laid out along the rigidity axis ρ ∈[0, 1], between the fully fungible limit (ρ = 0, every selected unit has a structural substitute) and the unique backbone limit (ρ = 1, every selected unit is irreplaceable). Blue circles: natural texts (at the knn each text is most discriminating at; see Table 1). Red diamonds: engineered QOuLiPo texts written for this paper.</figcaption>
</figure>

Top row — QOuLiPo (engineered) texts: graphs designed by construction as exact unit-disk or unit-ball graphs. Bottom row — natural texts: k-nearest-neighbour graphs in multilingual sentence-embedding space. Red nodes are members of an exact MIS computed by ILP.</figcaption>
</figure>

Each marker is one engineered text; markers are slightly offset within each (tier, geometric-class, ρ) bucket so all 29 are visible. Red diamonds: corpus members run on the QPU (FRESNEL_CAN1). Blue circles: corpus members run only on the emulator. Marker size scales with N.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a framework for mapping the semantic structure of books onto neutral-atom quantum processors using the Maximum Independent Set problem. By creating an engineered corpus called QOuLiPo, the authors provide a new benchmark for tracking the scaling of Rydberg-based quantum hardware. The work bridges quantum computing and digital humanities through a new metric called 'rigidity'.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates a novel application of Rydberg blockade-based computing to non-physical, semantic graphs, testing the limits of hardware-native optimization and register engineering.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Exploring how a quantum computer perceives textual structure by mapping semantic graphs of books to physical atom registers to solve the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem.

**Main result.** The authors successfully used a neutral-atom processor to recover the structural backbone of both natural and engineered texts, introducing a 'rigidity' metric to quantify textual uniqueness.

**Method.** An end-to-end pipeline uses LLM-based OCR and embeddings to transform text into k-nearest neighbor graphs, which are then mapped to Rydberg atom arrays using pulse and register engineering.

**Model / system.** The physical platform is a neutral-atom quantum processor (Pasqal FRESNEL) utilizing the Rydberg blockade effect to implement the constraints of the MIS problem via the Rydberg Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Rigidity (rho), Backbone size (MIS/N), Approximation ratio, and Edge recall.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of atoms (N), blockade radius (Rb), k-nearest neighbor parameter (k), and cosine similarity threshold (t).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary information loss occurs during the 2D embedding of the semantic graph, and the structural conclusions are sensitive to the choice of embedding model.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the Rydberg blockade mechanism and mapping text to atoms; Figure 2 illustrates the end-to-end pipeline; Figure 3 plots the corpus rigidity; Figure 4 compares engineered vs. natural graphs; Figure 10 shows the impact of atom placement on the approximation ratio.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the concept of reading books via quantum hardware, describes the pipeline for graph construction and atom register design, presents the QOuLiPo engineered corpus, evaluates hardware performance on natural and engineered texts, and discusses the implications for the Digital Humanities.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

What does a book look like to a quantum computer? This paper takes eight classical works of the Renaissance and its late-antique inheritance -- from Augustine to Galileo -- and runs each through a neutral-atom quantum processor. The bridge is graphs: each textual unit becomes an atom, and graph edges are physical blockade constraints for engineered exact unit-disk designs, or a 2D approximation to the semantic graph for natural texts.   Three contributions follow. First, we introduce rigidity rho, a metric for how unique a book's structural backbone is -- distinguishing Marguerite de Navarre's Heptameron (rigid, twelve-nouvelle hard core) from Boethius (fully fungible, every chapter substitutable). Second, we invert the pipeline: rather than extracting a graph from existing prose, we pick a target graph the hardware encodes natively, and write a book whose structure matches it. The twenty-nine texts written this way, collected under the name QOuLiPo, extend the OuLiPo tradition to graph-topological constraints and, together with the eight natural texts, form a benchmark distribution against which neutral-atom hardware can be tracked as it scales. Third, we run both natural and engineered texts on Pasqal's FRESNEL processor up to one hundred atoms; engineered texts reach high approximation ratios, the cleanest instances returning the exact backbone.   A cloud-accessible quantum machine plus an agentic coding environment now lets a single investigator run this pipeline end-to-end. What is reported is an application layer, not a speedup -- humanistic instances ready to load onto neutral-atom processors as they scale, already complementing classical text analysis. The Digital Humanities community has a stake in building familiarity with this hardware now: the engineered-corpus design choices made today fix the benchmark distribution future hardware will be measured against.

</details>

**Authors:** Prasad Koviri, Tomoya Okita, Rina Yabumoto, Yuta Fujihashi, Masahiro Yabuno, Hirotaka Terai, Shigehito Miki, Kali P. Nayak, Ryosuke Shimizu  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14314v1>

**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `interference shaping light` **4/5** · 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

**Summary.** The researchers developed a method to synthesize high-dimensional frequency-bin qudits by manipulating the temporal structure of photon pairs in a bidirectional SPDC setup. They achieved precise control over frequency bin spacing and phase, demonstrating that these high-dimensional states can be reliably transmitted over a 1.3 km fiber network. This approach offers a versatile platform for scalable, wavelength-multiplexed quantum communication and sensing.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a scalable, low-loss method for generating and controlling high-dimensional qudits using the frequency domain, which is compatible with existing WDM telecommunications infrastructure.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of generating precisely controlled, high-dimensional frequency-bin qudits without the high optical losses or limited tunability associated with existing filtering or fixed-architecture platforms.

**Main result.** Demonstrated the engineering of high-dimensional frequency-entangled states with tunable bin spacing (12.5 GHz to 750 GHz) and phase, achieving a Hilbert-space dimensionality of at least 289 and proving robust distribution over a 1.3 km fiber network.

**Method.** Time-domain Fourier optical synthesis is used to manipulate the temporal structure of a biphoton wave packet via a nonlinear interferometer, effectively controlling the spectral modulation through Fourier duality.

**Model / system.** A bidirectional Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC) source using a type-II PPMgSLT crystal, where a pump pulse passes through the crystal twice to create temporally separated wave packets.

**Key observables.** Joint Spectral Intensity (JSI), Schmidt decomposition (Schmidt number K), Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference visibility, and frequency-bin spacing.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Frequency bin spacing (12.5 GHz to 750 GHz), Hilbert space dimension (at least 289), fiber network length (1.3 km), and White Rabbit synchronization precision (28 ps).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Low-gain regime of SPDC, classical treatment of the pump field, and perfect spatial mode matching between the two crystal passes.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the generation and detection setup including the SPDC source and SNSPDs; Fig 3 illustrates spectral phase tuning via JSI and HOM interference patterns.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of high-dimensional state generation, describes the experimental setup and the Fourier synthesis mechanism, presents the results of spectral engineering and dimensionality characterization, and concludes with a demonstration of state distribution over a campus-scale fiber network.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Frequency modes of light are one of the most promising platforms that provide access to high-dimensional quantum states amongst different photonic degrees of freedom capable of high-dimensionality, enabling robust, error-tolerant, and scalable quantum optical information systems. We demonstrate engineering of precisely controlled two-photon high-dimensional states entangled in frequency through time-domain Fourier optical synthesis. We generate and convert a continuous broadband frequency-entangled state into a large range of discrete frequency bins suitable for ITU standards, with spacings ranging from 12.5 GHz to 750 GHz, and observe spectral anticorrelations over 38 frequency bins, including intra-bin pure states at a 100 GHz bin spacing. We characterize the full quantum state dimensionality via Schmidt decomposition and observe lower bounds on the frequency-binned Hilbert-space dimensionalities of at least 289, formed by two entangled qudits with dimension 17. Furthermore, we demonstrate quantum nonlocality via frequency correlations in a transmission experiment over a campus-scale two-node fiber network. This work represents a crucial step towards building a versatile and relatively simple way of generating precisely controlled high-dimensional spectral qudits, with the potential of harnessing in wavelength-multiplexed quantum networks, high-dimensional information processing, and communication of quantum states specifically, and fiber-optic quantum remote sensing.

</details>

### [Spin chirality across quantum state copies detects hidden entanglement](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14515v1)

**Authors:** Patrycja Tulewicz, Karol Bartkiewicz, Franco Nori  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14515v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Chirality across quantum state copies reveals hidden entanglement. a, Chirality landscape: purity Tr[ρ2] versus negativity N versus |C4| for 107 random states (2×2 blue, 2×3 red). Black curve: pure 2×2 states. Dashed red: separable bound |C4| = 1/27. b, Detection architecture: controlled-SWAP circuits access negativity (NPT), the non-Hermiticity gap Dk (separable vs bound entangled), and the chirality fingerprint Ck (bound entanglement).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14515_figures/2605.14515_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Experimental detection of hidden entanglement on IBM Torino. a, |C4| versus negativity N for the parametrised family |ψ(θ)⟩in 2×2 (squares) and 2×3 (triangles). Solid curve: |C4| = 4N 2(1 −N 2). b, Werner state chirality |C4|(p). Blue diamonds: hardware data from multiplexed k-copy SWAP tests (k = 2, 3, 4; 10 circuits, 4,000 shots) with matched-depth |00⟩ calibration; all p values reconstructed from a single experiment using LU-equivalence of Bell states (Supplementary Section S5). Solid black: theory |C4| = 3</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14515_figures/2605.14515_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Bound entanglement detection via machine learning. Dataset: 13,600 Carathéodory-certified 3×3 states (6,800 BE from seven families, 6,800 SEP; eight features: Σ1, G1, D1, Σ2, G2, D2, C3, C4). a, Lasso LR coefficients (22/44 non-zero). Red/blue: increases/decreases P(BE). b, RF score P(BE) sorted by state: separable (blue), then BE families. c, Hardware scores (IBM Fez) using hybrid MLE. All BE states above P = 0.5. d, ROC curves. RF: 99.9% recall at zero FP. Analytical criteria: CCNR 40%, range 58%. e, RF confusion matrix: 99.96% recall, 0.06% FP. f, Lasso LR: 93% recall, 5.2% FP.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a method to detect undetectable bound entanglement by using spin chirality correlations across multiple copies of a quantum state. By combining these chirality features with spectral data in a machine learning classifier, the authors achieve near-perfect detection on real IBM quantum hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** It bridges quantum information theory and condensed matter by showing that entanglement detection can be framed through the lens of spin chirality, a fundamental concept in chiral spin liquids.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Detecting 'hidden' entanglement, specifically bound entanglement (PPT states) that is invisible to standard single-copy measurements and the CCNR criterion.

**Main result.** The authors prove that the difference between partial transpose and purity moments decomposes as a spin chirality-chirality correlator, and they develop a multi-channel machine learning classifier that achieves 99.9% recall for bound entanglement.

**Method.** The study uses controlled-SWAP circuits and randomized measurements to extract higher-order moments, combined with a Random Forest and Lasso logistic regression classifier trained on spectral and chirality features.

**Model / system.** Bipartite quantum systems in 2x2 (qubit-qubit), 2x3 (qubit-qutrit), and 3x3 (qutrit-qutrit) dimensions, implemented on IBM Heron-architecture superconducting processors (IBM Kingston, Torino, and Fez).

**Key observables.** Negativity, partial transpose moments, scalar spin chirality (C3, C4), realignment matrix singular values (Sigma), and the non-Hermiticity gap (D).

**Important parameters / regimes.** PPT (Positive Partial Transpose) vs NPT (Non-Positive Partial Transpose) regimes, CCNR-invisible bound entanglement, and noise levels (depolarizing noise).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) to account for hardware degradation and the assumption that the trained classifier generalizes to unseen bound entangled families.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the chirality landscape (purity vs. negativity vs. C4); Figure 3 illustrates the machine learning pipeline, including Lasso coefficients and Random Forest feature importance.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of hidden entanglement, provides a theoretical proof relating moments to spin chirality, describes the development of a multi-channel ML classifier, presents experimental validation on IBM hardware, and discusses noise robustness and efficiency.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Entanglement can hide in two fundamentally different ways. First, multi-copy correlations can carry information that no single-copy measurement on an unknown state is able to access. Second, bound entangled states possess a positive partial transpose, which makes them invisible to the Peres-Horodecki criterion and all moment inequalities that depend on it. Here we show that the moment difference between the partial transpose and purity decomposes exactly as a chirality-chirality correlator, where the relevant operator is the scalar spin chirality -- the same quantity that governs chiral spin liquids and the topological Hall effect. This decomposition identifies the specific physical structure that multi-copy entanglement detection probes. Using the same controlled-SWAP circuits, we develop a multi-channel spectral classifier for bound entanglement. The classifier combines realignment spectral features with chirality corrections and achieves 99.9% recall at zero false positives across all three known 3x3 bound entangled families, compared with ~40% for the CCNR criterion alone. We also introduce a marginal-noise construction that produces CCNR-invisible bound entangled states, which the classifier detects but which remain invisible to all single-parameter criteria. We validate our approach experimentally on three IBM Quantum processors and demonstrate negativity reconstruction with mean errors of 0.002-0.027, chirality detection for pure and mixed entangled states, and bound entanglement detection across two structurally distinct families (Horodecki and chessboard) on a single gate-based superconducting processor.

</details>

**Authors:** Johannes Mögerle, Frederic Hummel, Alicia Keil, Tangi Legrand, Eduard J. Braun, Henri Menke, Jonathan King, Beatriz Olmos, Sebastian Hofferberth, Hans Peter Büchler, Sebastian Weber

**Summary.** This paper introduces an updated version of the PairInteraction software, which uses MQDT and Green's tensors to accurately model Rydberg atom interactions. The new version is significantly faster and can handle complex divalent atoms and atoms near surfaces. It is a vital resource for designing and interpreting experiments in Rydberg-based quantum technologies.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a highly efficient, scalable tool for researchers designing Rydberg-based quantum architectures, specifically addressing the complex physics of divalent atoms and the modification of interactions by nearby surfaces/nanostructures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for an accurate, efficient, and scalable computational framework to model the complex single-atom properties and mutual interactions of Rydberg atoms, particularly divalent species and atoms in structured electromagnetic environments.

**Main result.** The development of PairInteraction v2, an open-source software that achieves a tenfold speedup in pair-potential calculations and provides a unified framework for modeling atoms near surfaces using MQDT and Green's tensors.

**Method.** A unified theoretical approach combining Multi-Channel Quantum Defect Theory (MQDT) for atomic states and static electromagnetic Green's tensors for interactions, implemented via a modular C++/Python architecture.

**Model / system.** The system involves one or two Rydberg atoms (alkali or divalent species like Sr and Yb) in free space or near structured environments like cavities or conducting plates, described by a Hamiltonian including external electric/magnetic fields and dipole-dipable interactions.

**Key observables.** Stark maps, Zeeman maps, pair-potential curves, van der Waals coefficients, Casimir-Polder shifts, and effective spin Hamiltonians.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Effective principal quantum number (nu), angular quantum numbers (l, j, L, J, F), interatomic distance, and atom-surface distance in the static limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interaction model assumes the static limit (distances much smaller than relevant wavelengths) and neglects exchange interactions at distances larger than the LeRoy radius.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the physical setup and Hamiltonian construction; Figure 2 illustrates MQDT concepts and channel coupling; Figure 3 shows state labeling via quantum number variance; Figure 5 benchmarks software performance; Figure 6 compares calculated and experimental Yb Stark maps; Figure 7 shows pair-interaction potentials.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific problem, presents the theoretical framework (MQDT and Green's tensors), details the software implementation and performance gains of PairInteraction v2, validates the model against experimental Yb data, and discusses applications to surface interactions and effective spin models.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Rydberg atoms provide a powerful platform for exploring strongly interacting quantum systems, both in free space and in structured electromagnetic environments, with growing applications in quantum technology. Accurately modeling their single-atom properties and mutual interactions is essential for interpreting experiments and designing new architectures. We present a unified theoretical framework for Rydberg atoms and their interactions based on multi-channel quantum defect theory (MQDT) and static electromagnetic Green's tensors. MQDT provides a precise description of Rydberg states of divalent atoms such as strontium and ytterbium, while the Green's tensor formalism provides a general and flexible approach for calculating interactions between two Rydberg atoms in arbitrary geometries, including modifications induced by nearby surfaces. We implement this framework in an updated version of the open-source PairInteraction software [Weber et al., J.~Phys.~B~50 (2017)]. The implementation leverages high-performance libraries and achieves speedups of one order of magnitude for pair-potential calculations compared to prior software. We demonstrate the capabilities of the framework through example applications to divalent atoms and show excellent agreement with experimental data for an exemplary Stark map of $^{174}$Yb. The modular software architecture enables the community to extend it further.

</details>

The unitary operation acting across the bipartition factorizes into UL ⊗UR, where UL and UR have support bounded by d near the left (x = 1) and right boundaries, respectively, up to unitaries that act on the bulk of A and B. (Bottom) Construction of the short-range entangled “bare” state |Φ⟩by systematically applying translated copies of the inverse boundary unitary, U † L, to the original state |Ψ⟩.</figcaption>

CL,k,A = d since there is a closed string that does not touch any incoming or outgoing leg of OA. The permutation matrix Pπ is an identity. (b) L = 6, k = 2, and the two-body operator OA acts on the 3rd and 5th sites. CL,k,A = d since there is a closed string that does not touch any incoming or outgoing leg of OA. **Summary.** The paper identifies a new way for mixed-state systems to exhibit long-range entanglement through a mismatch in the dimensions of symmetric subspaces. It demonstrates that the maximally mixed translation-invariant state possesses long-range bipartite entanglement and unique correlation properties. This mechanism is unique to mixed states and does not require traditional symmetry anomalies or long-range correlations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper introduces a novel, intrinsic mechanism for long-range entanglement in mixed states that is fundamentally different from known pure-state mechanisms, which is highly relevant for understanding the entanglement structure of steady states in open quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Identifying a new mechanism for long-range entanglement (LRE) in many-body mixed states that does not rely on symmetry anomalies or long-range correlations.

**Main result.** The authors discover that LRE can arise from a 'dimensional mismatch' between the exponentially growing rank of a translation-invariant mixed state and the polynomially growing dimension of the space spanned by short-range entangled states.

**Method.** The study uses dimensional counting, group theory (Schur-Weyl duality), and matrix product state (MPS) formalism to compare subspace dimensions, alongside information-theoretic measures like entanglement of formation and conditional mutual information.

**Model / system.** A 1D ring of L qudits (dimension d) with periodic boundary conditions, specifically focusing on the Maximally Mixed Invariant State (MMIS) within the translation-invariant subspace.

**Key observables.** Entanglement of formation, conditional mutual information, Rényi-index-dependent operator-space entanglement, and variance-normalized Rényi-2 correlators.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size L, local dimension d, and the thermodynamic limit (L to infinity).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often assumes L is a prime number to utilize the ergodicity of the p-shift; it also assumes the subregion size |A| is a finite fraction of L.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 visualizes the MMIS; Figure 2 shows the fidelity of the state approaching the steady state; Figures 4-6 illustrate the reduction of traces and permutations of indices in the subregion; Figure 7 shows the doubled Hilbert space representation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of LRE in mixed states, presents the dimensional mismatch proof, analyzes the entanglement and correlation properties of the MMIS, explores the permutationally symmetric subspace, investigates operator space entanglement, and concludes with the emergence of the state via a Lindbladian.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a new mechanism for long-range entanglement (LRE) in strongly symmetric many-body mixed states that does not rely on symmetry anomalies or long-range correlations. Our primary example is the maximally mixed state in the translation-invariant subspace on a one-dimensional ring. This state is LRE because translationally symmetric short-range entangled states span a subspace whose dimension grows only polynomially with system size, whereas the full translation-invariant subspace grows exponentially. We further discuss certain unconventional properties of this state, including logarithmically growing conditional mutual information, strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry-breaking, and Rényi-index-dependent operator-space entanglement. We also construct a geometrically non-local Lindbladian to stabilize this state as the steady state. Our results identify dimensional mismatch as a novel route to LRE that is intrinsic to many-body mixed states.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a programmable quantum memory using erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate that integrates efficient photon storage with high-speed electro-optic control. By leveraging the Pockels effect, the device can rapidly route telecom-wavelength photons, enabling the development of scalable, spectrally multiplexed quantum networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a scalable, integrated platform for controlling light-matter interactions and managing quantum correlations in a multiplexed network.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for integrated, high-efficiency, and programmable quantum memories at telecom wavelengths that can support spectrally multiplexed quantum networks.

**Main result.** Demonstration of a cavity-enhanced quantum memory in erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate that achieves 23.3% storage efficiency and enables high-speed (20 MHz) frequency-selective routing of entangled photons.

**Method.** The researchers used the Atomic Frequency Comb (AFC) protocol in an isotopically purified 167Er3+-doped thin-film lithium niobate microring resonator, utilizing the electro-optic effect for fast frequency control and verifying entanglement via a Franson interferometer.

**Model / system.** An isotopically purified 167Er3+-doped thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) microring resonator operating at cryogenic temperatures (260 mK) and high magnetic fields (1.855 T).

**Key observables.** Storage efficiency, AFC lifetime, inter-channel crosstalk, optical coherence time (T2), entanglement witness (W), and second-order cross-correlation function g2(0).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Wavelength ~1532 nm, storage efficiency of 23.3%, AFC lifetime of 277.6 s, electro-optic tuning efficiency of 1.11 GHz/V, and modulation rates up to 20 MHz.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the device layout, SEM, and optical characterization; Figure 3 illustrates high-speed routing pulse sequences and frequency-selective histograms; Figure 4 details the entanglement storage setup and verification results.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the device architecture and material properties, describes the spectral preparation and cavity characterization, demonstrates high-speed electro-optic routing, and concludes with the storage and retrieval of time-energy-entangled photons.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Spectrally multiplexed telecom quantum networks require quantum memories that combine efficient storage with programmable frequency addressing. An ideal integrated implementation should therefore unite a native telecom transition, efficient storage and fast on-chip spectral control. Here we demonstrate a cavity-enhanced quantum memory in an isotopically purified $^{167}\mathrm{Er}^{3+}$-doped thin-film lithium niobate microring resonator. Long-lived hyperfine shelving states support persistent, high-contrast atomic frequency comb preparation, with a single-component comb lifetime of $277.6 \pm 52.6$s. Together with cavity impedance matching, this yields an on-chip storage efficiency of $23.3 \pm 0.5\%$ for 100-ns storage. The intrinsic electro-optic response of lithium niobate enables frequency-selective storage and routing of retrieved photons at rates up to 20~MHz with inter-channel crosstalk below $10^{-4}$. We further store and retrieve time-energy-entangled telecom photons, violating an entanglement-witness bound by more than 11 standard deviations and thus verifying the quantum nature of the storage process. Our results establish erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate as a programmable light--matter interface for spectrally multiplexed quantum networks.

</details>

<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14959_figures/2605.14959_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 5: (a) FAR and (b) FRR as a function of the number of detected clicks and of the error correction threshold for 𝐷= 6 ports. Fabrication variations for the FAR are modelled through the distribution of voltages 𝑉%&amp;&#x27;&#x27;~𝑈(7, 8.6)𝑉. (c) EER as a function of threshold 𝑁( with the 𝑁)&#x27;*)+</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a silicon nitride photonic integrated circuit that functions as a quantum-secure Physical Unclonable Function. By leveraging fabrication-induced phase variations and single-photon states, the system provides a highly secure method for hardware authentication that is resistant to cloning and eavesdropping.

**Why it may be interesting.** It integrates quantum optical principles, such as the no-cloning theorem and the use of maximally mixed states, with integrated photonics to create a hardware-based security primitive.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a quantum-secure Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) using silicon photonics that is resistant to side-channel, cloning, and man-in-the-middle attacks.

**Main result.** Demonstrated a silicon nitride (SiN) photonic mesh acting as a PUF with extremely low error rates (EER as low as 10^-14) and validated a quantum readout protocol that protects the device fingerprint using single-photon states.

**Method.** The researchers used experimental extraction of fabrication-induced phase offsets in a SiN MZI mesh and combined this with a quantum protocol using single-photon states and maximally mixed states, validated via Monte Carlo simulations.

**Model / system.** A 6x6 rectangular silicon nitride (SiN) programmable photonic Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) mesh consisting of 15 balanced MZIs and 13 external phase shifters, where security arises from uncontrollable waveguide variations.

**Key observables.** False Acceptance Rate (FAR), False Rejection Rate (FRR), Equal Error Rate (EER), inter-Hamming distance, and intra-Hamming distance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of detected clicks (N_clicks), error correction threshold (N_B), total propagation loss (~11 dB), and single-photon detector efficiency (25%).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The security assumes the no-cloning theorem applies to the readout and that the adversary's view of the input is a maximally mixed state due to random input port driving.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the MZI mesh schematic; Fig 2 displays MZI power output and the distribution of nullification voltages; Fig 3 shows Gaussian probability density functions for Hamming distances; Fig 5 illustrates FAR and FRR trends.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the vulnerability of classical PUFs, describes the SiN photonic platform and its fabrication-induced fingerprint, details the quantum readout protocol and security analysis against various attacks, and concludes with statistical validation of error rates.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) are hardware security primitives whose inherent physical complexity can be exploited for secure authentication and cryptographic key generation. Silicon photonic devices, owing to their suitability for quantum and artificial intelligence applications alongside standard CMOS fabrication processes, constitute a highly promising substrate for integrated multifunctional PUFs. Despite the advanced security guarantees offered by quantum cryptographic protocols and the central role of silicon photonics in quantum technologies, quantum readout strategies based on single-photon states for photonic PUFs remain largely unexplored. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate a silicon nitride (SiN) programmable photonic Mach Zehnder interferometer mesh that implements a unitary transformation and operates as a PUF, whose secret physical signature arises from uncontrollable waveguide variations during fabrication. Using experimentally derived parameters from the SiN integrated mesh, we further introduce and numerically evaluate a quantum readout protocol that combines single-photon states with PUFs. Maximally mixed quantum states are employed to conceal the underlying unitary transformation from passive eavesdropping. Security against adversaries possessing devices fabricated under similar conditions is assessed, with authentication performance quantified through Monte Carlo analysis of the false acceptance and false rejection rates as a function of the number of detected events and corrected errors. The results indicate exceptional performance with equal error rates as low as 10 to the minus 14, highlighting the potential of quantum secure PUFs for high security authentication applications.

</details>

### [Neural Fields for NV-Center Inverse Sensing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13988v1)

**Authors:** Zhixuan Zhao, Tao Zhong, Yixun Hu, Nathalie P. de Leon, Christine Allen-Blanchette  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13988v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **4/5** · `QC/QI experiment` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13988_figures/2605.13988_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">

</figure>
