<img src="2605.13988_figures/2605.13988_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: NeTMY pipeline. Coordinates pass through annealed Fourier features and a coordinate MLP fθ to a density ρ and Larmor field ωL; a differentiable forward operator F predicts a spectrum compared with the observation under loss L. The same MLP is trained per instance across two resolutions in sequence; gradients flow back through the operator, with no paired training data.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13988_figures/2605.13988_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Qualitative reconstructions on two F3-generated samples inverted under F2. Free-density solvers center-collapse; HybridNeTMY and NeTMY preserve off-center structure.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13988_figures/2605.13988_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Optimization-geometry diagnostics. (a) Epoch-200 reconstructions. (b) Loss along ρ(t) = (1 −t)ρcollapse + t ρ⋆. (c) Center-mass ratio at epoch 200.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13988_figures/2605.13988_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 3 illustrates the pixel-level pattern: free-density solvers execute the centered iter-0 gradient of (P2)–(P3) and end at a centrally collapsed minimum, while NeTMY preserves off-center structure and reconstructs finer details. The matched-operator results in Table 2 reuse the inversion operator as the data-generation operator and are therefore subject to the inverse-crime caveat [35]. More qualitative examples are in Appendix E.11. We use Table 1 as primary evidence and the real-data check (Section 5.5) as a simulator-free test.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces NeTMY, a neural field-based method for reconstructing spin densities from NV-center magnetic noise spectra. It identifies a specific optimization failure mode called 'center-collapse' caused by physically accurate dipolar operators and shows how a coordinate-based neural network can mitigate this by acting as a structured filter. The method outperforms both classical regularized solvers and supervised deep learning models in both synthetic and real-world experimental settings.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a sophisticated example of using machine learning (neural fields) to solve highly non-linear, physically-constrained inverse problems in quantum sensing, specifically addressing how the choice of physical operator affects the optimization geometry.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the ill-posed inverse problem of reconstructing spatial spin-source density and Larmor frequency from noisy NV-center magnetic noise spectra, specifically overcoming the 'center-collapse' optimization failure in traditional solvers.

**Main result.** The proposed NeTMY method achieves superior localization and distributional accuracy compared to classical and supervised baselines, demonstrating that neural field parameterization acts as a beneficial structural filter that mitigates pathological gradient updates.

**Method.** NeTMY uses an amortization-free, coordinate-based neural field (MLP) with annealed Fourier positional encoding, multiscale optimization, and a physics-informed architecture including density gating and energy-anchored scale correction.

**Model / system.** The system involves Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) center arrays in diamond used for nanoscale magnetic noise sensing, modeled via a dipolar forward operator that maps spin-source density to frequency-dependent magnetic noise spectra.

**Key observables.** Frequency-dependent noise spectrum, spin-source density, Larmor frequency, and localization metrics (GMSD, Hungarian F1, Sliced Wasserstein distance).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Standoff height (z0), Larmor frequency (omega_L), linewidth (gamma), and the distinction between scalar (F1) and tensor (F2) forward operators.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis focuses on the fluctuation-dominated dipolar regime and assumes the tensor operator is exact for locally constant Larmor frequencies.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the NV-center sensing task, the NeTMY architecture, the impact of operator fidelity on optimization landscapes, the superiority of NeTMY in cross-fidelity benchmarks, and specific failure modes like axis-aligned artifacts and high-frequency leakage.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the inverse sensing problem, analyzes the physical forward models (F1 vs F2), identifies the 'center-collapse' pathology, presents the NeTMY architecture and optimization strategy, evaluates performance via cross-fidelity and matched-operator benchmarks, validates with real-world alpha-RuCl3 data, and discusses limitations and failure modes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Inverse problems in scientific sensing are often solved with either hand-designed regularizers or supervised networks trained on simulated labels, yet both can fail when the forward model is nonlinear, spectrally coupled, and physically delicate. We study this issue for noise sensing based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, where a quantum sensor measures magnetic-noise spectra generated by sparse spin sources. We show that replacing a common scalar/coherent forward approximation with a tensor power-summed dipolar operator changes the inverse landscape and exposes a center-collapse failure mode in free-density optimization. We propose NeTMY, an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to the differentiable NV forward model, with annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating, and spectrum-fidelity losses. Across sparse synthetic reconstructions generated by the corrected operator, NeTMY achieves the best localization and distributional metrics in the tested benchmark. Mechanism experiments show that NeTMY does not directly execute the raw density-space gradient; its parameterization smooths and redistributes updates, mitigating the center-collapse pathology. These results position NV quantum sensing as a useful testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse problems.

</details>

**Authors:** B. Göblyös, S. Kollarics, R. Kucsera, D. Plitt, K. Koltai, B. G. Márkus, L. Forró, F. Simon  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14697v1>

The data was obtained by compiling several individ- ual frequency-swept datasets at given magnetic fields. c) calculated magnetic field dependent energy dispersion data for all four possible orientations when the magnetic field points in the ϑ = 16.9◦, ϕ = 61◦</figcaption>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 4. Schematics of the wavelength-resolved ODMR instrument. A 532 nm laser excites the diamond sample through a focusing lens. The same lens collects the photoluminescent photons, which are fo- cused on the input slit of a Czerny-Turner spectrograph with another lens, followed by a long-pass filter (we used 532, 600, or 1000 nm filters). The spectrograph is equipped with a single-channel PMT for visible and an InGaAs detector for NIR detection. The sample is in- side an electromagnet on a conductor-backed CPW, which acts as a microwave antenna.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that spin information in diamond NV centers can be read out via infrared singlet transitions near 1042 nm. By showing that this emission is sensitive to magnetic fields in the same way as visible fluorescence, the authors establish a direct, conversion-free interface for quantum communication using standard telecom wavelengths.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it proposes a hardware-agnostic method to integrate solid-state spin qubits with existing fiber-optic telecommunications infrastructure without active frequency conversion.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of interfacing diamond-based NV center spin-qubits with telecommunication infrastructure due to the need for lossy, complex frequency conversion from visible to infrared wavelengths.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrated that spin-state information is faithfully transcribed to the infrared singlet emission (near 1042 nm), providing a direct, conversion-free readout channel compatible with telecom bands.

**Method.** Wavelength-resolved optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) spectroscopy using a 532 nm laser, microwave irradiation, and a spectrometer capable of detecting NIR emission up to 2000 nm.

**Model / system.** Negatively charged nitrogen-vacancy (NV-) centers in diamond, described by a ground-state spin Hamiltonian including axial and transverse zero-field splitting.

**Key observables.** ODMR contrast in the 1042 nm emission, NIR photoluminescence intensity, and wavelength-resolved ODMR maps as a function of magnetic field.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Zero-field splitting D = 2872.3 MHz, transverse splitting E = 7.44 MHz, and the 1042 nm singlet-singlet transition wavelength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The identification of the NIR signal relies on the assumption that the spin-dependent intersystem crossing (ISC) mechanism is the primary driver of population redistribution.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the NV center structure, Jablonski diagram, and NIR emission spectra; Figure 2 displays wavelength-resolved ODMR spectra and the 1042 nm transition; Figure 3 presents ODMR intensity maps at 680 nm and 1310 nm compared with Hamiltonian eigenvalues.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the interface problem, describes the NV center electronic structure and the proposed NIR readout mechanism, details the experimental spectrometer setup, presents the wavelength-resolved ODMR results and spin-state transcription evidence, and concludes with the implications for quantum networking.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond are a leading platform for solid-state quantum sensing and quantum information processing. While most optical studies rely on the visible fluorescence associated with the triplet transitions, the infrared singlet transition near $1042$ nm, which is typically considered dark within the singlet manifold of the NV optical cycle, provides an alternative optical channel. Here, we report wavelength-resolved optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) measurements of this infrared emission. We directly observe ODMR contrast in the $1042$ nm emission and analyze its dependence on the magnetic field. The field-dependent spectral dispersion of the ODMR signal demonstrates that the spin-state information encoded in the NV center is transcribed to the infrared singlet emission through the spin-selective intersystem crossing, in close analogy to the visible fluorescence readout. These results establish infrared ODMR as a high-fidelity optical readout pathway. Crucially, by extending spin-state transcription directly into the $1300-1600$ nm range, this work demonstrates a direct, conversion-free interface between diamond spin-qubits and standard telecommunication infrastructure, bypassing the efficiency bottlenecks of active frequency conversion and benefiting from the already well-developed technologies in this range of the electromagnetic spectrum.

</details>

### [Excitation Flow, Positivity, and Fisher Information for Open Subsystems of an $N$-Qubit Network](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15036v1)

**Authors:** Tommy Chin, Sarah Shandera  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15036v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

<img src="2605.15036_figures/2605.15036_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">

(18), (27)] for ∆t = 0.05, K ∈{1, 2, 3, 4}, and N = 5, plotted against t in units of 2π/(NJ). Positive values indicate net dispersal away from the excited qubit (CP dynamics). Negative values indicate backflow (non-CP).</figcaption>
</figure>

Propagators Λi(t1, t2) contract toward the fixed point when φi τ &gt; 0 and expand away from it when φi τ &lt; 0.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: z-component of the domain of positivity (shaded region) of Λi(t, t + ∆t) with N = 5. Contracting propagators (moving toward the fixed point) preserve positivity across the full Bloch interval pi z ∈[−1, 1], whereas expanding propagators (moving away from the fixed point) preserve positivity only on a restricted subset. Overlapping shades appear brown.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper develops an analytical framework to study the dynamics of subsystems within a larger N-qubit network. It reveals that the direction of excitation flow uniquely determines the positivity and complete positivity of the dynamical maps and links these properties to the sensitivity of the system for parameter estimation via Quantum Fisher Information.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a unified framework for understanding how local observations of non-Markovian dynamics (including non-CP maps) can be used to infer global properties like coupling strength and network size.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating what an observer can infer about a closed N-qubit network by observing its correlated open subsystems, specifically addressing non-Markovian and non-completely positive dynamics.

**Main result.** The authors show that a single transition amplitude controls excitation flow, positivity, complete positivity, entanglement entropy, and quantum Fisher information; they also find that positivity and complete positivity coincide and are determined solely by the direction of excitation flow.

**Method.** Analytical derivation of closed-form propagators using an operator-sum representation and a decomposition of Quantum Fisher Information into state, process, and cross contributions.

**Model / system.** A closed N-qubit network with a single conserved excitation governed by a homogeneous all-to-all XXZ Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Excitation flow, positivity and complete positivity (CP), entanglement entropy, and Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for global parameters J and N.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Total number of qubits N, subsystem size K, coupling strength J, and the transition amplitude u_d(t).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is restricted to the single-excitation sector (charge q=1) and the global dynamics are unitary.

**Figures summary.** Figure 4 illustrates the action of dynamical maps on the Bloch ball and trajectories of physical states; Figure 5 shows the domain of positivity for different time intervals; Figure 9 demonstrates the decomposition of Fisher information into state, process, and cross contributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of subsystem observation, defines the N-qubit XXZ model, derives closed-form propagators, analyzes the conditions for positivity and CP, characterizes entanglement and excitation flow, and concludes with a detailed decomposition and analysis of Quantum Fisher Information.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We derive closed-form propagators for any $K$-qubit subsystem of a closed $N$-qubit network with a single conserved excitation. A single transition amplitude simultaneously controls excitation flow between subsystems, the positivity and complete positivity of every propagator, the entanglement entropy of every subsystem, and the quantum Fisher information for global parameters. Positivity and complete positivity coincide, determined solely by the direction of excitation flow, independently of subsystem size, coherence, or entanglement structure. A propagator is positive and completely positive if and only if it contracts the subsystem state toward its fixed point. The ensemble of propagators collectively constrains global properties inaccessible to any single subsystem. For single-qubit subsystems, we characterize the ensemble's fixed-point distribution and domain of positivity, finding a band of states that lies inside the positivity domain of every propagator yet is never visited by the physical dynamics. The quantum Fisher information decomposes into state and process contributions over any observation window $[t_1,t_2]$, with the state contribution bounded while the process contribution grows secularly. The total Fisher information is minimal when all future propagators are nonpositive and not completely positive, and near its maximum when they are positive and completely positive.

</details>

### [Superconducting single-photon detectors for integrated quantum photonics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14829v1)

**Authors:** Ilya A. Stepanov, Oksana I. Shmonina, Evgeniy V. Sergeev, Aleksandr S. Baburin, Danila Yu. Ulyanov, Kirill A. Buzaverov, Sergey S. Avdeev, Aleksey B. Kramarenko, Yuri V. Panfilov, Ilya A. Rodionov

**Summary.** This review examines the development of integrated superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (iSNSPDs) for use in photonic integrated circuits. It covers the transition from fundamental physical models to advanced material engineering and integration strategies. The paper highlights how these detectors are essential for the scalability of quantum communication and computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for quantum optics and quantum information researchers as it details the hardware advancements necessary for scalable photonic quantum networks and high-fidelity photon-number-resolving measurements.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of integrating high-performance single-photon detectors with photonic integrated circuits (PICs) to enable scalable quantum technologies like communication, computing, and sensing.

**Main result.** The review provides a comprehensive overview of the progress in integrated superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (iSNSPDs), highlighting the superiority of NbN and NbTiN for high-speed/high-efficiency applications and the potential of amorphous materials for mid-IR detection.

**Method.** A comprehensive review of literature covering fundamental physical models (hotspot, vortex, and VAP), material engineering strategies (ion irradiation, material selection), and integration architectures (waveguide-coupled, W-shaped nanowires).

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of integrated superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (iSNSPDs) embedded in photonic platforms such as silicon nitride or lithium niobate waveguides. Physical models include the hotspot model, the vortex model, and the vortex-antivortex pair (VAP) unbinding model.

**Key observables.** System detection efficiency (SDE), intrinsic detection efficiency (IDE), dark count rate (DCR), timing jitter, recovery time, and photon-number resolution (PNR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nanowire geometry (width, thickness, length), superconducting transition temperature (Tc), critical current (Ic), bias current (Ib), and photon wavelength (from visible to mid-IR).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The review notes that no single universal model currently explains all experimental observations across all device geometries and operating conditions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the SNSPD detection process, readout circuitry, and architecture; Figure 2 illustrates the breakdown of detection efficiency components and pulse dynamics; Figure 3 summarizes how performance metrics depend on device and environmental parameters; Table 1 compares material properties like Tc and Jc.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with fundamental detection principles and physical models, moves to the challenges and strategies of device integration and material engineering, discusses performance benchmarks and metrics, and concludes with future opportunities and challenges in the field.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Single-photon detection possibility is a fundamental requirement for quantum technologies, including communication, computing and sensing. To achieve scalability and practical deployment, increasing attention is being directed toward integration of detectors with photonic integrated circuits, which offer compactness and compatibility with mass production. Superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors have emerged as the leading solution, combining near-unity efficiency, high temporal performance and the ability to be embedded across a wide range of photonic material platforms. In this review we trace the development of integrated superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors from early demonstrations to recent advances, outlining the progress in device architectures, material engineering and integration strategies. We also discuss performance benchmarks, emerging alternative designs, the future opportunities and challenges for this rapidly evolving field.

</details>

**Authors:** Dianpeng Wang, You Xiao, Jiamin Xiong, Chenrui Wang, Zhen Wan, Hongxin Xu, Chaomeng Ding, Jia Huang, Lixing You, Hao Li

**Summary.** This paper identifies that stochastic baseline fluctuations in SNSPD readout electronics are a primary cause of timing jitter at high photon count rates. By modeling the readout as a finite-memory stochastic process, the authors provide a quantitative framework to predict and mitigate timing uncertainty. This work offers essential design principles for optimizing high-speed, high-precision single-photon detection systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonic quantum information, as it identifies a fundamental physical limit to the temporal precision of single-photon detection, which is critical for high-rate quantum communication and computing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the degradation of timing resolution (timing jitter) in Superconducting Nanowire Single-Photon Detectors (SNSPDs) during high-count-rate operation, which cannot be fully explained by deterministic waveform distortions.

**Main result.** The authors identify stochastic baseline fluctuations, caused by the accumulation of overlapping recovery responses in the readout chain, as a fundamental source of count-rate-dependent timing jitter and provide a predictive scaling framework.

**Method.** The researchers developed a stochastic-process framework using Campbell's theorem and compared theoretical predictions against experimental measurements of timing jitter and baseline variance across varying count rates, recovery time constants, and inductance values.

**Model / system.** The system is an SNSPD with an AC-coupled or high-pass-filtered readout chain characterized by finite-memory dynamics. The model treats photon arrivals as a stochastic process (Poisson or Bernoulli) driving a linear system with a single-event response kernel.

**Key observables.** Timing jitter (FWHM of the single-photon response), baseline standard deviation, pulse amplitude, rise time, and pulse area.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Count rate (lambda), readout recovery time constant (tau_e), kinetic inductance (Lk), and the perturbation parameter (Q).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes the readout architecture is effectively high-pass-filtered and initially assumes independent detection events, though it notes this assumption breaks down when inter-event intervals approach the recovery time constant.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows experimental evidence of baseline broadening; Figure 2 illustrates the physical mechanism of baseline accumulation and jitter conversion; Figure 3 shows scaling laws; Figure 5 shows the dependence of baseline standard deviation on the recovery time constant.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of jitter degradation, presents a theoretical stochastic framework for baseline fluctuations, validates this framework through systematic experimental variations of detector and readout parameters, and concludes with design principles for high-speed photon counting.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) have demonstrated timing jitter in the few-picosecond regime, yet their timing resolution deteriorates substantially under high-count-rate operation. Existing interpretations mainly attribute this degradation to deterministic waveform distortions, such as multiphoton responses and pulse pile-up, yet the experimentally observed jitter broadening at high count rates cannot be fully accounted for within this picture. Here, we show that stochastic baseline fluctuations arising from finite-memory readout dynamics constitute an intrinsic source of the count-rate-dependent timing jitter in SNSPD systems. For stochastically arriving photons, overlapping recovery responses accumulate in the readout chain and generate statistically fluctuating baselines, which are converted into timing uncertainty through threshold-based timing extraction. We develop a stochastic-process framework that quantitatively connects photon statistics, readout dynamics, and timing jitter. The framework predicts characteristic scaling behaviors, including a nonmonotonic dependence of baseline fluctuations under pulsed excitation with a maximum near half of the repetition frequency. These predictions are quantitatively verified through systematic variations of count rate, circuit time constant, and detector dynamical properties. Our results identify stochastic baseline dynamics as a fundamental mechanism limiting timing resolution in high-count-rate SNSPD operation and provide a general framework for optimizing finite-memory high-speed photon-counting systems.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides analytical solutions for the evolution of Gaussian states in a driven, dissipative quantum harmonic oscillator. It proves that the drift of the state is independent of bath temperature and fast-rotating modes, and characterizes the system's behavior at exceptional points and under various driving protocols.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides exact analytical solutions for driven-dissipative dynamics and clarifies the role of environmental noise parameters in the evolution of quantum trajectories.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the time evolution of Gaussian mixed states for a linearly driven quantum harmonic oscillator under a generic Markovian master equation, specifically focusing on how different Liouvillian components affect displacement dynamics.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that the displacement dynamics (first moments) are independent of bath temperature and fast-rotating modes, and that the exceptional point structure of the driven system remains identical to the non-driven system.

**Method.** The author uses an analytical approach involving superoperator algebra, displacement superoperators, and biorthogonal eigenbasis solutions to solve the master equation and analyze phase-space trajectories.

**Model / system.** A single-mode quantum harmonic oscillator subject to a Markovian master equation of the GKSL type, including unitary evolution, dissipation, and linear driving (constant, impulsive, or harmonic).

**Key observables.** First moments (average position and momentum) and second moments (variances and squeezing parameters).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Natural frequency (omega_0), decay rate (gamma), bath temperature, driving strength, and the presence of exceptional points (critical damping).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is assumed to be within the Markovian approximation and the states are restricted to the Gaussian regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1(a) shows phase space trajectories under constant driving across different damping regimes; Figure 1(b) illustrates the effect of impulsive driving (kicks) on the oscillator's position.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the master equation and the driven system, moves to deriving analytical solutions for Gaussian states, explores the relationship between driven and non-driven Liouvillians (including exceptional points), and concludes with the analysis of specific time-dependent driving cases (impulsive and harmonic).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study a generic quantum Markovian master equation for a linearly displaced or driven harmonic oscillator. It was known that the displacement dynamics of Gaussian mixed states depends on the unitary part of the Liouvillian, the decay rate of the system but not on the bath temperature. Here we further show that the fast-rotating modes do not affect the system's displacement dynamics under linear driving forces. Analytical solutions of the quantum master equation are obtained for displaced Gaussian mixed states. Because the non-driven and driven Liouvillians are related by a unitary displacement operator, they are expected to share the same exceptional points structure. At the exceptional points, the displacement of critically damped oscillator displays a characteristics polynomial-in-time prefactor multiplied by an exponential decay. We discuss how external time-dependent forces affect the displacement dynamics using impulsive force and harmonic force as examples. The results obtained for constant driving remain valid in the presence of time-dependent driving.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a systematic method to generalize continuum fractional quantum Hall wave functions to lattice systems by replacing contact clustering with discrete lattice-displaced zeros. The resulting model states for the Read-Rezayi series are exact ground states of specific density interactions and preserve essential topological features. This provides a blueprint for engineering topological phases in cold-atom and synthetic flat-band systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a constructive route for engineering topological orders in synthetic flat-band platforms like cold atoms, offering a way to realize complex non-Abelian states through controllable density interactions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of constructing exact lattice model wave functions for fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states (like Laughlin, Moore-Read, and Read-Rezayi) beyond simple bosonic systems, specifically overcoming the limitation that particles on a lattice cannot be infinitesimally close to satisfy continuum clustering conditions.

**Main result.** The authors systematically construct lattice model states for the entire Read-Rezayi series by using a delta-deformation that replaces infinitesimal contact zeros with discrete, distributed lattice zeros. These states are proven to be exact gapped ground states of specific density-density interactions and retain the idealized entanglement features of their continuum counterparts.

**Method.** The work combines analytical construction using elliptic functions (e.g., modified Weierstrass sigma functions) with numerical exact diagonalization on square and rectangular tori.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on lattice-based synthetic platforms, such as the Kapit-Mueller model (a Hofstadter model with flat bands) and density-density interaction Hamiltonians where the interaction pattern is matched to the zeros of the wave function.

**Key observables.** Many-body energy spectrum, pair and three-particle correlation functions (g2, g3), particle-cut entanglement spectrum (PES), projected structure factor, and spectral flow under twisted boundary conditions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Filling factor (nu = 1/3, 1/4, 1/2), interaction displacement (delta), magnetic flux density (phi), and interaction range.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The construction is specific to 'lattice ideal bands' like the Kapit-Mueller model; the broader implications for non-ideal bands are not fully addressed.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows diagnostics for the nu=1/3 Laughlin state including the many-body spectrum, correlation function zeros, and entanglement gap; Figure 2 demonstrates phase transitions induced by increasing interaction range; Figure 3 shows diagnostics for non-Abelian states; Supplemental figures show spectral flow, bosonic states, and the nu=1/4 Moore-Read state.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of lattice FQH construction, presents the delta-deformation theory for Laughlin states, extends the construction to Moore-Read and Read-Rezayi series, analyzes phase transitions via interaction range, and provides detailed numerical verification in the supplemental material.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Model wave functions are essential for studying fractional quantum Hall phases, yet lattice model states have so far been limited to bosonic systems with on-site interactions. In this work, by combining analytical and numerical methods, we systematically construct lattice model states for the Laughlin, Moore--Read, and general $\mathbb{Z}_k$ Read--Rezayi series. Our lattice-specific states are characterized by their idealized energy and entanglement features and are distinguished from their continuum counterparts by a modified clustering behavior. Our theory advances the understanding of the stability of topologically ordered phases and illustrates the organizing principles of the conformal Hilbert space on lattices. Practically, this work paves the way for further studying lattice-specific excitations and offers a constructive route for engineering topological orders within density interactions, with potential immediate implications for cold-atom and synthetic flat-band platforms.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops an open quantum theory to explain how dissipation suppresses shot noise in chiral transport by stacking electrons into lower energy states. It introduces a novel mathematical method to reconstruct the internal particle occupancy distribution of quantum channels using only measurable current noise cumulants.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful analytical tool (the inversion scheme) for extracting hidden many-body statistics from measurable noise, which is highly relevant for researchers studying non-equilibrium dynamics and open quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding the microscopic mechanism of shot-noise suppression in dissipative chiral transport and developing a method to reconstruct hidden internal occupancy distributions from measurable noise cumulants.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that current noise is governed by the competition between occupancy distribution and particle-number fluctuations, and they provide a successful inversion scheme to reconstruct the occupancy distribution from higher-order noise cumulants.

**Method.** The study uses an open quantum theory framework involving a discrete-time master equation, quantum trajectory simulations via Matrix Product States (MPS), and a mathematical inversion scheme based on filter cumulants and combinatorial expansions.

**Model / system.** An N-channel chiral transport system (such as quantum Hall edge states) modeled as a multi-layer fermionic quantum circuit in energy space, where electrons undergo elastic scattering via Haar-random unitary gates and energy exchange with a bath via dissipative jump operators.

**Key observables.** Average occupancy distribution, shot-noise spectral density (S11, S12), Fano factor, transmission coefficient, and higher-order noise cumulants.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of channels (N), number of energy levels (M), dissipation rate (gamma_0), source temperature (T_in), and bath temperature (T_bath).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes perfect intra-level channel mixing, inter-level statistical independence, and that dissipative jump operators act primarily on the first channel due to symmetric mixing.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 compares exact and effective CGFs and shows noise suppression; Figure 3 demonstrates the sign reversal of inter-channel noise (S12) in different heating regimes; Figure 4 shows the successful reconstruction of the occupancy distribution using the proposed inversion scheme.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of shot-noise in dissipative transport, presents a quantum circuit mapping and master equation model, validates an effective CGF framework, demonstrates a sign reversal in correlation noise, and concludes with a mathematical derivation of an inversion scheme for occupancy reconstruction.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop an open quantum theory for shot-noise dynamics in dissipative chiral transport. By mapping a system under consideration onto a quantum circuit, we show that current noise is governed by two competing factors: the average occupancy distribution and particle-number fluctuations. With energy fully relaxed, shot noise is strongly suppressed, reflecting the stacking of electrons into lower energy states due to dissipation. This process quenches the partition noise from partially occupied levels, and finally isolates the residual noise protected by strong $U(1)$ symmetry. Moreover, selectively heating the source against the bath uncovers the underlying competition between the noise contributions from the occupancy distribution and those from the particle-number fluctuations. It triggers a sign reversal in inter-channel correlation noise, a signature masked by seemingly identical single-channel thermal noises. We propose an inversion scheme to experimentally reconstruct the hidden occupancy distribution directly from measurable noise cumulants.

</details>

### [Revealing Hidden Correlations in a Fermi-Hubbard system via Interaction Ramps](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14909v1)

**Authors:** Botond Oreg, Carter Turnbaugh, Jens Hertkorn, Ningyuan Jia, Martin Zwierlein  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14909v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `analog quantum simulation` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

**Summary.** The researchers developed a technique using a rapid interaction ramp to make nonlocal pairs visible as local doublons in a 2D Fermi-Hubbard system. This method successfully reveals hidden charge-density-wave correlations and distinguishes between the Fermi liquid and pseudogap regimes. It offers a new way to study complex phases like stripe order or FFLO states in ultracold atoms.

**Why it may be interesting.** This provides a powerful new experimental tool for observing exotic pair orders and phase transitions in many-body systems by using non-equilibrium dynamics to reveal static correlations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Detecting hidden spatial correlations, specifically charge-density-wave order, in the strongly interacting regime of the attractive Hubbard model where nonlocal pairs are difficult to image.

**Main result.** An interaction ramp technique significantly enhances the visibility of charge-density-wave correlations by projecting nonlocal pairs into detectable local doublons. This allows for the clear distinction between the Fermi liquid and the pseudogap phase.

**Method.** A rapid linear magnetic field ramp (1 ms) is used to increase the interaction strength, contracting extended pairs into local doublons before single-site, spin-resolved fluorescence imaging.

**Model / system.** A 2D spin-balanced Fermi gas of 40K atoms in a square optical lattice, realizing the attractive Hubbard model with controlled tunneling (t) and interaction (U) via Feshbach resonance.

**Key observables.** Doublon density, density-density correlators, density structure factor, charge-density-wave strength, singlon fraction, and spin/magnetization correlations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strength U/t (ranging from weak/Fermi liquid to strong/local pairs), tunneling t = 340 Hz, temperature T/t ~ 0.5, and lattice depth.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The ramp is fast enough to prevent many-body evolution but slow enough to allow pair contraction; the density distribution remains frozen during the ramp.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the ramp schematic and enhanced CDW patterns; Fig 2 displays density-density correlation maps; Fig 4 compares magnetization fluctuations and singlon density across different U/t regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of hidden correlations, describes the experimental setup and the interaction ramp technique, presents results showing enhanced CDW visibility and the identification of the pseudogap phase, and concludes with potential applications to spin-imbalanced and repulsive Hubbard models.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We observe an enhanced visibility of charge-density-wave correlations in a cold-atom realization of the attractive Hubbard model following a rapid boost of the interaction strength. The interaction boost associates nonlocal pairs into doublons which mark the center of mass of the original pairs. The enhancement is largest in the strongly correlated regime where pairing is nonlocal. We distinguish the unpaired Fermi liquid from the pseudogap phase of preformed pairs by analyzing atom-resolved spin-charge correlations after the ramp. The technique we establish here may facilitate the observation of exotic forms of pair order in spin-imbalanced systems, and of stripe order in the dual case of the doped repulsive Hubbard model.

</details>

### [Toward Covert Quantum Computing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14325v1)

**Authors:** Evan J. D. Anderson, Kaushik Datta, Boulat A. Bash  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14325v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

Bottom row: IBM ibm_fez (heavy-hex lattice, n = 2, 12, 54, 108). </figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces 'covert quantum computing,' a method to hide computations from adversaries on shared quantum hardware. While theory predicts that a small buffer of qubits can hide activity, experimental results on IBM and IQM processors show that long-range crosstalk can expose the computation, necessitating better spatial isolation.

**Why it may be interesting.** It addresses the physical-layer security of quantum hardware, specifically how many-body interactions (crosstalk) can be used as a side channel for information leakage, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and noise in large-scale quantum architectures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** How to perform quantum computations on a multi-tenant cloud QPU such that an adversary cannot detect the ongoing computation via crosstalk-induced noise in spectator qubits.

**Main result.** The authors derive a 'Square-Root Law' stating that a computation can be made covert using only O(sqrt(n)) additional idling qubits, but experimental tests on IBM and IQM hardware reveal long-range crosstalk that creates a side channel for detection.

**Method.** The paper uses the quantum-strategy framework (quantum combs) and discrete isoperimetric inequalities for theoretical bounds, alongside Ramsey interferometry to experimentally measure frequency shifts in spectator qubits.

**Model / system.** Superconducting quantum processors with planar topologies, specifically IQM's 54-qubit Emerald (square lattice) and IBM's 156-qubit ibm_fez (heavy-hex lattice), utilizing tunable-coupler architectures.

**Key observables.** Frequency shifts (delta f) in spectator qubits, ZZ-crosstalk, and the probability of detection (false alarm and missed detection rates).

**Important parameters / regimes.** n-qubit circuit size, number of shots (k), diamond-norm distance, and the scaling of the boundary size relative to the computational area.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The initial theoretical model assumes nearest-neighbor crosstalk; the adversary has access to quantum memory and can perform adaptive operations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows optimal geometric layouts (diamonds/disks) for minimizing boundaries; Figure 2 shows the Ramsey circuit protocol; Figure 3 shows experimental Ramsey oscillation data; Figure 4 shows the physical qubit layouts on Emerald and ibm_fez; Figure 5 presents bar charts of detected frequency shifts.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the concept of covert quantum computing, develops a theoretical framework using information theory and graph theory to derive scaling laws, presents experimental validation using Ramsey interferometry on two different hardware platforms, and discusses the implications of discovered long-range crosstalk.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

As quantum computers become available through multi-tenant cloud platforms, ensuring privacy against adversaries sharing the same quantum processing unit becomes critical. We introduce and explore \emph{covert quantum computing}, a new concept that ensures an adversary with access to all other quantum computational units (QCUs) of a quantum computer cannot detect computation on the subset that they cannot access. Analogous to covert communication, we employ information theory. However, since here the adversary controls the systems used for detection, we require a richer framework for covertness analysis that accounts for the use of quantum memories and adaptive operations. Thus, we adopt the \emph{quantum-strategy} framework used in quantum game theory and memory channel discrimination. Current quantum computers use planar graph circuit layouts and typically assume nearest-neighbor crosstalk. We derive discrete isoperimetric inequalities to show that, for an $n$-qubit circuit under this model, only $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n})$ border qubits provide detection information to the adversary. We then explore this scaling law on IQM's 54-qubit \emph{Emerald} processor and IBM's 156-qubit \emph{ibm\_fez} machine employing the Heron 2 architecture. We implement Ramsey experiments on qubits not used in computation, and detect nearest-neighbor crosstalk, as expected. However, we also observe long-range coupling effects beyond the border qubits, revealing a side channel that the adversary can exploit. We hypothesize that this long-range crosstalk is induced by leakage from the drive and control lines. Beyond weakening covertness, it exposes co-tenants to both adversarial and unintended crosstalk and degrades circuits that span spatially distributed qubits, motivating further work on spatial isolation and crosstalk characterization.

</details>

### [Translation symmetry-enforced long-range entanglement in mixed states](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15200v1)

**Authors:** Ryan Thorngren, Lei Gioia, Carolyn Zhang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15200v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15200_figures/2605.15200_page5.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 5</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15200_figures/2605.15200_page6.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 6</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper demonstrates that translation symmetry can force a mixed state to possess long-range entanglement that cannot be decomposed into short-range entangled components. This effect is particularly interesting because it remains hidden from standard local correlation function measurements. The study provides a rigorous lower bound on the circuit depth required to simulate such states.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it establishes a fundamental link between symmetry, entanglement structure, and the complexity (circuit depth) required for thermalization and state preparation.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether translation symmetry can enforce long-range entanglement (LRE) in mixed states, specifically determining if the maximally mixed state of translation-invariant states is LRE.

**Main result.** The authors prove that the maximally mixed state of translation-invariant states is LRE because the dimension of the zero momentum sector exceeds the dimension of the space spanned by short-range entangled (SRE) states. This LRE is subtle because it cannot be detected by long-range connected correlation functions.

**Method.** A dimension-based counting argument is used to compare the size of the zero momentum sector to the space of SRE states, supported by a 'Tails Lemma' and circuit decomposition into Matrix Product Operators.

**Model / system.** 1D quantum chains of length n with periodic boundary conditions and local Hilbert space dimension q, focusing on the zero momentum sector of translation-invariant states.

**Key observables.** Trace distance (to measure approximation fidelity), connected correlation functions, and circuit depth/evolution time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size (n), local dimension (q), circuit depth (d), and evolution time (tau).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary results are derived for 1D systems; the proof assumes translation symmetry of the resulting state but not necessarily the generating circuit.

**Figures summary.** A circuit diagram illustrating a depth-4 circuit with a highlighted light-cone (sub-circuit C0) and internal bonds representing Schmidt decompositions.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of LRE in mixed states, introduces a counting argument and dimension bounds, develops a mathematical framework using circuit decomposition and MPS counting, provides the main theorem on LRE, and discusses the implications for correlation functions and higher dimensions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show by a counting argument that even though translation symmetry admits symmetric short-range entangled (SRE) eigenstates, there are not enough such SRE eigenstates to span the zero momentum sector. This means that the fixed point strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry breaking state of translation symmetry is long-range entangled: it cannot be written as a mixture of SRE states. This is a subtle form of long-range entanglement in mixed states that cannot be detected by long-range connected correlation functions.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.15008"></a>
### [Majorana Constellations: A Geometric Lens on Multipartite Entanglement and Geometric Phases](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15008v1)

**Authors:** Chon-Fai Kam  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15008v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `Dicke superradiance` **2/5** · `Tavis-Cummings & cavity-many-emitter` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15008_figures/2605.15008_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1: Overall Section Roadmap outlining the logical architecture of this review article. The flow follows an S-shaped trajectory from mathematical Foundations (Chaps. III–IV) through Core Applications (Chaps. V–VII), transitioning to Experimental Implementations, and culminating in Future Horizons (Chap. VIII).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This review synthesizes the Majorana stellar representation as a geometric framework for studying multipartite entanglement and geometric phases. It demonstrates how mapping quantum states to constellations of points on a sphere simplifies the classification of entanglement and makes the evaluation of many-body invariants computationally tractable. The work also establishes a profound mathematical link between quantum entanglement patterns and the classification of gravitational fields in general relativity.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful geometric tool for analyzing many-body entanglement and phase-space properties, offering a way to bypass computational complexity in symmetric quantum systems and linking quantum correlations to spacetime geometry.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of characterizing multipartite entanglement and geometric phases in large-scale quantum systems using traditional algebraic methods, which often face #P-hard computational bottlenecks.

**Main result.** The paper establishes the Majorana stellar representation as a unified geometric language that simplifies entanglement classification (SLOCC) and reduces the complexity of evaluating many-body invariants from #P-hard to polynomial time.

**Method.** The authors use a mapping of spin-S states to a constellation of points (stars) on a Bloch sphere via the roots of a characteristic polynomial, leveraging stereographic projection and spinor calculus.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on symmetric multi-qubit systems and higher-spin (S) systems, including bosonic ensembles, Dicke states, and continuous-variable (CV) systems in the Bargmann-Fock space.

**Key observables.** Concurrence, three-tangle, genuine multipartite entanglement (GME), Berry and Hannay phases, and Husimi Q-function zeros.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The spin magnitude (S), the number of qubits (N=2S), and the thermodynamic limit (j approaching infinity).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary analytical framework assumes permutation symmetry (the symmetric subspace) and focuses on pure quantum states.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a roadmap of the article, outlining the progression from mathematical foundations to applications in entanglement, dynamics, and experimental implementations.

**Paper structure.** The paper moves from mathematical foundations (polynomials and isomorphisms) to geometric quantities and entanglement metrics, then explores unitary dynamics (geometric phases), and concludes with applications in metrology, error correction, and condensed matter.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Majorana stellar representation translates abstract quantum spin states into intuitive geometric constellations on the Bloch sphere, revealing symmetries, degeneracies, and correlations that traditional algebraic methods often obscure. Within quantum information science, this framework provides a powerful lens for characterizing symmetric multi-qubit and higher-spin systems. By encoding entanglement directly into spatial coordinates, the constellation geometry yields exact measures of concurrence, three-tangle, and genuine multipartite entanglement, while its dynamical evolution uncovers internal anomalous contributions to geometric phases. While interest in stellar representations has resurged, existing literature remains fragmented, lacking a unified treatment of these entanglement-specific metrics and their higher-dimensional dynamics. This review synthesizes the entanglement-centric perspective on Majorana representations, bridging discrete algebraic classifications (e.g., SLOCC orbits) with continuous geometric interpretations. Crucially, we highlight how this framework circumvents \#P-hard computational bottlenecks, leveraging polynomial-time tractability to evaluate multipartite invariants. We detail the interplay between constellation topology and higher-spin Berry/Hannay phases, explore extensions beyond pure symmetric states, and review applications in quantum metrology, state engineering, and condensed-matter physics. By foregrounding entanglement as the unifying theme, this comprehensive examination establishes Majorana stars as a fundamental geometric language, uniquely positioned to inspire new theoretical and experimental directions in quantum technologies.

</details>

**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15087v1>

(c) The frequency-domain amplitude of the single electron motion along x-direction for various ϕx. All top panels are with the relative driving strength ε applied instantly at t = 0 µs, whereas all bottom panels with the relative driving strength ε ramping up from 0 to 0.1...</figcaption>

Specif- ically, the system is initialized at temperature T with ri = p</figcaption>

Inset: power spetrum statistics at 200 MHz, displaying the density distri- bution (shaded blue area), the mean and standard deviation (center dot with error bars), and the raw power spectrum values of individual trajectories (small dots). The dotted red line in the inset is the thermal noise floor. (b) SNRs of the voltage power signal of the image current with different detection times.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new method for detecting single electrons in Paul traps by using a ramped parametric drive to lock the electron's frequency during the transient regime. This approach overcomes the limitations of traditional steady-state detection, such as frequency fluctuations and signal broadening. The method enables faster and more robust nondestructive readout, essential for electron-based quantum information processing.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a novel way to utilize transient dynamics rather than steady-state assumptions to achieve robust frequency-locking, which is highly relevant for high-fidelity readout in trapped-particle quantum technologies.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The nondestructive detection of single-electron motion in Paul traps is hindered by motional frequency fluctuations caused by trap anharmonicities and RF field instabilities.

**Main result.** A controlled ramp of the parametric drive strength effectively locks the electron's motional frequency in the transient regime, providing a robust and fast readout method resilient to noise and micromotion.

**Method.** The study uses numerical simulations of 3D electron dynamics, comparing instantaneous versus ramped parametric driving protocols using high-order stochastic differential equation solvers.

**Model / system.** A single electron trapped in a three-layer PCB Paul trap, coupled to an RLC resonator for image current detection, modeled with nonlinear oscillator dynamics including 6th-order anharmonic terms.

**Key observables.** Image current (induced voltage), electron motion amplitude and phase, power spectrum of the resonator voltage, and Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Secular frequency of 2pi x 200 MHz, driving strength epsilon = 0.1, temperature T = 4 K, and a parametric drive frequency of approximately twice the secular frequency.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model uses a pseudopotential approximation and neglects motional cross-coupling between different axes.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the trap geometry and phase-space trajectories of the separatrix; Fig 2 compares instantaneous vs. ramped driving in time and frequency domains; Fig 3 demonstrates robustness to frequency detuning; Fig 5 shows the probability density distribution of voltage power density.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the detection problem, describes the PCB trap platform and RLC circuit, presents the nonlinear oscillator model, compares driving protocols (instantaneous vs. ramped), analyzes noise impact and statistical distributions, and concludes with implications for experimental realization.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nondestructive detection of single-electron motion is crucial for quantum information processing with electrons trapped in Paul traps. The standard approach in Penning traps is to detect the image current induced on the trap electrodes by the electron's oscillatory motion. However, applying this approach in Paul traps for single electrons is currently hindered by motional frequency fluctuations arising from trap anharmonicities and instabilities in the rf trapping field. In this work, we propose a robust detection scheme exploiting the transient dynamics of parametric driving to overcome these limitations. Distinct from traditional steady-state approaches, our method focuses on the transient regime to break the temporal constraints imposed by steady-state assumptions, thereby enabling fast readout. We show that a controlled ramp of the parametric drive effectively locks the frequency of the electron motion in the transient regime, rendering the signal highly resilient to realistic experimental noise and inherent micromotion. This work paves the way for the experimental realization of nondestructive detection of single-electron motion in Paul traps.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.14529"></a>
### [Quantum-enabled complete RF-polarimetry with an optically-wired atomic sensor](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14529v1)

**Authors:** Matthew Chilcott, Laurits N. Stokholm, Matthew Cloutman, J. Susanne Otto, Amita B. Deb, Niels Kjærgaard

**Summary.** This paper presents a method for complete RF-polarimetry using Rydberg atoms as an optically-wired sensor. By analyzing the spectroscopic fingerprints of RF-dressed atoms, the researchers can determine the full polarization state of an RF field without needing calibration or metallic antennas. This approach leverages the fundamental quantization of angular momentum to create a universal sensing framework.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and AMO physics as it demonstrates a novel way to use the internal quantum structure (angular momentum quantization) of atoms to perform high-precision, non-invasive sensing of external classical fields.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a complete, calibration-free RF-polarimetry method to determine the full Stokes vector of an RF field using Rydberg atoms without the field distortions caused by metallic antennas.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrated that the state of polarization (SOP) of an RF field is encoded in the spectroscopic fingerprints of Rydberg atoms, allowing for universal, calibration-free measurement of the full Stokes vector.

**Method.** The method uses Electromagnetically Induced Transparency (EIT) in a Rubidium vapor cell to probe RF-dressed Rydberg states, employing an analytical inversion of the EIT spectra based on eigenvalue envelope ratios.

**Model / system.** An optically-wired atomic sensor using 87Rb atoms in a vapor cell, specifically utilizing Rydberg states (n approx 50) and a ladder-type EIT scheme with 780 nm and 480 nm lasers.

**Key observables.** Atomic eigenenergy spectrum, EIT peak positions, and the eigenvalue envelope ratio R.

**Important parameters / regimes.** RF phase angle (phi), RF polarization (linear, circular, elliptical), and principal quantum number (n).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the dipole approximation and the rotating wave approximation (RWA).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the mapping of phase angles to EIT spectra, the identification of mathematical ambiguities in the phase angle extraction, and the use of peak intensity/symmetry breaking to resolve those ambiguities.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of RF-polarimetry, describes the Rydberg-based experimental platform, presents the analytical framework for mapping SOP to the Poincaré sphere, details the inversion method and ambiguity resolution, and concludes with experimental demonstrations in Rubidium.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Rydberg atomic electrometry leverages the extreme sensitivity of highly excited atoms for calibration-free electric field measurements. The technique uses a non-metallic vapor cell to link properties of an RF field to a spectroscopic readout in the optical domain. Most demonstrations have so far focused on detecting linearly-polarized fields, for which the induced splitting of dressed atomic levels is rotationally invariant. Here we report on Rydberg atomic measurements of RF fields in a general state of polarization (SOP) which we map onto the Poincaré sphere through spectroscopic fingerprints. For a Stokes vector circumnavigating a Poincaré sphere meridian, we witness a continuous transformation of the atomic eigenenergy spectrum. Because the relative positions of eigenenergies are locked in place by quantization of angular momentum, the framework is universal and calibration free. We provide a specific demonstration in rubidium, which generalizes to all systems with a single valence electron.

</details>

### [Blind Quantum Computation on a Modular Superconducting Processor](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14656v1)

**Authors:** Yongxin Song, Johannes Knörzer, Kieran Dalton, Andreas Wallraff, Jean-Claude Besse  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14656v1>

The server generates the resource cluster state and transfers it to the client. (a) Measurement pattern used to realize arbitrary single- qubit unitaries. The cluster state nodes (gray disks) are measured in the sequence indicated by the arrow, resulting in the output state (black). (b) Experimental process matrix |εin,out| of a deterministic measurement-based T gate (filled bars), realized by setting α1 = −π/4, α2 = α3 = −π/2 in (a). The ideal and simulated processes are shown with solid and dashed wire frames. (c) Measurement-based generation of the output state |ψ⟩= (|00⟩−i|11⟩)/ √</figcaption>

Mea- sured Pauli operator expectations values of (a) the server and (c) the client qubit at different stages of a measurement-based T-gate execution, gate sequence in (b). Solid wire frames show the ideal expectation values. The purity γ of each state is quoted below each tomogram. Each of the first three blocks represents generating and measuring one node along the 1D cluster state in the basis B(αi), i = 1, 2, 3. The last block rep- resents the SWAP circuit between the server and the client module.</figcaption>

(a) An elementary circuit that realizes a measurement-based single-qubit rotation parameterized by the projection angle θ. Passing an input qubit in state |ψin⟩ and an auxiliary qubit in state |0⟩through the sequence gener- ates the output state |ψout⟩accompanied by the binary mea- surement result s. (b) Three measurement-based single-qubit rotation cells are concatenated to realize arbitrary single- qubit unitary parameterized by the Euler angles (α1, α2, α3).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates the first implementation of a blind quantum computation protocol on a modular superconducting processor. By using a server-client architecture, the researchers showed that a client can run algorithms like Deutsch-Jozsa without revealing the task or results to the server. The study quantifies extremely low information leakage, proving the feasibility of secure, privacy-preserving cloud quantum computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates a practical implementation of a quantum communication/computing protocol (BQC) using a modular hardware architecture, which is highly relevant to the development of quantum networks and distributed quantum computing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of privacy for algorithms and data in cloud-based quantum computing. The paper aims to demonstrate a proof-of-principle for Blind Quantum Computation (BQC) on a modular superconducting processor.

**Main result.** Successful execution of a measurement-based BQC protocol and the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm on a modular architecture, with verified negligible information leakage (Holevo information of 2.3e-4 bits) to the server.

**Method.** A measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) protocol where a server prepares a cluster state and a client performs adaptive single-qubit rotations and measurements, using iterative state transfer between modules.

**Model / system.** A modular superconducting processor consisting of two flip-chip-bonded modules (a server and a client), each hosting three flux-tunable transmon qubits connected via static qubit-qubit couplers.

**Key observables.** Cluster state fidelity, process fidelity of T-gates and two-qubit gates, Holevo information for leakage analysis, and Pauli expectation values.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Median T1 of 43 us, median T2* of 39 us, single-qubit gate error of 0.09%, and two-qubit gate error of 0.8%.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes no information leakage via unintended hardware side channels like crosstalk and assumes the absence of unintended pathways between modules.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the experimental principle and circuit diagrams; Figure 2 shows cluster state fidelity estimation; Figure 3 details measurement-based gate implementation; Figure 4 shows the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm execution; Figure 5 verifies blindness via Pauli operators; Figure 6 illustrates single-qubit rotation patterns; Figure 7 provides qubit coherence and error rate distributions; Figure 8 shows the experimental setup schematic.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the privacy problem in cloud computing, describes the modular hardware architecture, details the MBQC/BQC protocol implementation, presents results for cluster state generation and gate fidelities, demonstrates the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm, quantifies information leakage, and concludes with an error decomposition and scalability analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Current cloud-based quantum processors offer access to advanced hardware hosted on a remote server, but do not guarantee data or algorithm privacy. Blind quantum computation provides information-theoretic privacy by enabling a client to execute an algorithm without disclosing information about either the task or the final result. Here, we execute a measurement-based blind quantum computation protocol on a superconducting processor comprising two flip-chip-bonded modules, one acting as a server and the other as a client. The server generates a two-dimensional cluster state and forwards it to the client. Using this resource, the client implements a universal gate set with only adaptive single-qubit rotations and measurements. To illustrate this approach, we execute a three-qubit instance of the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm. We analyze the server's quantum state after each rotation of a measurement-based single-qubit gate to verify that negligible information about the computation is revealed to the server, consistent with the one-way flow of information that guarantees blindness. This proof-of-principle demonstration establishes key elements of blind quantum computation in superconducting-circuit architectures, indicating that intermediate-scale implementations of blind protocols may become feasible with realistic near-term improvements in gate fidelities.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.15114"></a>
### [New approaches to almost i.i.d. information theory](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15114v1)

**Authors:** Filippo Girardi, Giacomo De Palma, Ludovico Lami  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15114v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15114_figures/2605.15114_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: A pictorial representation of the hierarchical relation between the various notions of almost i.i.d.-ness discussed in this paper: the Mazzola–Sutter–Renner notion is the strictest, the one derived from Wasserstein distance is the intermediate one, and, finally, weak almost i.i.d.-ness is the broadest. All inclusions are strict.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper introduces new mathematical frameworks for 'almost i.i.d.' quantum sources to better model real-world systems with local correlations. It proves that these new definitions form a strict hierarchy, ranging from the highly constrained MSR model to a looser definition based on average k-body marginals.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as it provides a more realistic mathematical framework for studying quantum processes where the assumption of independent, memoryless noise or sources is physically unrealistic.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The standard i.i.d. assumption in quantum information theory is often too restrictive for practical, physical settings. The paper seeks to formalize and compare alternative mathematical definitions of 'almost i.i.d.' sources that better represent real-world devices with memory or local perturbations.

**Main result.** The authors establish a strict hierarchical relation between three notions of almost i.i.d. states: MSR almost i.i.d. is the strictest, followed by Wasserstein almost i.i.d., with weakly almost i.i.d. being the loosest. They provide explicit counter-examples to prove that these inclusions are strict.

**Method.** The study utilizes quantum Wasserstein distance (order 1), trace distance, and analysis of k-body marginals. Mathematical tools include the symmetric group, type theory, and multilinear polynomials to establish bounds and separations.

**Model / system.** The paper considers n-partite quantum states (and classical probability distributions) acting on a Hilbert space H^n, specifically investigating sequences of states that deviate from the ideal i.i.d. structure.

**Key observables.** von Neumann entropy, conditional entropy, squashed entanglement, k-body marginals, and quantum Wasserstein distance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** n (number of subsystems), r (number of defects), k (size of local marginals), and the scaling regime where r = o(n).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The initial definitions and notation assume a finite-dimensional Hilbert space.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by introducing two new definitions of almost i.i.d. states (Wasserstein and weakly almost i.i.d.) relative to the MSR definition. It then explores their properties, proves the hierarchical inclusion of the three classes, and concludes with explicit constructions of counter-examples to demonstrate the strictness of the hierarchy.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) states are ubiquitous in quantum information theory. However, in a practical setting, the i.i.d. assumption is too stringent, and possibly not realistic. A physically more compelling class of 'almost i.i.d.' sources was recently proposed by [Mazzola/Sutter/Renner, arXiv:2603.15792]. In this paper, we introduce two alternative definitions of almost i.i.d. states, based on the normalised quantum Wasserstein distance and on the idea of looking at the average $k$-body marginal. We explore some basic properties of these notions and prove a strict hierarchical relation among them, with Mazzola et al.'s notion being the strictest, the one based on $k$-body marginals the loosest, and the one based on the quantum Wasserstein distance in between. Strict separation is established by means of explicit examples.

</details>

### [Power sensitivity of broadband radiofrequency detectors based on quantum diamond spins](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14244v1)

**Authors:** Nicholas Gillespie, Christopher T. -K. Lew, Ryan Kinsella, Andy Sayers, Brant Gibson, David A. Broadway, Jean-Philippe Tetienne
