**Summary.** This paper provides a theoretical framework for optimizing the power sensitivity of NV-based RF detectors. It reveals that miniaturizing the RF-spin interface can improve power sensitivity, offering a new design paradigm for broadband quantum RF sensing.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and sensing, as it provides a fundamental framework for optimizing the interface between classical electromagnetic signals and quantum spin ensembles, which is critical for developing scalable quantum-enhanced RF sensors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Optimizing the power sensitivity of broadband radiofrequency (RF) detectors based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, specifically shifting focus from magnetic field sensitivity to input RF power sensitivity.

**Main result.** The study finds that power sensitivity scales inversely with the physical dimensions of the RF-spin interface, meaning smaller structures and smaller probed NV volumes can actually improve power sensitivity, contrary to traditional magnetic field sensing optimization.

**Method.** The authors use an analytical framework to derive scaling laws for power sensitivity across different noise and detection regimes, supplemented by COMSOL and CST Studio Suite numerical simulations to validate field distributions and optimize geometry.

**Model / system.** The system consists of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center ensembles in diamond coupled to RF concentrators, such as coplanar waveguides (CPW) or loop antennas, interrogated via laser-induced photoluminescence.

**Key observables.** Power sensitivity (expressed in W/Hz for slope-detection and W/sqrt(Hz) for variance-detection), RF magnetic field distribution, and PL contrast.

**Important parameters / regimes.** CPW track width (w), loop antenna radius (R), NV density, photon shot noise limit, and the distinction between linear and saturated photoluminescence regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes uniform NV density and laser intensity over the probe volume, and uses simplified models for the magnetic field enclosure and optimization constants.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the generalized detector structure; Figure 2 illustrates parallel vs. perpendicular laser geometries and sensitivity vs. width; Figure 4 presents COMSOL simulations of magnetic field distribution and optimization of probe volume parameters; Figure 5 shows CST simulations of loop antenna performance.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of power sensitivity, moves to an analytical derivation of scaling laws for different geometries and protocols, presents numerical validation via electromagnetic simulations, and concludes with parameter estimates for achievable sensitivities.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centres in diamond can be used to detect radiofrequency (RF) signals through coupling of the RF magnetic field with the NV spins, combined with optical readout of the spin state. The sensitivity of such RF detectors has so far been mainly studied in terms of magnetic field sensitivity, which is relevant when the RF signal is generated by a near-field source. However, for applications where the RF input is delivered externally, a more relevant quantity is the sensitivity in terms of the input RF power. Here we theoretically analyse the power sensitivity of NV-based RF detectors as a function of the RF-spin interface geometry. We derive scaling laws of the power sensitivity for both slope-detection and variance-detection RF sensing protocols, and for various noise regimes. We find that, in most scenarios, the power sensitivity scales inversely with the characteristic physical dimension of the RF-spin interface, for instance the width of a coplanar waveguide or the diameter of a loop antenna. In other words, the smaller the structure and the probed NV volume, the better the power sensitivity, which is contrary to the case of magnetic field sensitivity. Lastly, we numerically estimate that photon shot noise limited sensitivities of 10^{-20} W Hz^{-1} (slope) and 10^{-12} W Hz^{-1/2} (variance) are achievable. This work lays the groundwork for further optimisation of NV-based RF detectors.

</details>

Polarizations of pump (Epu) and probe (Epr) pulses are parallel to the a-axis (6 K, Iex= 0.1 mJ/cm²). (c)-(f) ΔR/R spectra at td=-10, 0 fs (c), 10 fs (d), 90 fs (e), 200 fs(f) are shown. (a) Two- dimensional plot of the time evolution of ΔR/R spectrum, measured using a 100 fs pulse (10 K, pump energy of 0.89 eV, Iex= 0.04 mJ/cm²). Polarizations of pump (Epu) and probe (Epr) pulses are parallel to the a-axis. (b) Steady-state R spectrum and (c)(d) ΔR/R spectra measured at td=0 ps (c), 0.2 ps (d). The spectral range below 1 eV is shown multiplied by 3. (e) Time evolutions of ΔR/R measured at (I) 0.62 eV (mid-IR band), (II) 1.17 eV (peak of ΔR/R), (III) 1.90 eV (ZRS band), and (IV) 2.92 eV (CT band).</figcaption>
</figure>

(a)(b) Results of the DLM analysis for the ΔR/R spectra at td = 0 ps (a) and 0.2 ps (b) are presented as the solid lines. (c)-(i) Time evolutions of γMIR and fMIR [(c) and (f)], γ1.4 eV and f1.4 eV [(d) and (g)], and γZRS and fZRS [(e) and (i)]. The size of the circles indicate the hole densities of 3d and 2p orbitals. (c) Relation between the CT energy[𝑝𝑑= 𝑝−𝑑 with the on-site energy of the O 2p (Cu 3d) orbital 𝑝 (𝑑) in the hole picture] and fZRS calculated based on the exact diagonalization [25]. The parameters and boundary conditions used here are the same as those in the 1-hole case of Fig. 12(b) in Reference 25 except for the CT energy.</figcaption>

</div>

**Summary.** This paper uses ultrafast spectroscopy to resolve the initial stages of quasiparticle generation in YBCO. It reveals that electron-electron Umklapp scattering occurs almost instantaneously, followed by a 90 fs decoupling of quasiparticles from spin fluctuations. These findings offer new clues regarding the pairing mechanism in high-temperature superconductors.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper provides high-resolution insights into many-body dynamics, specifically the non-equilibrium decoupling of particles from a bosonic (spin-fluctuation) background, which is relevant to the study of driven many-body systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding the ultrafast dynamics of quasiparticle generation and the decoupling of quasiparticles from spin fluctuations following a rapid quench of superconductivity in high-Tc cuprates.

**Main result.** The study identifies an initial enhancement of electron-electron Umklapp scattering within a few femtoseconds, followed by an ultrafast decoupling of quasiparticles from the spin-fluctuation background on a 90 fs timescale.

**Method.** Ultrafast broadband (0.16-4.1 eV) and near single-cycle (6 fs) transient reflectivity spectroscopy using pump-probe techniques.

**Model / system.** Optimally doped YBCO (YBa2Cu3Oy, y=6-6.94) single crystals, analyzed using a Drude-Lorentz model and the three-band Hubbard (Emery) model context.

**Key observables.** Transient reflectivity (delta R/R), oscillator strengths (f), scattering rates (gamma), and central frequencies (omega) of various electronic bands.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temporal scales of <10 fs, 90 fs, and 2 ps; energy scales including the mid-IR band (0.1-1 eV), 1.4 eV band, and ZRS band (~1.9 eV).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The low-frequency response in the superconducting state is approximated with a Drude term, and the Drude-Lorentz model parameters are assumed to be uniform within the layers of a multilayer film model.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the steady-state permittivity spectrum; Fig 2 shows the time evolution of delta R/R at 10 K; Fig 3 presents high-resolution temporal dynamics using 6 fs pulses; Fig 4/S-series show Drude-Lorentz parameter evolution and spectral analysis.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to the problem of quasiparticle generation, experimental setup description, steady-state spectral identification, results of broadband and ultrafast spectroscopy in both SC and normal states, and discussion of the physical mechanisms (Umklapp scattering and Hartree shift).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Understanding how quasiparticles are generated following a rapid quench of superconductivity in high-Tc cuprates is a key unresolved problem in nonequilibrium superconductivity. Here we resolve these processes in optimally doped YBCO [YBa2Cu3Oy(y=6.94, Tc=92 K)] using broadband (0.16 -4.1 eV, ca. 100 fs) and nearly single-cycle (6 fs) transient reflectivity spectroscopy. We show that within a few femtosecond, enhanced electron-electron Umklapp scattering dominates, signaling a transient modulation of long-range Coulomb interactions on the eV scale. This regime is followed by a rapid suppression of the scattering rate of the mid-infrared absorption associated with carriers dressed by spin fluctuations. We attribute this observation to an ultrafast decoupling of quasiparticles from the spin-fluctuation background, occurring on a 90 fs timescale set by the inverse optical gap. These findings reveal the correlated many-body dynamics underlying quasiparticle generation in cuprates and provide further clues for unconventional pairing mechanism.

</details>

### [Extensive long-range magic in non-Abelian topological orders](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15150v1)

**Authors:** Yuzhen Zhang, Isaac H. Kim, Yimu Bao, Sagar Vijay  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15150v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15150_figures/2605.15150_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Circuit architecture depicting a one-dimensional qudit array with short-range magic.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15150_figures/2605.15150_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Patch construction for non-Abelian states. In a non-Abelian string-net ground-state (left) or a finite energy-density excited state (right) evolved by a depth-d local unitary circuit, there are extensively-many well-separated patches that are anyon-free and hence locally agree with a ground state density matrix. The entanglement bootstrap axioms allow us to define and extract universal topological data within each such patch. A fictitious experiment, involving creating and braiding anyons in each patch reveals the existence of a non-trivial fusion space, which we demonstrate to be inconsistent with stabilizerness. This leads to extensive, long-range magic in either case.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15150_figures/2605.15150_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: (a) Original axioms of the entanglement bootstrap. Left: Sσ0(BC) + Sσ0(C) − Sσ0(B) = 0. Right: Sσ0(BC) + Sσ0(CD) −Sσ0(B) −Sσ0(D) = 0. (b) New axioms, which are weaker but have the advantage of being robust against constant-depth quantum circuits [43]. Left: Sσ(BC) + Sσ(C) −Sσ(B) = 0. Right: the state can be locally extended from BE to BC. That is, let A be the purification of BCDE, there exist a channel ΦBE→BC such that IA ⊗ΦBE→BC(σABE) = σABC.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15150_figures/2605.15150_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: The state σ = Uσ0U † obtained by a constant-depth circuit, along with regions Ω⊂Ω′ used in the proof of Proposition 1. Here, the image is shown in one spatial dimension for convenience.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15150_figures/2605.15150_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: The n-holed disk. The information covex set of the n-holed disk decomposes into a direct sum over convex sets labeled by topological charges measured on the thin annular neighborhoods of its boundary components. a1, . . . , an are the charges in the holes measured by thin annuli surrounding them; an+1 is the overall charge measured by the thin annular neighborhood of the outer boundary. This particular sector is isomorphic to the fusion space where a1, . . . , an fuse into an+1.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that non-Abelian topological orders are fundamentally characterized by extensive, long-range magic that cannot be eliminated by local operations. It establishes that stabilizer states are insufficient to represent these phases, providing a new resource-theoretic way to distinguish topological orders.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental link between topological order and the resource theory of magic, which is crucial for understanding the complexity of preparing non-Abelian states and the robustness of non-Clifford resources in many-body systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether non-Abelian topological orders possess intrinsic, long-range 'magic' (non-Clifford resources) that cannot be removed by constant-depth local unitary circuits.

**Main result.** The authors prove that ground states and low-energy states of non-Abelion string-net models possess extensive long-range magic, and establish a no-go theorem stating that stabilizer states cannot realize these non-Abelian phases.

**Method.** The study utilizes the entanglement bootstrap framework, resource theory of magic, and information-theoretic tools like fidelity bounds and the analysis of information convex sets.

**Model / system.** The primary models are non-Abelian and Abelian string-net models, higher-dimensional quantum double models, and stabilizer states/projection states.

**Key observables.** Long-range magic (LRM), robustness of magic, mutual information, and fusion space dimensions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Circuit depth (d), system size (n), qudit dimension (q), and energy density (epsilon).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The results assume the existence of reference states satisfying the entanglement bootstrap axioms and focus on the regime of constant-depth local unitary transformations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 illustrates the patch construction method; Figure 3(a) shows Abelian string-net phases; Figure 4 shows the transformation of states by depth-d circuits; Figure 5 depicts n-holed disks and topological charge measurement; Figure 6 shows the generation of stabilizers on a ball from smaller balls.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining long-range magic and the problem of non-Abelian complexity, then presents a no-go theorem for stabilizer states using the entanglement bootstrap, extends these findings to low-energy states and higher dimensions, and concludes with bounds on magic scaling and implications for quantum complexity.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that the low-energy states of non-Abelian topological orders possess extensive magic which is long-ranged, and cannot be eliminated by a constant-depth local unitary circuit. This refines conventional notions of complexity beyond the linear circuit depth which is required to prepare any topological phase, and provides a new resource-theoretic characterization of topological orders. A central technical result is a no-go theorem establishing that stabilizer states--even up to constant-depth local unitarie--cannot approximate low-energy states of non-Abelian string-net models which satisfy the entanglement bootstrap axioms. Moreover, we show that stabilizer-realizable Abelian string-net phases have mutual braiding phases quantized by the on-site qudit dimension, and that any violation of this condition necessarily implies extensive long-range magic. Extending to higher spatial dimensions, we argue that any state obeying an entanglement area law and hosting excitations with nontrivial fusion spaces must exhibit extensive long-range magic. This applies, in particular, to ground-states and low-energy states of higher-dimensional quantum double models.

</details>

### [Nonlocal Topological Maxwell Demon Teleporting Ergotropy via Surface-Code Quantum Error Correction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14924v1)

**Authors:** M. Y. Abd-Rabbou, Cong-Feng Qiao  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14924v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `measurement-induced transitions` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14924_figures/2605.14924_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Five-stage ergotropy teleportation protocol. Purple: Alice operations; teal: Bob operations; blue dashed: logical operator ¯ZL; amber dashed: classical channel; red: thermally excited anyon pairs. Alice charges the logical qubit (stage (1)), measures M = 2L boundary plaquettes (stage (2)), and transmits ⃗sA to Bob (stage (3)). Bob decodes via minimum-weight perfect matching (stage (4)) and conditionally charges his battery (stage (5)), extracting ergotropy EB = (2Psucc −1)∆E with energy balance ∆E + Wops + Wbulk = EB + Qdiss (Eq. 6).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14924_figures/2605.14924_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Topological protection of ergotropy transfer. Decod- ing success probability Psucc versus code distance L at fixed separation N = 310 for four error rates. Below pth ≈0.013 (sub-threshold curves, blue, green, and orange), Psucc →1 ex- ponentially with L, demonstrating that topological protection converts physical qubits directly into recoverable ergotropy. Above threshold (p = 0.015, pink), Psucc →1</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14924_figures/2605.14924_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Thermodynamic phase transition at L = 7, N = 40, ∆E = 146.5 J. Stored ergotropy EB = (2Psucc −1)∆E (green dashed), total costs Wops+Wbulk (blue dashed), and net work Wnet (red solid) as functions of the physical error rate p. The demon phase (Wnet &gt; 0, green shading) and thermal phase (Wnet &lt; 0, red shading) are separated by the thermodynamic critical error rate pc ≈0.014 (vertical dotted line), which ex- ceeds the topological threshold pth ≈0.013. The two phase boundaries are physically distinct. The transition is contin- uous, reflecting the smooth degradation of Psucc(p) with in- creasing temperature. Each point: 8000 Monte Carlo shots.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14924_figures/2605.14924_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Ergotropy versus syndrome rounds at L = 7, N = 40, p = 0.005. Without error correction (gray dashed), the er- gotropy decays exponentially on a timescale independent of N, reflecting the entropic instability of two-dimensional topo- logical order at any T &gt; 0 [17]. With active monitoring (or- ange), the demon sustains EB near ∆E over 80 rounds. The shaded area quantifies the ergotropy recovered by continuous operation. It represents the thermodynamic value of the in- formation extracted per syndrome round. Each point: 8000 shots.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to teleport extractable work across a distance using a shared surface code. It shows that while quantum error correction protects the work from thermal noise, the growing cost of maintaining the error-correcting infrastructure imposes a fundamental limit on how far the work can be sent.

**Why it may be interesting.** It bridges quantum error correction with thermodynamics, demonstrating how topological order can be used as a resource for non-local work extraction in the presence of thermal noise.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether ergotropy (extractable work) can be nonlocally transferred at finite temperatures using a Maxwell demon mechanism supported by surface-code quantum error correction.

**Main result.** The protocol achieves exponential protection of transferred ergotropy below a topological threshold and identifies a thermodynamic horizon (N_max) beyond which the quadratic infrastructure costs make the process unprofitable.

**Method.** The authors use a five-stage protocol involving encoding, measurement, classical communication, decoding (via MWPM), and extraction, supported by Stim and PyMatching simulations.

**Model / system.** A rotated surface code on a rectangular lattice with two spatially separated batteries (Alice and Bob) subject to thermal fluctuations and depolarizing noise.

**Key observables.** Net ergotropy (W_net), success probability (P_succ), entropy production (Delta S_total), and information fraction (f).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Topological threshold (p_th), thermodynamic critical error rate (p_c), separation distance (N), and infrastructure cost scaling (N^2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The protocol assumes a causal constraint where syndrome information must travel at a finite signal speed, leading to quadratic scaling of monitoring costs.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows success probability vs. code distance; a phase diagram maps net ergotropy as a function of error rate and information fraction.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nonlocal ergotropy transfer, defines the surface code model and protocol, presents numerical results on error thresholds and profitability, and concludes with a thermodynamic analysis of the cost-benefit trade-off.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce a nonlocal Maxwell demon teleporting ergotropy at finite temperature via classical communication and a shared surface code. The teleported ergotropy is exponentially protected below a topological threshold. We identify a thermodynamic phase transition separating a profitable demon phase from a thermal phase. A quadratic infrastructure cost strictly enforces the second law, imposing a fundamental thermodynamic horizon on separation distance. This establishes quantum error correction as a resource for nonlocal thermodynamics beyond fault-tolerant computation.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.15029"></a>
### [A Resource-Driven Framework for Configurable Entanglement in Quantum Networks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15029v1)

**Authors:** Francesco Mazza, Claudio Pellitteri, Angela Sara Cacciapuoti, Marcello Caleffi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15029v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15029_figures/2605.15029_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1: High level representation of our research problem. The generation and distribution of a multipartite resource state (1) establishes a whatever channel between a subset of network nodes (2). By applying LOCC operations on the shared resource state, entanglement can be reconfigured and shared between a subset of network nodes (3), for instance, parallel communication resources can be extracted through the resolution of the whatever channel.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15029_figures/2605.15029_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: Pictorial representation of the system model and node hierarchy. Tier2 node(s) retains no orchestration qubits Vo of a shared two-colorable graph state, while the κ peer qubits Vc are distributed to Tier1 nodes.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15029_figures/2605.15029_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3: Illustration of the Entanglement Rolling effect induced by a Pauli X measurement on orchestration qubit oi of a GTL resource state (G, ˆκb = 2) with arbitrary length.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15029_figures/2605.15029_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4: Example resource state extraction using the Entangle- ment Rolling procedure on a GTL resource state (G, ˆκb = 3): a configurable sequence of Pauli X measurements at Tier2, followed by local Pauli Z measurements at the Tier1 end- node level, extracts two independent entangled resources (LU equivalent to 3-qubit GHZ states).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15029_figures/2605.15029_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5: Example of (a) Entanglement Rolling with noisy GTL graph state (G, ˆκb = 2). Node labels denote Tier1 nodes; repeated labels indicate that the same Tier1 node holds multiple peer qubits, as the general case discussed in Sec. III. Pauli X measurements at Tier2, followed by local Pauli-Z measurements at Tier1, isolate independent Bell pairs under depolarizing noise (b) Fidelity of the extracted Bell pairs vs. the depolarizing parameter p (one curve per extracted pair).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper proposes a new way to use multipartite entanglement in quantum networks by treating it as a programmable substrate that can be reconfigured into different network topologies. By using a protocol called 'Entanglement Rolling,' users can dynamically create Bell pairs or GHZ states from a shared resource. The study demonstrates that this method is efficient and robust against common types of quantum noise.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a systematic way to manage multipartite entanglement as a programmable resource, which is highly relevant for the development of scalable quantum internet architectures and the control of distributed quantum information.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a resource-driven framework to treat multipartite entanglement as a programmable 'whatever channel' that can be reconfigured into various entanglement-connectivity graphs via LOCC.

**Main result.** The introduction of the 'Entanglement Rolling' protocol, which maximizes the number of concurrent Bell pairs extracted from a GTL resource state, and a proof that this approach remains resilient under realistic depolarizing and dephasing noise.

**Method.** A mathematical framework using graph states and the Noisy Stabilizer Formalism (NSF) to derive closed-form noise maps and analyze the reconfiguration of resource states through Pauli measurements.

**Model / system.** A hierarchical quantum network model consisting of Tier-2 orchestration nodes and Tier-1 peer nodes, utilizing Generalized Tree-like (GTL) graph states as the shared entanglement resource.

**Key observables.** Entanglement fidelity (F), number of extractable Bell pairs/GHZ states, and peer proximity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Bridge rank (r), bridge degree (kappa_b), depolarizing noise parameter (p), and dephasing time (T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The resource states are restricted to two-colorable GTL graph states, and noise is modeled as independent single-qubit Pauli noise.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the three-tier reconfiguration process; Figure 2 depicts the hierarchical network model; Figure 3 illustrates the Entanglement Rolling effect; Figure 4 shows the two-stage extraction process; Figure 5 visualizes noise impact on rolling; Figure 6 presents heatmaps and curves of fidelity thresholds.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the resource-driven framework and the 'whatever channel' concept, introduces the GTL graph state model and structural parameters, formalizes the Entanglement Rolling protocol and its topological effects, provides a noise analysis using the Noisy Stabilizer Formalism, and concludes with numerical validation of fidelity under realistic noise.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Shared multipartite entanglement defines a ``whatever channel'', i.e., a latent communication substrate that does not determine a priori which end-to-end entangled links are activated, but can be configured to support different entanglement-connectivity graphs through Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC). Building on this, we propose a resource-driven framework in which multipartite entanglement is treated as a programmable resource that induces a space of admissible entanglement-graph configurations. Within this framework, connectivity provisioning emerges as a particular instance of a more general resource reconfiguration process. To support this paradigm, we introduce a set of structural design parameters that characterize the operational degrees of freedom of the resource and define the admissible transformations independently of the specific mechanism used to realize them. We then formalize Entanglement Rolling as a measurement-based protocol that operates over the induced configuration space, enabling the systematic reconfiguration of the shared resource across a family of multipartite states. Finally, we analyze the proposed framework under realistic noise conditions. Leveraging the Noisy Stabilizer Formalism (NSF), we derive closed-form noise maps that characterize the effect of noise on the resource transformations and show that the proposed approach maintains reliable performance under relevant noise processes.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [A Toolbox to Understand the Physics of Quantum Data Management](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14719v1)

**Authors:** Wolfgang Mauerer, Manuel Schönberger  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14719v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Overview of our Toolbox: Based on existing quantum formulations for optimisation problems that can be transcribed into a simple intermediate format, we use HPC systems to simulate the dynamics of a quantum annealing process. After post- processing the results (deliberately decoupled from the long-running and compute intensive simulation) to obtain quantities of interest, an accompanying set of visualisation scripts is used to understand the salient characteristics of the problem, and the implications on performance, achievable result quality, etc.; this can lead to (simplified) models, surrogates and others that allow for characterising properties of approaches, or predict...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14719_figures/2605.14719_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 7 shows temporal dynamics of spins how (and thus: optimisa- tion variables) evolve into their final measured states. As they can be in a superposition state, we use expected values ⟨𝜎𝑧 𝑖⟩over nor- malised annealing time. MQO instances exhibit early and largely</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces an open-source computational toolbox designed to analyze the physical properties of quantum annealing applied to database management problems. By simulating energy gaps and spin dynamics, the authors show that query optimization problems possess a complexity structure intermediate between simple ferromagnets and complex spin glasses. This work establishes a foundation for the co-design of quantum algorithms and database systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a bridge between combinatorial optimization and the physics of phase transitions, specifically studying how the ruggedness of energy landscapes in disordered systems (like spin glasses) impacts the success of adiabatic protocols.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of understanding regarding how the physical behavior of quantum annealing relates to the structure and difficulty of database management (DBMS) optimization problems.

**Main result.** The authors developed a computational toolbox that demonstrates MQO problems exhibit structured evolution intermediate between easy ferromagnetic models and hard spin-glass (SK) models, characterized by specific spectral gap behaviors and spin polarization patterns.

**Method.** A physics-informed numerical analysis pipeline using High-Performance Computing (HPC) and matrix-free eigenvalue solvers (SLEPc/PETSc) to simulate the dynamics of time-dependent Hamiltonians.

**Model / system.** A time-dependent Hamiltonian H(s) interpolating between a driver Hamiltonian and a problem-specific Ising/QUBO Hamiltonian, applied to Multi-Query Optimization (MQO) and Sherrington-Kirkpatrick (SK) spin-glass models.

**Key observables.** Minimum spectral gap, energy eigenvalues and eigenstates, spin expectation values, spin-spin correlations, and transition matrix elements.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Annealing parameter (s), system size (N), energy gaps, and the adiabatic condition ratio.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis uses a truncated low-energy subspace approximation, which may fail near avoided crossings or in dense spectra; the study focuses on standard linear annealing schedules.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the toolbox pipeline; Figures 2-3 compare spectral gaps and their locations for SK and MQO; Figures 4-5 show energy branch evolution; Figure 6 analyzes the adiabatic condition ratio; Figure 7 shows spin trajectories; Figure 9 displays spin-spin correlation patterns.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the disconnect between DBMS and quantum physics, presents the toolbox implementation and numerical methods, applies the tool to compare MQO against SK and Ferromagnetic models, and concludes with structural insights into optimization dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The application of quantum computing to data management has attracted growing interest, yet remains constrained by a limited understanding of how the physical behaviour of quantum devices relates to the structure and difficulty of database problems. In particular, evaluating quantum annealing approaches for combinatorial optimisation, which is central to many data management tasks, poses significant challenges beyond the scope of conventional empirical and complexity-theoretic methods.   We present a computational toolbox for the systematic numerical analysis of quantum annealing processes derived from data management problem formulations. Adopting a physics-informed perspective, the toolbox enables the study of spectral and dynamical properties -- such as energy gaps and eigenstate structure -- that are inaccessible through direct hardware measurements, yet essential for understanding computational hardness and scaling behaviour.   Our approach further provides derived quantities and visualisation techniques that support the interpretation of optimisation dynamics, the identification of structural similarities to canonical physical models, and the construction of reduced effective descriptions. By bridging methodological gaps between quantum computing and database systems research, this work establishes a principled foundation for evaluating quantum approaches and guiding future co-design efforts.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.14395"></a>
### [Interference visibility as a witness of preparation contextuality via overlap inequalities](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14395v1)

**Authors:** Mohd Asad Siddiqui  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14395v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `interference shaping light` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14395_figures/2605.14395_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Optimal Bloch-vector configurations for the n-cycle overlap inequality. (a) n = 3: three coplanar detector states with uniform angular separation ∆= π/3 on the equatorial great circle of the Bloch sphere. (b) n = 4: four coplanar detector states with uniform angular separation ∆= π/4. In both panels the Bloch vectors lie on a common great circle and saturate the bound Smax n = n cos2(π/2n) −1, uniquely up to global rotations, reflections, and cyclic relabelings of the detectors.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a way to detect preparation contextuality using simple interference visibility measurements in multi-path interferometers. It proves that for any n-path system, a specific inequality of visibilities can be violated by qubit states, and shows that larger systems actually make the test easier by lowering the required visibility threshold.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a resource-efficient, scalable method for witnessing fundamental quantum properties like contextuality and coherence using only pairwise measurements, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonic quantum information.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing an operational method to test preparation noncontextuality and basis-independent coherence using standard multi-path interferometry without requiring full state tomography or SWAP tests.

**Main result.** The paper derives a tight qubit bound for n-path interferometers, $S_n^{max} = n \cos^2(\pi/2n) - 1$, and shows that violations of these visibility-based inequalities witness preparation contextuality.

**Method.** The author uses Gram matrix positivity and trigonometric parametrization of Bloch vectors to derive overlap inequalities and optimizes the configuration of detector states to find the maximal quantum violation.

**Model / system.** An n-path interferometer where a quantum particle is prepared in a pure state and coupled to detectors, with the detector states encoded as qubits (e.g., photon polarization).

**Key observables.** Two-path interference visibility (V_ij) and the n-cycle overlap expression (S_n).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The visibility threshold (eta_min) required to violate the classical bound, which scales as 1/sqrt(n-1) for large n.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Ideal symmetric path amplitudes, pure qubit detector states, no higher-order interference, and uniform visibility reduction across all path pairs.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the optimal Bloch-vector configurations (equally spaced on a great circle) for n=3 and n=4 cases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of testing noncontextuality, defines the n-path interferometric model, derives the 3-path inequality, generalizes the result to n-paths, performs a robustness analysis for experimental visibility, and compares the method to existing techniques.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that standard multi-path interferometry, using only pairwise visibility measurements, provides an operational route to tests of preparation noncontextuality. Under ideal symmetric conditions, interference visibility directly encodes state overlaps, without requiring tomography or SWAP tests. For three paths, any jointly diagonalizable (coherence-free) description must satisfy ${V}_{12}^2+{V}_{23}^2-{V}_{13}^2\le 1$, where ${V}_{ij}$ are two-path visibilities. Pure qubit detector states violate this bound, achieving a maximal value of $5/4$. We generalize to arbitrary $n$-path interferometers and derive the tight qubit bound $S_n^{\max}=n\cos^2(π/2n)-1$ for all $n\ge3$, achieved by coplanar pure qubit states with uniform angular separation $π/n$. A robustness analysis yields explicit experimental thresholds. Under the operational equivalences used in overlap-based generalized noncontextuality frameworks, violations of these visibility inequalities also witness preparation contextuality. For $n$-cycle inequalities, only the pairwise visibilities appearing in the cycle need to be measured.

</details>

### [Optimizing the preparation of Dicke states using counterdiabatic driving methods](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14378v1)

**Authors:** Fengzhe Tang, Gangcheng Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14378v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Dicke superradiance` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">

The solid lines represent the instantaneous eigenvalues of the Hamil- tonian defined by Eq. The driving field starts to decay at t = 0 and is completely turned off when αt/χ = 1. Under this condi- tion, the chirp rate is set to α = 0.1χ2, the maximum driving field is Ωmax = 0.88χ, and the shear parameter is χ = 10.</figcaption>

The black dashed line shows the driving pulse shape Ω(t). The final state is predominantly prepared in the two lowest energy states. During the evolution, the system success- fully transfers population to these two lowest energy states, while the population in other higher-energy states is significantly reduced. The chirp rate is α = 0.1χ2. Other parameters are the same as Fig. 1.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14378_figures/2605.14378_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 4 shows the result of further increasing the scanning speed in the preparation of two-atom Dicke states. When the chirp rate α increases, the system evolution deviates more quickly from the ideal adiabatic condition, and part of the pop- ulation is transferred to non-target states, leading to a decrease in the population of the target state. This is because α controls the rate of change of the parameters with time. The larger α is, the more difficult it becomes for the system to maintain perfect adiabatic tracking [56, 57], and non-adiabatic effects are enhanced, thereby transferring population from the target state to other energy levels. Therefore, in practical applica- tions, the...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper proposes a method to prepare highly entangled Dicke states using a combination of rapid adiabatic passage and counterdiabatic driving. By applying corrections specifically during the parameter turn-off stage, the authors demonstrate that non-adiabatic transitions can be suppressed, allowing for fast and high-fidelity state preparation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers in AMO and quantum control as it provides a scalable strategy to achieve high-fidelity entanglement in atomic ensembles, which is a prerequisite for quantum metrology and quantum information processing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Efficiently preparing high-fidelity Dicke states while suppressing non-adiabatic transitions that occur during fast-sweeping parameter protocols.

**Main result.** The introduction of counterdiabatic (CD) driving, particularly during the turn-off stage of the protocol, significantly improves the fidelity of Dicke state preparation even at high chirp rates.

**Method.** The authors combine Rapid Adiabatic Passage (RAP) with a variational approach to approximate the counterdiabatic driving term by minimizing an action functional.

**Model / system.** A system of N non-interacting two-level atoms governed by a Hamiltonian featuring one-axis twisting (OAT) interaction, a time-dependent longitudinal field, and a transverse driving field.

**Key observables.** State preparation fidelity and population distribution among Dicke states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Chirp rate (alpha), one-axis twisting strength (chi), and driving strength (Omega).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The effectiveness of the CD correction in the intermediate stage is limited by increasing energy level density as N increases; the derivation assumes the validity of truncated adiabatic gauge potential approximations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows energy level dynamics and avoided crossings; Figure 5 compares target state populations for different CD correction scenarios in a two-atom system; Figure 6 shows population transfer efficiency for a four-particle system as a function of chirp rate.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of Dicke state preparation, defines the OAT Hamiltonian and RAP protocol, develops the counterdiabatic driving framework using a variational approximation, analyzes the impact of CD driving across different evolution stages (turn-on, intermediate, turn-off), and provides numerical verification for small particle numbers.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recently, the technique of counterdiabatic driving, which provides an effective strategy for accelerating adiabatic quantum evolution, has been widely applied in the preparation of many-body quantum states. In this work, we propose a theoretical scheme for the efficient preparation of Dicke states in a system of non-interacting two-level atoms. Our approach leverages the one-axis twisting (OAT) interaction to generate non-classical correlations and combines it with time-dependent external fields to achieve precise control over the dynamics of the system. By employing rapid adiabatic passage (RAP), it demonstrates how the system can be steered from an initial coherent spin state to a target Dicke state with high fidelity [S. C. Carrasco, M. H. Goerz, S. A. Malinovskaya, V. Vuletić, W. P. Schleich, and V. S. Malinovsky, Phys. Rev. Lett. \textbf{132}, 153603 (2024)]. To further optimize the preparation process, we introduce counterdiabatic driving (CD), which suppresses non-adiabatic transitions. Numerical simulations confirm that our scheme can achieve high-fidelity Dicke states for a moderate number of particles. Our results provide a scalable and experimentally feasible approach to prepare Dicke states, with potential applications in quantum metrology, quantum communication, and quantum information processing.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14338"></a>
### [Stopping Reliability in Adaptive Krylov-Shadow Quantum Fisher Information Estimation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14338v1)

**Authors:** Erjie Liu, Yangshuai Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14338v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14338_figures/2605.14338_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Narrow empirical intervals do not certify accuracy under width-only stopping. Termination-time empirical interval width wM versus post hoc absolute error E for all runs (n = 4, five dephasing levels, 50 independent replicates per noise level). The key feature is the cluster of width-only runs in the upper-left region (wM ≤ε, E &gt; ε): these are false stops in the sense of Eq. (15). The empirical gate can pass at the terminal low-K point, but higher-K trajectories and the reference value reveal the unresolved truncation bias identified in Remark IV.2. Guarded stopping removes this upper-left cluster by adding eligibility and persistence gates motivated by the two-component condition of...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14338_figures/2605.14338_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 3. Width-only stopping’s low effective cost re- flects premature termination at a biased value. Median post hoc absolute error versus pϕ (n = 4, 50 independent repli- cates per point). The key feature is the divergence between rules at pϕ ≥0.18: guarded stopping reduces median error by up to a factor of 4.8 relative to width-only stopping despite using up to 16× larger effective terminal budgets at these noise levels, confirming that width-only stopping’s apparent efficiency arises from early termination at a truncation-biased estimate, the mechanism of Remark IV.2.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14338_figures/2605.14338_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 2. Guarded stopping suppresses false success dec- larations under the default resource limit. False-stop rate (FSR, filled markers) and stop rate (SR, open markers) versus dephasing probability pϕ (n = 4, 50 independent repli- cates per point), with 95% Wilson confidence intervals. The key feature is the monotone increase of width-only FSR with pϕ (reaching 0.68 at pϕ = 0.24), while guarded-stopping FSR remains at zero throughout. Guarded SR is also zero at ev- ery tested noise level, so this fixed-resource grid measures false-success suppression; success declarations are tested in the recalibrated sampling-budget sequence of Section V E.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14338_figures/2605.14338_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Guarded calibration geometry at true 5% relative tolerance. Each point is a guarded run from the sampling-budget sequence, normalized by ε = 0.1885. All ter- minal estimates lie below the true-error threshold (horizontal dashed line). The guarded rule declares success only when the bootstrap width is also below threshold (vertical dashed line); otherwise it makes no success declaration at the terminal resource limit.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14338_figures/2605.14338_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Guard-threshold sensitivity under a resource- limited ablation. Each cell is one guard configuration (Kstop min , M stop min , P) aggregated over replicates at fixed noise (pϕ = 0.12, pdep = 0.03), ε = 0.2, resource limits (Kmax, Mmax) = (8, 512). Color and cell text give the false- stop rate. The only configuration with nonzero FSR is the weakest guard, (2, 32, 1). The default (4, 128, 2) is outlined in blue and has zero observed false stops in this ablation.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper identifies a failure mode in adaptive QFI estimation where algorithms stop too early due to unresolved truncation bias. By introducing a 'guarded' stopping rule that enforces minimum resource thresholds, the authors significantly improve the reliability of error certification, though they note challenges in scaling this approach to larger systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum metrology and open quantum systems, as it provides a critical warning that high-precision sampling does not guarantee the control of systematic truncation errors in randomized measurement-based estimation protocols.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the 'false-stop' pathology in adaptive Krylov-shadow Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) estimation, where a stopping rule prematurely declares convergence because it mistakes low sampling variance for true accuracy while ignoring significant Krylov truncation bias.

**Main result.** The authors propose a 'guarded' stopping rule using eligibility thresholds and a persistence condition that suppresses false success declarations and significantly reduces median absolute error. However, they also demonstrate that the guard's effectiveness scales poorly with system size as the Krylov decay rate approaches unity.

**Method.** The authors develop an adaptive interface (AKS-QFI) that monitors both inter-order change (dK) and bootstrap interval width (wM). They implement a guarded rule with minimum thresholds for Krylov order (K) and sampling budget (M), alongside a persistence requirement (P).

**Model / system.** The study uses a noisy mixed-state family of n-qubit systems (n=4, 6, 8) subject to independent single-qubit dephasing and global depolarizing noise. The estimation targets the QFI of a density operator relative to a specific Hamiltonian generator.

**Key observables.** Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) estimate, Krylov-stability measure (dK), empirical uncertainty interval width (wM), False-Stop Rate (FSR), and truncation bias (BK).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Krylov order (K), sampling budget (M), target error tolerance (epsilon), dephasing probability (p_phi), and the persistence parameter (P).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The estimator assumes Hadamard differentiability of the density operator family and exponential decay of the Krylov truncation bias. It also assumes sub-Gaussian concentration for the sampling-side error.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the clustering of width-only runs in a region of low width but high absolute error; subsequent figures illustrate the effectiveness of the guarded rule in reducing error and the scaling failure of the guard at larger system sizes.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the false-stop mechanism, provides a two-component error analysis, introduces the guarded stopping rule, validates it via benchmarks on 4-qubit systems, and finally assesses scaling limitations for larger qubit numbers.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Adaptive quantum Fisher information (QFI) estimation requires a stopping rule that distinguishes accuracy from apparent numerical stability. For Krylov-shadow QFI estimators, finite Krylov order $K$ produces truncation bias, while finite sample budget $M$ produces finite-$M$ sampling-side error. We show that a width-only empirical stopping rule, based on interval width and local Krylov stability, can declare convergence at small $(K,M)$ even when the post hoc error exceeds the requested tolerance; we call this event a \emph{false stop}. The mechanism is a narrow empirical interval centered on a biased low-$K$ estimate. We give a two-component stopping analysis that separates the Krylov and sampling terms, and we implement a guarded rule that permits a success declaration only after minimum thresholds in $K$ and $M$ and a persistence condition are satisfied. On a five-level dephasing benchmark at $n=4$ qubits, the guarded rule suppresses the false success declarations produced by the width-only empirical rule, whose false-stop rates range from $0.16$ to $0.68$ across the tested noise levels. Under the main fixed resource limit, the guarded rule refuses to make success declarations rather than accepting biased low-$K$ estimates; a separate true-relative-tolerance sampling-budget sequence then shows that, after Krylov and sampling recalibration, the same decision principle can make success declarations without observed false stops. These results show that stopping reliability is a distinct design requirement for adaptive QFI estimation: sampling precision at fixed $K$ does not by itself establish that Krylov truncation bias is controlled.

</details>

### [Transitions as the Native Objects of Dispersive Light-Matter Dynamics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14096v1)

**Authors:** Meguebel Mohamed, Maxime Federico, Louis Garbe, Nadia Belabas, Nicolas Fabre  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14096v1>

Detunings are indicated by loops at the atomic states reached by the transitions and positioned below or above the matter axis depending on whether the photon is absorbed (+ωc) or emitted (−ωc). Hermitian con- jugates – associated with opposite detunings – are obtained by mirror symmetry, i.e, by reversing the transition direction (exchanging initial and final states) and replacing ˆac ↔ˆa† c. The time-dependent weights associated with |g⟩⟨g| ⊗ˆa† cˆac, |g⟩⟨g| ⊗ˆacˆa† c and |e⟩⟨g| ⊗(ˆac)3 are, W Stark 1 (t), W BS 1 (t) and W2(t), respectively, and the off-resonant dia- grams mentioned in the text are sketched in the Supplemen- tary Material but are discarded over a time large with respect to their associated detunings.</figcaption>

In the state-centric view, the zeroth- order objects are the states |k, n⟩k=e,g (infinitely many in the Rabi Hamiltonian), with energies set by the free Hamilto- nian. The interaction Hamiltonian induces transitions within this fixed state space. In the Jaynes-Cummings model, dy- namics occur in subspaces {|e, n⟩, |g, n + 1⟩}n≥0 with photon- number-dependent Rabi frequency Ωn = p</figcaption>
</figure>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new theoretical framework that treats light-matter transitions, rather than states, as the primary objects of dynamics. This approach simplifies the derivation of high-order effective Hamiltonians and reveals that the dispersive regime is a reorganization of resonant polaritonic hybridization. It successfully identifies an intrinsic, photon-number-independent Rabi frequency in the operator-centric description.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a more efficient, transparent, and unified way to derive effective Hamiltonians for complex light-matter interactions without the need for complex unitary transformations or Hilbert space truncation, which is highly relevant for cavity and waveguide QED.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of tracking high-order processes and deriving effective Hamiltonians in the dispersive regime using traditional state-centric methods, which can become computationally overloaded.

**Main result.** The introduction of a transition-centric framework that unifies resonant and dispersive regimes, revealing a photon-number-independent intrinsic Rabi frequency and persistent polaritonic hybridization.

**Method.** A transition-operator diagrammatic perturbation theory in Liouville space, using JLM (Jaynes-Cummings-Liouville) transition operators as fundamental building blocks and Laplace space transformations for time-dependent weights.

**Model / system.** A two-level atomic system coupled to a cavity mode, described by the Rabi and Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonians, including both rotating-wave and counter-rotating terms.

**Key observables.** Intrinsic Rabi frequency, Stark shifts, Bloch-Sizable shifts, and the polaritonic joint population operator.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coupling strength (lambda), detuning (Delta), atomic frequency, and cavity frequency; regimes include resonant, dispersive, and ultrastrong coupling.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The expansion is valid in the dispersive regime where coupling is small compared to detunings; the operator subspace is assumed to be closed by including the joint population term.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a 2D representation of JLM transition operators and a zeroth-order diagram for the quantum Rabi interaction showing detunings.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a new transition-centric framework, develops a diagrammatic perturbation theory for higher-order effective Hamiltonians, applies the method to the Jaynes-Cummings and Rabi models, and demonstrates the unification of resonant and dispersive dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce a framework where light-matter transitions, rather than states, are the primary dynamical objects. Successive compositions of elementary transitions yield multiphoton processes with compact diagrammatic bookkeeping of resonant and off-resonant pathways. This approach enables transparent derivations of effective high-order Hamiltonians in the dispersive regime, foundational to quantum-information applications. Applied to the paradigmatic Jaynes-Cummings model, our framework reveals a photon-number-independent intrinsic Rabi frequency and persistent polaritonic hybridization in the dispersive regime, unifying resonant and dispersive limits.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.14538"></a>
### [Are free choices absolute, when internalized in Wigner's friend?](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14538v1)

**Authors:** Laurens Walleghem  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14538v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14538_figures/2605.14538_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Protocol; Alice and Bob each make a choice a, b ∈{0, 1}. They encode their choices in the computational basis of qubits that they send to Charlie and Debbie, who choose x, y whether to decode their outcomes or measure their received qubit in the X-basis. Wigner, a superobserver for Alice and Bob, chooses z = 0, 1. For z = 0 he asks Alice and Bob for their choices while for z = 1 he measures Alice’s and Bob’s labs jointly in an entangled PBR measurement. The only events unavailable after the protocol are the ‘free’ choices a, b of Alice and Bob.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a theoretical argument showing that if an observer is treated as a quantum system, their experimental choices cannot be considered absolute. By merging Wigner's friend paradoxes with the PBR theorem, the author demonstrates that 'free choice' must be a relational property rather than an absolute one.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is interesting for researchers in quantum foundations and open quantum systems as it challenges the fundamental status of experimental agency and explores the limits of observer-independent reality in complex quantum networks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether the 'free choices' (experimental settings) of observers are absolute or relative when those observers are internalized as quantum systems within a Wigner's friend-style setup.

**Main result.** The author derives a contradiction against the absoluteness of free choices by combining the Local Friendliness (LF) no-go theorem with the Pusey-Barrett-Rudolph (PBR) argument, suggesting that free choices must have a relational nature.

**Method.** The study uses a theoretical construction that combines the PBR scenario with an extended Wigner's friend setup, employing entanglement swapping and logical contextuality arguments to derive a contradiction at the possibilistic level.

**Model / system.** The model involves a multi-agent setup with two friends (Alice and Bob) in sealed labs, a super-observer (Wigner), and external observers (Charlie and Debbie) interacting with qubits (TA and TB) that encode the friends' choices.

**Key observables.** Measurement outcomes for the agents (a, b, c, d, w) and measurement settings (x, y, z).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The choice amplitudes (alpha and beta) and the distinction between the computational and X-basis measurements.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The argument relies on the assumptions of Local Agency, Absoluteness of Observed Events (AOE), and the validity of the PBR theorem and quantum universality.

**Figures summary.** A sketch of the protocol combining the Wigner's friend scenario with the PBR argument.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Wigner's friend problem, defines the extended setup using the PBR framework, presents the mathematical derivation of the contradiction against the absoluteness of free choices, and discusses the implications for quantum interpretations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Wigner's thought experiment illustrates quantum theory's measurement problem by considering an observer who measures a quantum system inside a sealed lab, modeled unitarily by an outsider. Recent extensions of this thought experiment, referred to as extended Wigner's friend arguments, question how different observers can reason consistently about each other in quantum setups, and challenge the absoluteness of the outcome value obtained by the friend under a notion of locality. In this work, we present an argument against the absoluteness of free choices under the same notion of locality, using an extended Wigner's friend scenario based on the Pusey--Barrett--Rudolph theorem. Similar arguments based on other contextuality or nonlocality models are possible.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Corner Charge Fluctuations in Higher Dimensions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13971v1)

**Authors:** Xiao-Chuan Wu, Pok Man Tam, Xuyang Liang, Zenan Liu, Dao-Xin Yao, Zheng Yan, Shinsei Ryu  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13971v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

(16), in a two-band Weyl semimetal model. One of the partition planes are taken to be parallel to a cubic lattice plane.</figcaption>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper extends the theory of corner charge fluctuations to three and higher dimensions, providing a universal framework to study quantum geometry and criticality. By analyzing trihedral corners and wedges, the authors show how to extract the many-body quantum metric and identify dimension-dependent scaling laws. The results are validated through Quantum Monte Carlo simulations of Heisenberg and Weyl semimetal models.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it provides a theoretical foundation for using real-space correlation measurements, such as those in quantum gas microscopy, to probe universal quantum geometry and criticality in higher dimensions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to generalize the framework of corner charge fluctuations from two spatial dimensions to higher dimensions (d >= 3) and to develop a protocol for extracting many-body quantum geometry in 3D.

**Main result.** The authors derived a universal angle dependence for trihedral corners in 3D and identified a wedge-corner contribution that probes the quantum metric, while establishing dimension-dependent scaling laws for insulators, CFTs, and metals.

**Method.** The study combines analytical derivations using Gram matrices and multi-region subtraction schemes with numerical Quantum Monte Carlo simulations of Heisenberg and Weyl semimetal models.

**Model / system.** The work investigates various many-body systems including the Columnar-Dimerized (CD) and Double-Cubic (DC) AFM Heisenberg models at the O(3) quantum critical point, as well as a lattice Weyl semimetel model.

**Key observables.** Bipartite charge fluctuations (second cumulant of the disorder operator), dimensionless corner contribution (C3), universal angle function (f3), and the quantum metric (S2).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spatial dimensions (d >= 3), corner/wedge angles, radial moment (Upsilon_d), and the distinction between insulating, conformal (CFT), and metallic regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation assumes continuous translation symmetry and rotationally invariant (isotropic) systems, and assumes the analyticity of the static structure factor in even dimensions.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the Heisenberg models used, finite-size extrapolations of the angle function, the real-space partition scheme for wedge-corner fluctuations, and the scaling of the disorder operator.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of higher-dimensional fluctuations, provides analytical derivations for 3D trihedral corners and general d-dimensional parallelotopes, presents numerical validation using QMC on spin models, demonstrates quantum metric extraction via Weyl semimetals, and concludes with scaling analysis across different phases of matter.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Measuring charge fluctuations within a subregion provides a powerful probe of quantum many-body systems. In two spatial dimensions, the shape dependence of the dimensionless corner contribution encodes universal data of quantum critical points and reveals observables of quantum geometry in various quantum phases. Here, we systematically extend this framework to higher dimensions. In three dimensions, we derive the universal angle dependence associated with trihedral corners of a generic parallelepiped and benchmark the predictions against Monte Carlo simulations of lattice models at the O(3) quantum critical point. We further identify a wedge-corner contribution that directly probes the quantum metric, supported by numerical results for a lattice Weyl semimetal model. More generally, we obtain angle functions for polyhedral corners of arbitrary parallelotopes in general dimensions and clarify the scaling of the corner contribution across phases of matter. While insulators and conformal critical points exhibit similar behavior across dimensions, metals display a characteristic even-odd dimensional effect.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14644"></a>
### [Generating Non-Decomposable Maps with Differentiable Semidefinite Programming](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14644v1)

**Authors:** Angela Rosy Morgillo, Davide Poderini, Fabio Anselmi, Fabio Benatti, Massimiliano F. Sacchi, Chiara Macchiavello  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14644v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14644_figures/2605.14644_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic overview of the optimization framework. A parametrized Choi matrix C, optionally constrained by a mask, is processed by two SDP layers. The first evaluates the witness-based objective ζ1, which certifies non-decomposability when it becomes negative. The second evaluates ζk, a positivity certificate based on PPT k-symmetric extensions. These quantities are combined in the loss function (21), possibly together with additional penalty terms implementing further structural constraints. Minimizing this loss with Adam yields an updated Choi matrix ˜C whose associated map is positive and non-decomposable.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14644_figures/2605.14644_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Example of a Choi matrix associated with a positive non-decomposable map on C4 ⊗C4.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14644_figures/2605.14644_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Examples of real Choi matrices corresponding to non-decomposable maps on C2 ⊗C4, obtained using different masking patterns in the optimization procedure. Nonzero entries indicate the locations selected by the masks.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14644_figures/2605.14644_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Number of epochs required to reach convergence, defined as the point at which the loss function in Eq. (21) becomes zero, as a function of the hyperparameters γ and ϵ.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14644_figures/2605.14644_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 4 shows the number of epochs required for conver- gence, defined as the point at which the loss in Eq. (21) reaches zero, as a function of γ and ϵ. For most param- eter choices, convergence is achieved in fewer than 400 epochs. There are, however, combinations with small γ and large ϵ for which convergence is not observed. This is consistent with the interpretation of the loss: increasing ϵ imposes a stronger requirement on non-decomposability, while decreasing γ weakens the relative pressure toward positivity, making it more difficult to identify a positive non-decomposable map. We also examine how ϵ and γ affect the loss trajec- tory during training. Figs. 5 and 6 show the evolution</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The authors present a new optimization method using differentiable semidefinite programming to systematically generate positive non-decomposable quantum maps. This framework allows for the discovery of new map families and provides numerical evidence for long-standing conjectures in quantum information theory. It effectively automates the search for complex mathematical structures used to detect bound entanglement.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful new numerical tool for exploring the geometry of quantum channels and entanglement, which is directly relevant to understanding the structure of open quantum systems and entanglement detection.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of systematically constructing positive, non-decomposable linear maps, which are essential for detecting bound entangled states.

**Main result.** The development of a differentiable SDP framework that successfully generates new numerical examples of non-decomposable maps, identifies a new parametrized family of maps, and provides numerical evidence for the PPT square conjecture.

**Method.** An optimization framework using differentiable semidefinite programming (SDP) layers within a gradient-based loop (Adam optimizer) to minimize a loss function based on positivity and non-decomposability certificates.

**Model / system.** Linear maps between finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, represented via the Choi-Jamiołkowski isomorphism using Choi matrices.

**Key observables.** Positivity certificates (via k-symmetric extensions), non-decomposability witnesses (zeta_1), and trace-preservation properties.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Input and output dimensions (d, d'), extension order (k), and loss function hyperparameters (epsilon, gamma, omega, nu).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of k-symmetric extensions provides a sufficient but not necessary condition for positivity, which may exclude some valid maps.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the optimization framework flow; Figure 2 displays Choi matrices and masks; Figure 3 shows real non-decomposable maps; Figure 4 shows convergence relative to hyperparameters; Figures 5-7 illustrate the evolution of loss functions and SDP objectives during training.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of non-decomposable maps, presents the differentiable SDP optimization framework, details the loss function engineering and masking techniques, demonstrates results including new map families and conjecture testing, and discusses limitations and future work.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Positive maps that are not decomposable are a key resource in entanglement theory because they can detect bound entangled states, yet systematic methods for constructing them remain limited. We introduce an optimization framework based on differentiable semidefinite programming (SDP) for generating positive non-decomposable maps under flexible structural constraints on their Choi matrices. The method combines SDP-based certificates of non-decomposability and positivity with gradient-based optimization, enabling a systematic search over maps with different input and output dimensions. Within this framework, we generate previously unknown numerical examples, identify a parametrized family of maps arising from masked Choi matrices, and construct real non-decomposable maps. We further show that the same approach can be adapted to explore open questions in quantum information theory, including the PPT square conjecture and recently proposed eigenvalue bounds for 2-positive trace-preserving maps.

</details>

**Authors:** Natsuki Kariya  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14432v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `interference shaping light` **2/5**
