<img src="2605.14080_figures/2605.14080_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic of the proposed circuit including Bloch transistor (BT), outlined by grey rectangle, and the two com- plementary circuits, i.e., the Bloch and the Josephson circuits. The series Bloch circuit includes high-ohmic resistance Rb, inductance Lb, voltage noise source Vf, and constant voltage source V0. Current IB = dq/dt is injected into the BT is- land; the island voltage VB depends on both quasicharge q and total phase ϕ. The parallel Josephson circuit comprises low-ohmic resistance Rs, rather large capacitance Cs, current noise source If, and constant current source I0. The BT volt- age VJ = dϕ/dt, while supercurrent Is depends on both phase ϕ and quasicharge q.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14080_figures/2605.14080_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Elementary cell (0 ≤q ≤2e and 0 ≤ϕ ≤2π) of the double-periodic surfaces of the two lowest energy bands, i.e., the ground state band (n = 0) and the first band (n = 1). The specific shape of these Bloch bands has been used in engineering the charge-phase qubits with inherently small decoherence due to operating in the so-called sweet points [39, 41].</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14080_figures/2605.14080_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Egg box potential Eq.(51) with adjusted individual slopes for variables θ and ϕ, ensuring steady motion down- wards along ”diagonal” path (schematically shown by the red dashed line). This motion is associated with the circuit oper- ation, when a working point is fixed on the step. The dimen- sions of the egg-box elementary cell are 1 × 1.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14080_figures/2605.14080_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Autonomous IV-curves of the Bloch circuit (the blue lines). The insets show the dual Shapiro steps of constant cur- rent in ”embryo stage” (µ →1), appearing at IB = eωJ/π. The dashed lines show the effect of relatively large noise. (a) Average current IB versus voltage V0. (b) The same I-V char- acteristic re-plotted as IB against VB.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14080_figures/2605.14080_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Transconductance I-V curves exhibiting slanted steps, where both the differential transconductance dIB/dVJ and the ratio IB/VJ take the fundamental value of R−1 Q . The curves are plotted at sweep of current I0 for three fixed values of voltage V0 = V01, V02, and V03.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to use a DC-driven Bloch transistor to create a quantum resistance standard. By phase-locking Josephson and Bloch oscillations, the device exhibits quantized transconductance related to the resistance quantum. This approach offers a way to perform quantum metrology without the need for high magnetic fields.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the possibility of achieving phase-locking between Josephson oscillations and Bloch oscillations in a DC-driven Bloch transistor to establish a new quantum standard of resistance.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that DC-driven phase-locking leads to the formation of dual Shapiro steps and a fundamental transconductance of 1/Rq, providing a resistance standard that does not require strong magnetic fields.

**Method.** The study uses a modified Resistively Shunted Junction (RSJ) model, a double Fourier series expansion of the ground-state energy, and the slowly-varying phase method to analyze the dynamics.

**Model / system.** A Bloch transistor (BT) consisting of two small-capacitance Josephson junctions in series with a superconducting island, coupled to a Josephson circuit and driven by DC voltage and current sources.

**Key observables.** Josephson frequency (fJ), Bloch frequency (fB), transconductance (IB/VJ), current steps (dual Shapiro steps), and island voltage (VB).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Resistance quantum (Rq), asymmetry parameter (a), modulation depth (mu), blockade voltage (Vc), and the high-frequency/low-impedance regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is assumed to remain in its ground state (adiabatic approximation), the Josephson part is overdamped, and the backaction of the Bloch transistor on the Josephson circuit is negligible.

**Figures summary.** Figure 5 shows transconductance curves with slanted steps; Figure 6 illustrates a BT design using a dc SQUID for tuning; Figure 7 presents a proposed superconducting version of the quantum metrology triangle.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by proposing the device and its physical mechanism, moves to the Hamiltonian and circuit dynamics, develops the mathematical framework for phase-locking and Fourier analysis, calculates specific observables like step width and transconductance, and concludes with implications for quantum metrology.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a superconducting circuit based on the Bloch transistor, a quantum device consisting of two small-capacitance Josephson junctions connected in series and having a small island in between. This device is driven by two dc electrical sources controlling Josephson oscillations of frequency $f_J = 2e\overline{V_J}/h$, related to the average voltage $\overline{V_J}$ on the transistor, and Bloch oscillations of frequency $f_B = \overline{I_B}/2e$, related to the average current $\overline{I_B}$ injected into the transistor island. Due to the Bloch transistor properties, these two types of oscillations can mutually phase lock, i.e., $f_J = f_B$. This leads to formation of current steps on the current-voltage curve at $\overline{I}_B = 2ef_J$, which are similar to the dual Shapiro steps appearing at current $\overline{I}=2ef$ under microwave irradiation of frequency $f$. Moreover, transconductance $\overline{I_B}/\overline{V_J}$ takes the fundamental value of $1/R_Q$, where $R_Q = h/4e^2$ is the resistance quantum. The obtained results pave the way to the alternative quantum standard of resistance, based on the superconducting circuit and operating without applying strong magnetic field.

</details>

### [Dynamical scaling near the pseudogap quantum critical point of the two-dimensional Hubbard model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15060v1)

**Authors:** Mathias Pelz, Gabriel Kotliar, Jan von Delft, Andreas Gleis  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15060v1>

(a) σ′ cl(ω, T) vs. ω for 12 temperatures, indicated by ticks on the color bar; in the NFL regime, σ′ cl scales as ω−1 (dashed line). Grey curves show the cluster bubble contribution σ′ cl,B, computed from dressed propagators without vertex contribu- tions. (b) Tσ′ cl(ω, T) vs. x = ω/T, showing a scaling collapse onto tanh(x/2)/x (dash-dotted curve). (c) Vertex contribu- tion σ′ cl,V = σ′ cl −σ′ cl,B, plotted vs. ω. In the NFL regime it is orders of magnitude larger than σ′ cl,B, and (d) obeys the same ω/T scaling as the full cluster response σ′ cl.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15060_figures/2605.15060_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. The spectral function Ak(ω = 0), computed at µ = 1.24 for (a) the PG phase at T = 10−7t, (b) the NFL at T = 10−3t and (c) the NFL at T = 10−1t. The black dotted line indicates the antiferromagnetic zone boundary. (d) The spectral part of the self-energy at the nodal patch K = (0, 0), Σ′′ (0,0)(ω, T) vs. ω, and (e) Σ′′ (0,0)(ω, T)/T vs. x = ω/T.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15060_figures/2605.15060_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Spectral part of the cluster optical conductivity, σ′′ cl(ω, T), at µ = 1.24. (a) σ′′ cl vs. ω for 12 different temper- atures, and (b) Tσ′′ cl vs. x = ω/T, showing a scaling collapse onto X ′′(x)/x (black dash-dotted curve). Black dashed lines indicate power law behavior.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper uses DCA and NRG to show that the 2D Hubbard model exhibits $\omega/T$ scaling and strange-metal transport near a quantum critical point. It demonstrates that the optical response is dominated by vertex contributions and identifies Marginal Fermi Liquid behavior in the nodal regions, providing a theoretical basis for observations in cuprate superconductors.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the dynamical scaling and the microscopic origin of the pseudogap-to-Fermi-liquid transition and the associated strange-metal behavior in the 2D Hubbard model.

**Main result.** The study finds $\omega/T$ scaling in spin and cluster-current susceptibilities, a dominant vertex contribution to optical conductivity leading to $1/T$ conductivity, and evidence for Marginal Fermi Liquid behavior in the nodal self-energy.

**Method.** Four-patch Dynamical Cluster Approximation (DCA) combined with the Numerical Renormalability Group (NRG) as a cluster impurity solver.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional square-lattice $t$-$t'$-$U$ Hubbard model representing hole-doped cuprates, specifically focusing on the regime near critical doping $p^* \simeq 0.14$.

**Key observables.** Local spin susceptibility, cluster-current susceptibility, optical conductivity (including bubble and vertex contributions), and nodal/antinodal electronic spectral functions and self-energy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Critical doping $p^* \simeq 0.14$, temperature scales $T_{FL}$ and $T_{NFL}$, and the scaling variable $x = \omega/T$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The self-energy is approximated as momentum-independent within four patches; the results may require more accurate computations to solidify the MFL claim.

**Figures summary.** Phase diagrams showing $T=0$ transitions; scaling collapses of spin and current correlators; $\omega/T$ scaling of susceptibilities and optical conductivity; and decomposition of current-current correlations by type.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes a phase diagram, identifies the non-Fermi-liquid regime, demonstrates dynamical scaling in spin and current responses, analyzes the vertex contribution to transport, and examines nodal/antinodal spectral dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study dynamical scaling in the quantum-critical fan of the pseudogap-metal to Fermi-liquid transition of the two-dimensional Hubbard model. Using a four-patch dynamical cluster approximation with the numerical renormalization group as a cluster impurity solver, we access real-frequency dynamics over several decades at arbitrary temperatures. Close to the critical doping, the local spin and cluster-current susceptibility spectra exhibit $x=ω/T$ scaling of the form $χ''(ω,T)\sim \tanh(x/2)$, and the cluster contribution to the optical conductivity obeys $Tσ'_{\mathrm{cl}}(ω,T) \sim \tanh(x/2)/x$, implying a $1/T$ cluster dc conductivity. In the scaling regime, the vertex contribution to the cluster optical response is much larger than the bubble contribution. We further find evidence for a marginal-Fermi-liquid nodal self-energy. This, together with the $1/T$ vertex contribution to the conductivity, implies strange-metal optical transport in the quantum critical region. Our results describe several qualitative aspects of several experimental observations.

</details>

### [Energy efficiency of quantum computers](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15090v1)

**Authors:** Miquel Carrasco-Codina, Pau Escofet, Paul Hilaire, Ariane Soret, Sam Nerenberg, Victor Champain, Gerard Milburn, Klara Theophilo, Sophie H. Li, Irais Bautista, Andrés Gómez, Jose Miralles, Sergi Abadal, Carmen G. Almudéver, Eduard Alarcón, Raja Yehia  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15090v1>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a framework to benchmark the energy efficiency of different quantum computing architectures. By analyzing the power profiles of superconducting, spin, ion, atom, and photonic platforms, it highlights the trade-offs between hardware complexity and computational throughput. The work is significant for assessing the environmental and economic sustainability of future quantum technologies.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a critical resource-based perspective on the scalability of quantum hardware, which is essential for understanding the long-term viability of large-scale quantum architectures and their integration with classical control systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Quantifying the energy consumption and energy efficiency of various quantum computing platforms to determine their potential sustainability compared to classical computers.

**Main result.** The authors establish a standardized framework for benchmarking energy efficiency (computations per Joule) and identify that while cooling is the primary energy driver in NISQ-era devices, control electronics will likely dominate as architectures scale.

**Method.** The study uses a hardware-aware approach, defining efficiency as the ratio of algorithms performed to energy consumed, and incorporates platform-specific compilation models (accounting for routing and gate sets).

**Model / system.** The analysis covers five representative platforms: superconducting qubits, silicon spin qubits, trapped ions, neutral atoms, and photonic qubits, using power values from existing hardware components.

**Key observables.** Energy efficiency (computations per Joule), total power consumption (P), circuit depth (D), and number of samples (N_samples).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Two-qubit gate time, reset time, qubit connectivity graphs, and the ratio of two-qubit gates to total layers.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model focuses on NISQ-era power consumption, excludes manufacturing/life-cycle costs, and assumes power consumption remains relatively constant between idle and active states.

**Figures summary.** A power breakdown chart showing the percentage of energy used by cooling, control, and classical components; and plots of energy efficiency versus circuit depth and number of possible computations over 24 hours.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining a new energy efficiency metric, followed by a platform-by-platform analysis (solid-state, atom-based, and photonic), and concludes with a discussion on scaling, limitations, and the potential rebound effect.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

How much energy does a quantum computer consume? Are they more efficient than their classical counterparts? In this work, we make a step towards answering these questions. We define the energy efficiency of a quantum computer as the ratio of the number of algorithms it can perform during a given time over the energy consumed by the hardware during this time. We analyze the most representative physical platforms currently envisioned to be used as building blocks of quantum computers: superconducting qubits, silicon spin qubits, trapped ions, neutral atoms and photonic qubits. Including insights from experts in all these technologies and taking into account algorithm compilation constraints, we discuss the advantages and inconveniences of each platform from an energy standpoint. Beyond providing concrete values of the energy consumption of current quantum computers, we lay the foundation of a framework to benchmark the energy efficiency of any future quantum computing architecture.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes a new superconducting circuit called 'Fraxonium' that uses engineered Josephson potentials to create stable, multi-level quantum systems (qudits). By using fractional fluxon states, these qudits are naturally protected from leakage errors, offering a scalable path for more efficient quantum computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** It presents a novel way to engineer multi-level quantum systems with geometric protection, which is highly relevant for researchers working on holonomic quantum computing and controlled quantum dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need to expand the local Hilbert space for quantum computing using qudits (d > 2) that are protected from leakage errors, which is a major limitation in current transmon-based architectures.

**Main result.** The proposal of 'Fraxonium,' a superconducting circuit architecture that uses engineered multi-harmonic Josephson potentials to create d degenerate low-lying 'fraxon' states protected from leakage.

**Method.** Fourier engineering of the Josephson potential using a multi-harmonic approach, combined with WKB approximations for tunneling and numerical diagonalization of the Hamiltonian.

**Model / system.** A generalized fluxonium circuit featuring a capacitor, inductor, and a tailored Josephson element. The Hamiltonian includes a multi-harmonic potential U_d(phi) created via 'kite' building blocks.

**Key observables.** Energy spectrum, tunneling matrix elements, dipole matrix elements for phase and charge operators, and transition energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Energy scales EJ, EC, and EL; the regime EJ >> EC, EL; and the periodicity of the engineered potential.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The regime EJ >> EC, EL is assumed; higher energy excitations (plasmons) and non-nearest-neighbor tunneling are neglected; and the potential is treated as a sum of Fourier harmonics.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the circuit diagram and the engineered potential with low-energy eigenstates. Figure 3 displays the spectrum for a ququart system. Figure 6 and 7 show dipole matrix elements and transition energies for the qutrit system.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the qudit problem, proposes the Fraxonium architecture, details the Fourier engineering method for potential sculpting, analyzes specific d=3, 4, and 5 cases, and proposes a STIRAP protocol for gate implementation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a superconducting circuit hosting $d$ low-lying states, well separated from the rest of the spectrum, that naturally realizes a qudit system protected from leakage errors. The system represents a generalization of the fluxonium and the low-energy states are constituted by fractional fluxon states, that we call {\it fraxons}, localized in the minima of a suitably designed Josephson potential. The latter is tailored through a Fourier engineering approach, that employs multi-harmonic Josephson building block elements composed by a Josephson junction and an inductance connected in series. We present the spectrum of a $d=4$ and a $d=5$ qudit system and study in detail the qutrit case. We analyze the dipole matrix elements for coupling to radiation and propose a non-Abelian, stimulated Raman adiabatic passage (STIRAP) protocol for single-qutrit gates, that is particularly suited for the present system. The proposed platform opens novel perspectives in circuit engineering and quantum computing beyond the qubit paradigm.

</details>

**Authors:** Carles Martorell, Rubén Calvo, Alessia Annibale, Miguel A. Muñoz  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14916v1>

**Summary.** This paper extends the classic Sompolinsky-Crisanti-Sommers theory to E/I networks with structured inhibition. It demonstrates that tuning how inhibition targets different populations can fundamentally reorganize the network's phase diagram, even suppressing chaos in favor of synchronized oscillations.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding how target-specific inhibition in recurrent excitatory-inhibitory (E/I) networks regulates the emergence of macroscopic dynamical regimes like asynchronous chaos, persistent activity, and coherent oscillations.

**Main result.** The study identifies three distinct classes of phase diagrams based on inhibitory coupling, showing that target-specific inhibition can suppress chaotic fluctuations and drive the system into coherent oscillatory activity.

**Method.** The authors use Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) and the Martin-Siggia-Rose-De Dominicis-Janssen formalism to derive self-consistent equations for macroscopic observables and perform linear stability analysis.

**Model / system.** A two-population recurrent neural network consisting of segregated excitatory and inhibitory neurons with sparse, asymmetric Gaussian connectivity and target-specific inhibitory couplings.

**Key observables.** Mean activities, two-time autocorrelation functions, Largest Lyapunov Exponent (LLE), Kuramoto order parameter, and phase lag between populations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Target-specific inhibition parameters (beta and delta), coupling strength (g), connectivity mean (J0) and variance (J2), and the fraction of excitatory neurons (f).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is performed in the thermodynamic limit (N to infinity), assumes a tanh activation function, and uses a sparse connectivity construction to preserve Dale's principle.

**Figures summary.** Figures show phase diagrams and heat maps of mean activity and autocorrelation, trajectories of E/I activity, eigenvalue spectra, and the Largest Lyapunov Exponent across parameter spaces.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the E/I network model, develops the DMFT framework for sparse networks, classifies phase diagrams based on inhibitory parameters, analyzes stability transitions (Type I and II), and validates results with numerical simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Biological neural networks can operate in qualitatively distinct dynamical regimes, and transitions between these regimes are thought to underlie changes in computation and behavior. The seminal work of Sompolinsky, Crisanti, and Sommers (SCS) showed that random recurrent networks undergo a transition from quiescence to asynchronous chaos, establishing a paradigmatic link between random connectivity, dynamical instability, and internally generated fluctuations in neural circuits. Here, we extend this framework to two-population firing-rate networks with segregated excitatory and inhibitory neurons and target-specific inhibitory couplings that break excitation--inhibition balance. Using dynamical mean-field theory, we derive self-consistent equations for the macroscopic mean activities and autocorrelations, together with stability criteria distinguishing mean-driven and fluctuation-driven instabilities. We show that target-specific inhibition organizes the phase diagram into three qualitative classes: inhibition-dominated or strictly balanced networks display only quiescent activity and asynchronous chaos; excitation-dominated networks display persistent activity together with either synchronous chaos with non-vanishing mean activity or coherent oscillations, depending on the stability-matrix eigenvalues. Crucially, coherent oscillations do not coexist with chaotic fluctuations around the periodic mean trajectory; rather, their onset suppresses the chaotic component, reminiscent of input-induced suppression of chaos. These results generalize SCS theory to recurrent networks with explicit excitatory--inhibitory structure and identify target-specific inhibition as a key control parameter for large-scale neural dynamics.

</details>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 4 shows a similar analysis for βℏω = 4.89, this time examining the convergence of the potential energy, calculated using Equation (19), as a function of P. Here too, we observe that GSF converges much faster than PF; the latter requires about P = 24 beads while the former needs only P = 8 beads. Likewise, we see that the rate of convergence is consistent with Equations (29) and (30). Both Figures 3 and 4 demonstrate that at low P, the statistical noise increases, which is a known byproduct of the re-weighting procedure [21]. At the same time, we notice that the error bars quickly diminish in size as P increases, such that the statistical inefficiencies associ- ated with re-weighting...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the fourth-order Generalized Suzuki-Chin Factorization can be applied to bosonic and fermionic Path Integral Molecular Dynamics. By using a re-weighting technique, the method achieves significantly faster convergence of energy estimators with respect to the number of beads. This allows for more efficient simulations of quantum statistics in trapped particle systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This provides a highly efficient numerical tool for simulating the thermodynamic properties of ultracold quantum gases and many-body bosonic/fermionic systems without increasing the complexity of the underlying sampling algorithm.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need to accelerate the convergence of Path Integral Molecular Dynamics (PIMD) simulations for indistinguishable particles (bosons and fermions) as the number of imaginary-time beads increases.

**Main result.** The Generalized Suzuki-Chin Factorization (GSF) can be successfully integrated into bosonic PIMD, providing a 2-4x speedup in convergence with respect to the Trotter number with only modest computational cost.

**Method.** The authors implement the fourth-order GSF scheme as a re-weighting factor for observables, allowing the use of standard second-order primitive factorization for sampling.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on non-interacting indistinguishable particles (bosons and fermions) in harmonic traps and sinusoidal potentials, modeled via ring polymers in a discretized path integral framework.

**Key observables.** Kinetic energy, potential energy, total energy, radial distribution functions, and the fermionic sign/weight.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Trotter number (P), inverse temperature (beta), tuning parameter (alpha), and particle number (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The re-weighting efficiency may decrease at very low Trotter numbers due to lack of overlap between distributions; the analytical derivations are specific to harmonic potentials.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the number of beads required for convergence, the speedup of GSF over PF as a function of temperature, and the stability of the fermionic sign under GSF re-weighting.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the GSF implementation for bosons, demonstrates its efficiency through convergence tests in harmonic and sinusoidal potentials, provides analytical derivations for exact partition functions using polynomial theory, and evaluates its performance for fermionic systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Modern implementations of path integral molecular dynamics (PIMD) simulations of distinguishable particles frequently make use of high order factorization schemes for the Boltzmann operator to expedite convergence of equilibrium averages. Among these methods is the generalized Suzuki-Chin factorization (GSF), which is accurate up to fourth order in the imaginary-time step. In this work, we show that the GSF decomposition of the Boltzmann operator is applicable to bosonic PIMD, and results in an improved convergence of estimators. In particular, we show that the recently developed quadratic scaling bosonic PIMD need not change when using the GSF. The GSF scheme is implemented as a re-weighting factor for observables, without affecting the sampling generated by the standard, second-order, primitive factorization. We study the effect of this factorization for bosons in a harmonic trap and a sinusoidal potential. We also assess the effectiveness of GSF in calculating fermionic expectation values for harmonically-trapped atoms. In all these cases, we find that the GSF speeds up convergence with the Trotter number by a factor of $\sim 2-4$ across a wide temperature range, at only a modest computational cost.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14523"></a>
### [HQTN-SER: Speech Emotion Recognition with Hybrid Quantum Tensor Networks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14523v1)

**Authors:** Mahad Mohtashim, Nouhaila Innan, Muhammad Shafique  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14523v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14523_figures/2605.14523_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1: Overview of the HQTN-SER workflow for speech emotion recognition. Raw audio (.wav) from SAVEE, RAVDESS, and MDER is converted into 128-dimensional MFCC features (Librosa) and reduced to 32 dimensions using PCA. The resulting feature vector is mapped into a shared representation and processed in parallel by a classical encoder and an MPS-structured variational quantum circuit; their outputs are concatenated and fed to a final classifier to produce the emotion prediction.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14523_figures/2605.14523_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: MPS-structured variational circuit used in HQTN-SER. Trainable single-qubit rotations (Ry, Rz) are interleaved with nearest-neighbor CNOT gates in a left-to-right sweep. Measurement statistics are obtained as expectation values on each qubit and used as quantum features.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a hybrid quantum-classical framework for speech emotion recognition that uses a Matrix Product State (MPS) inspired quantum circuit. By using a structured tensor network approach, the model efficiently captures correlations in acoustic features using very few qubits and remains robust to simulated hardware noise. It provides a scalable, hardware-aware design for applying quantum machine learning to complex signal processing tasks.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) is fragile in real-world conditions due to subtle cues and high parameter requirements of classical models, while existing quantum machine learning approaches lack the architectural inductive bias needed to model correlated acoustic features.

**Main result.** The HQTN-SER framework achieves high accuracy (up to 80.12% on RAVDESS) and demonstrates significant robustness to hardware noise, showing that MPS-inspired quantum circuits can effectively model speech correlations with very low qubit counts.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical pipeline using PCA for dimensionality reduction, an MPS-inspired variational quantum circuit for feature extraction, and a fusion strategy that combines quantum measurement statistics with classical latent embeddings.

**Model / system.** A hybrid quantum-classical model utilizing a 1D chain of 3 to 4 qubits with an MPS-style architecture featuring angle encoding (Ry rotations), parameterized Ry and Rz gates, and nearest-neighbor CNOT entanglement.

**Key observables.** Expectation values of single-qubit Z operators.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Small qubit counts (n=3, 4), shallow circuit depth, and 32-dimensional PCA-compressed input features.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the structured connectivity of a tensor network can act as a sufficient inductive bias for speech features and does not claim unconditional quantum advantage.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the HQTN-SER workflow; Figure 2 details the MPS-structured variational circuit; Figures 3-5 display training/validation loss and accuracy curves across datasets.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of SER fragility, proposes the HQTN-SER framework, details the MPS-inspired architecture and fusion strategy, presents experimental results on three datasets, performs ablation and hardware-noise studies, and concludes with future directions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Speech emotion recognition (SER) remains fragile in real-world conditions because emotional cues are subtle, speaker-dependent, and easily confounded by recording variability, while high-performing deep models typically rely on large and carefully curated training sets. Quantum machine learning offers an alternative way to introduce nonlinear correlation modeling with compact modules, yet existing quantum SER studies remain limited and the impact of circuit structure is not well understood. This paper presents HQTN-SER, a hybrid quantum-classical framework that investigates how quantum tensor network connectivity can support SER under small-qubit settings. HQTN-SER introduces (i) an MPS-inspired quantum tensor network module that enforces structured interactions to model correlations in speech representations with a small number of trainable parameters, and (ii) a fusion strategy that combines quantum measurement features with a learned classical latent embedding for end-to-end emotion classification. We evaluate HQTN-SER on three public benchmarks (RAVDESS, SAVEE, and MDER) under a unified preprocessing and training protocol. The proposed model achieves consistent performance across datasets, RAVDESS = 80.12%, SAVEE = 78.26% and MDER = 73.51% accuracy, with stable convergence and low qubit counts, showing that tensor network structure can be an effective and hardware-aware design choice for quantum-assisted SER. The results provide a reproducible baseline and clarify when structured quantum modules can add value to affective computing today.

</details>

### [Interference of dynamical arrest, thermodynamic instabilities and energy-scale competition in symmetric binary mixtures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14064v1)

**Authors:** Ricardo Peredo-Ortiz, Edilio Lázaro-Lázaro, Magdaleno Medina-Noyola, Luis Fernando Elizondo-Aguilera  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14064v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

(a) Kinetic Suppression (α = 0.8): Strong cross-attraction causes the arrest line Tc (blue) to de- tach from and remain strictly above the demixing instability (green). The system vitriﬁes into a homogeneous glass, kinet- ically masking the demixing region. (b) Emergent Demix- ing (α = 0.5): Weaker cross-attraction allows the demixing line to pierce the arrest line, creating a window for gelation.</figcaption>
</figure>

(a) In the condensation regime, the low-k peak is driven by density ﬂuctuations Sρρ (red). (b) In the demixing regime, the peak is driven by con- centration ﬂuctuations Scc (green). Note that the standard partial structure factors Sii (black lines) appear qualitatively identical, illustrating structural blindness.</figcaption>

(a) Thermodynamic Prediction: The 3D insta- bility surface colored by the equilibrium instability angle |θ| (Dark = Condensation, Light = Demixing). The blue wire- frame indicates the dynamic arrest surface Tc. Thermody- namics predicts the driving force only at the instability limit. (b) Non-Equilibrium Reality: A cross-section of the Ki- netic Atlas at c = 0.5 for α = 0.5, classiﬁed by the structural order parameter χ. Here, the energy scale α is tuned to allow demixing. The metric χ acts as the non-equilibrium extension of θ, classifying the state deep into the arrested region. The color gradient visualizes the continuous...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14064_figures/2605.14064_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 3(b) maps a slice of the Kinetic Atlas for the equimolar concentration and the cross-interaction pa- rameter α = 0.5 [same as Fig. 1(b)]. The smooth gra- dient from the darker region χ →1 (condensation) to</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a unified non-equilibrium description of symmetric binary mixtures by exploring how the competition between different energy scales dictates the interplay between phase separation and glass formation. It introduces a new structural metric to distinguish between gels formed by density fluctuations versus those formed by concentration fluctuations. This work is significant for understanding the complex phase behavior of soft matter systems like colloids and proteins.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the inadequacy of equilibrium phase diagrams to describe soft-matter mixtures where kinetic barriers and dynamical arrest prevent equilibrium, specifically investigating the competition between thermodynamic instabilities and kinetic arrest.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that energy-scale competition (alpha) partitions the phase diagram into distinct kinetic regimes, and introduce a structural order parameter (chi) to resolve 'structural blindness' in distinguishing condensation-driven from demixing-driven arrested states.

**Method.** The study utilizes the Non-Equilibrium Self-Consistent Generalized Langevin Equation (NESCGLE) theory and a number-concentration formalism to analyze structural relaxation and stability.

**Model / system.** Symmetric binary mixtures (SBM) of particles with equal diameters using a Hard-Sphere plus Square-Well (HSSW) interaction potential.

**Key observables.** Partial structure factors (Sii), density fluctuations (Srho-rho), concentration fluctuations (Scc), localization length (gamma), and the structural order parameter (chi).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Energy-scale ratio (alpha) of cross-species to self-species attraction, density (rho), temperature (T), and concentration (c).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The direct correlation functions are approximated using the Ornstein-Zernike Equation with a closure relation, and the attractive tail is treated as a perturbative term.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the classification of kinetic regimes (Type I-IV) based on alpha, the resolution of structural blindness using density/concentration fluctuations, and the evolution of instability surfaces (spinodal and lambda-lines).

**Paper structure.** The paper moves from defining the problem of incomplete phase diagrams to establishing a theoretical framework (NESCGLE), classifying topological regimes via energy-scale competition, and finally introducing a structural metric to distinguish between different types of arrested states.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The equilibrium behavior of binary mixtures can be understood through the competition of energy scales, which classifies their corresponding phase diagrams into distinct topological regimes (Types I-IV). However, in many soft-matter mixtures, strong competing interactions and kinetic barriers often promote dynamical arrest, disrupting the formation of equilibrium and metastable states, and thus rendering conventional phase diagrams incomplete. Here we extend the description and classification of binary systems inside regions of thermodynamical instability. Specifically, we discuss how the interplay between two kind of instabilities and kinetic arrest generates a variety of amorphous states driven by different underlying mechanisms. For strong cross-attraction, for example, dynamical arrest suppresses demixing, whereas in competitive regimes, a mixture may display either condensation-driven or demixing-induced arrested states. The crossover between these regimes can be described by a structural order parameter $χ$, providing a unified non-equilibrium description that reconciles theoretical predictions with experimentally observed arrested states.

</details>

**Authors:** Rui Wen, Kansei Inamura, Sakura Schafer-Nameki  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15194v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper establishes that any weakly integral fusion category can be realized as a symmetry on a 1D tensor-product Hilbert space when allowing for Quantum Cellular Automata refinements. It provides a systematic way to calculate the indices of these symmetries and demonstrates the construction using anyon chains.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a fundamental link between abstract algebraic structures (fusion categories) and concrete lattice models, which is essential for understanding the classification of non-invertible symmetries in many-body dynamics and topological phases.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the realizability of (1+1)-dimensional fusion category symmetries on tensor-product Hilbert spaces, specifically addressing how Quantum Cellular Automata (QCAs) refine these realizations.

**Main result.** The authors prove that any weakly integral fusion category admits a QCA-refined realization on a tensor-product Hilbert space and show that the indices of the symmetry operators and QCAs are uniquely determined by the categorical data.

**Method.** The study uses fusion category theory, diagrammatic calculus, and the construction of anyon chain models to analyze the relationship between non-invertible symmetries and GNVW indices.

**Model / system.** The paper focuses on (1+1)-dimensional quantum many-body systems, specifically 1D spin chains and anyon chains with tensor-product Hilbert spaces and finite-dimensional on-site degrees of freedom.

**Key observables.** Quantum dimensions, GNVW indices, left and right defect dimensions, and the index of Quantum Cellular Automata.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Weak integrality of the fusion category, grading group E, and the lattice quantum dimension.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The proof relies on five physical assumptions regarding the locality of defects, the behavior of symmetry compositions, and the homogeneity of the index.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the rearrangement of local Hilbert spaces in anyon chains, the structure of the Ising category sector, and the sequential circuits used to map different Hilbert space sectors.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by investigating constraints on QCA-refined realizations, moves to a general construction of lattice models for weakly integral categories, and concludes with specific applications to Tambara-Yamagami and Metaplectic categories.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate realizations of (1+1)-dimensional fusion category symmetries on tensor-product Hilbert spaces, allowing for mixing with quantum cellular automata (QCAs). It was argued recently that any such realizable symmetry must be weakly integral. We develop a systematic analysis of QCA-refined realizations of fusion categories and prove two statements. First, we show that, under certain physical assumptions on defects, any QCA-refined realization has QCA and symmetry-operator indices determined by the categorical data, up to the freedom of redefining the symmetry operators. Second, we construct a lattice model that provides a QCA-refined realization for any weakly integral fusion category symmetry on a tensor product Hilbert space. We also compute indices of the QCAs in our lattice model and show agreement with the first result. As an application of the general construction, we give an explicit QCA-refined realization of general Tambara-Yamagami categorical symmetries.

</details>

### [Observation of Switchable Chiral Magnons in an Altermagnet](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14124v1)

**Summary.** This paper provides the first direct experimental observation of switchable chiral magnons in the altermagnet MnTe. By using polarized neutron scattering, the authors show that the handedness of spin waves can be controlled by magnetic field cooling, providing a foundation for future altermagnetic spintronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The researchers aimed to experimentally verify the existence of chiral magnons in altermagnets, which are a predicted but previously unobserved hallmark of the altermagnetic state arising from non-relativistic exchange.

**Main result.** The study directly observed chiral magnons in the altermagnet MnTe and demonstrated that their chirality can be reversibly switched using a magnetic-field cooling process.

**Method.** The team used polarized inelastic neutron scattering (PINS) on the HYSPEC and IN20 spectrometers, combined with polarized neutron diffraction (PND) to control and measure domain imbalance.

**Model / system.** The physical system is MnTe, a prototypical altermagnet with a collinear magnetic structure and $g$-wave spin symmetry, featuring two opposite-spin sublattices.

**Key observables.** Chiral magnon signal (Mch), symmetric structure factor (S(Q, E)), magnon band splitting, and the Néel vector component (n_x).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic-field cooling (FC) through the Neel temperature (TN = 307 K) using fields of approximately +/- 4.2 mT.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The observed chirality is attributed to an imbalanced multi-domain state rather than a weak ferromagnetic moment.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the crystal and magnetic structure; Fig 2 displays 1D cuts of the neutron structure factor showing split magnon peaks and opposite chirality; Fig 3 details the experimental setup and the sign reversal of the chiral signal after field cooling.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the concept of altermagnetic chiral magnons, describes the experimental setup and MnTe crystal properties, presents the observation of magnon splitting and chirality via neutron scattering, demonstrates the switchability of chirality via field cooling, and concludes by ruling out alternative ferromagnetic origins.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Chiral magnons, the quanta of handed spin waves, transport spin angular momentum without energy loss due to Joule heating. The recently discovered altermagnets were proposed to host chiral magnons arising from a non-relativistic exchange mechanism, similar to that in ferromagnets but without net magnetization, offering a stray-field-free platform for efficient magnon spin-current manipulation. In this work, we directly observed chiral magnons in the altermagnetic prototype MnTe using polarized inelastic neutron scattering. Furthermore, the magnon chirality was found to be reversibly switched by magnetic-field control, establishing a robust foundation for functional altermagnetic magnonics.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14881"></a>
### [QSeqSim: A Symbolic Simulator for Qiskit While Loops Using Sequential Quantum Circuits](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14881v1)

**Authors:** Zihao Li, Ji Guan, Mingsheng Ying  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14881v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14881_figures/2605.14881_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1: Classical–quantum analogy from programs to IRs to circuits.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14881_figures/2605.14881_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: QSeqSim workflow.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14881_figures/2605.14881_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3: Circuit-level view of one SQC iteration (composition, dynamic body with measurement on the external register, retention).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** QSeqSim is a new symbolic simulator for Qiskit that enables the execution of quantum programs containing while-loops. By using BDDs and weighted model counting, it can efficiently simulate complex iterative protocols like quantum random walks with thousands of qubits.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Current Qiskit-native tools lack the ability to simulate quantum programs containing 'while-loop' constructs and mid-circuit measurements with feedback.

**Main result.** The development of QSeqSim, a symbolic simulator that successfully scales to large-scale sequential circuits, including quantum random walks with over 1000 qubits.

**Method.** The tool uses a Binary Decision Diagram (BDD)-based symbolic representation and weighted model counting to execute sequential quantum circuits (SQCs) by modeling loops as processes with state composition and retention.

**Model / system.** The framework models Qiskit while-loops as Sequential Quantum Circuits (SQCs) composed of combinational, dynamic, and sequential components with explicit internal and external qubits.

**Key observables.** Quantum state reachability, measurement outcome reachability, and global path probability.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits, loop depth (iterations), gate density, and mid-circuit measurement count.

**Assumptions / limitations.** All unitaries must be decomposable into the Clifford+T gate set, and the external register is assumed to be prepared in a computational basis state.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares classical and quantum loop hierarchies; Figure 2 shows the QSeqSim architecture; Figure 3 illustrates a single SQC iteration; Figure 4 demonstrates an RUS protocol implementation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the simulation gap, defines the SQC mathematical model, details the QSeqSim architecture and BDD-based kernel, provides formal proofs for the soundness of state composition and retention, and concludes with scalability benchmarks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a tool QSeqSim, a Qiskit-integrated symbolic backend that fills the current gap of having no Qiskit-native support for simulating while-loop quantum programs and their induced sequential quantum circuits. QSeqSim takes Qiskit QuantumCircuit objects, translates them into OpenQASM 3 code, and organises the resulting program into a combination of combinational, dynamic, and sequential circuits, thereby assigning while-loops a precise sequential circuit semantics with explicit internal and external qubits. Building on this semantics, QSeqSim adopts a Binary Decision Diagram (BDD)-based symbolic representation and integrates weighted model counting to compute measurement probabilities efficiently by exploiting sharing in structured and sparse BDDs. On top of this Boolean backbone, it introduces dedicated symbolic operators for state composition and state retention, thereby enabling efficient symbolic execution of sequential quantum circuits. Our experiments demonstrate that QSeqSim scales to substantial while-induced sequential circuits; in particular, in the quantum random walk benchmark we successfully simulate circuits with over 1000 qubits for more than 10 loop iterations.   QSeqSim is available at https://github.com/Veri-Q/QSeqSim.

</details>

### [Quantum Advantage in Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14235v1)

**Authors:** Simranjeet Singh Dahia, Claudia Szabo  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14235v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper explores the use of quantum entanglement to enable coordination between independent agents in reinforcement learning. It proves that entanglement allows agents to break classical performance bounds in specific games, while also identifying that quantum circuit expressiveness can drive advantages in other navigation tasks.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether quantum entanglement can serve as a coordination mechanism in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) and distinguishing genuine quantum advantage from mere increases in model capacity.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that entangled agents can exceed classical performance limits in the CHSH game by approaching the Tsirelson limit, while in navigation tasks, the advantage stems from VQC expressiveness and hybrid architectures rather than entanglement.

**Method.** The authors use a Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) framework, comparing classical MAA2C, unentangled QMARL, and entangled QMARL using Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) across various benchmarks.

**Model / system.** The model utilizes Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) as policy networks for agents, employing Bell and GHZ states for entanglement, and is tested in the CHSH game, CoinGame, and Cooperative Navigation (CoopNav) environments.

**Key observables.** Win rates in CHSH, expected total discounted reward, success rate, collision count, and episode length.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Tsirelson limit (0.854), classical CHSH ceiling (0.75), qubit count (n_q), circuit depth (d), and entropy regularization coefficient (beta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Agents are cooperative and operate under strictly decentralized execution without runtime communication; simulations are conducted in a noiseless statevector environment.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the QMARL CTDE framework; Figure 3 compares performance in CoinGame; Figure 4 shows CoopNav success rates and collisions; Figure 7 illustrates win rates approaching the Tsirelson bound; Figure 8 shows qubit/depth ablation studies.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QMARL framework, defines the quantum and classical architectures, details the experimental environments (CHSH, CoinGame, CoopNav), presents results for entanglement-based coordination and VQC expressiveness, performs ablation studies on circuit parameters, and concludes with a parameter-parity analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present an empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement in agent coordination within quantum multi agent reinforcement learning (QMARL). While QMARL has attracted growing interest recently, most prior work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. We address this directly by evaluating a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors with shared entangled states. In the CHSH game, which has a mathematically proven classical performance ceiling of 0.75 win rate, we show that entangled QMARL agents approach the Tsirelson limit of 0.854, providing clear evidence of their quantum advantage. We show that unentangled quantum circuits match the classical baseline, confirming that entanglement and not the quantum circuit itself is the active coordination mechanism. We also explore the effect of specific entanglement structures, as some Bell states enable coordination gains while others actively harm performance. On cooperative navigation (CoopNav), QMARL without entanglement achieves $\sim2\times$ improvement in success rate over classical MAA2C ($\sim$0.85 versus $\sim$0.40), with the hybrid configuration, quantum actor paired with a classical centralised critic, outperforming both fully classical and fully quantum solutions. We present our experimental analysis and discuss future work.

</details>

### [Spontaneous symmetry breaking and Goldstone modes for deep information propagation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14685v1)

**Authors:** Nabil Iqbal, T. Anderson Keller, Yue Song, Takeru Miyato, Max Welling  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14685v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14685_figures/2605.14685_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Equilibrium configurations are minima of the potential energy V (ϕ). If r &gt; 0 we have a single isolated minimum at ϕ = 0 and the symmetry is unbroken. If r &lt; 0 we find a ring of minima with constant |ϕ|, of which the system must choose one; the symmetry is spontaneously broken.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14685_figures/2605.14685_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Comparison of mean-field result for c⋆</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14685_figures/2605.14685_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Test accuracy after 5 epochs on Fashion-MNIST for U(1) (left) and O(4) equivariant (right) models with 64 features and 100 layers. We compare against models with the same nonlinearity but generic linear layers, thus isolating the effect of the equivariance. We vary σw to probe the symmetry breaking phase transition, normalizing it so that the transition is always at σw = 1. The training of the equivariant models appears to begin precisely when we enter the SSB phase, as measured by the order parameter c⋆, which we compute at initialization from (6) averaged over the last 30 layers. Training of the non-equivariant models is poor, as expected for deep networks with no mitigation.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14685_figures/2605.14685_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 5: Effective rank dynamics during training (left) and across network depth (right).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14685_figures/2605.14685_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 4: Demonstration of how performance on Fashion-MNIST degrades with increasing layer number for a generic network and for different sorts of equivari- ance. Note the O(4) model does not degrade at all.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes using the physics of spontaneous symmetry breaking as a mechanism for information propagation in neural networks. By using equivariant architectures, the authors show that 'Goldstone-like' modes protect signal integrity across many layers or time steps, effectively replacing the need for architectural stabilizers like skip connections.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how spontaneous symmetry breaking and the resulting Goldstone modes can enable stable, long-distance information propagation and prevent signal collapse in deep and recurrent neural networks.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that in the symmetry-broken phase, equivariant networks possess a protected component of the Jacobian that remains O(1) regardless of depth, enabling improved trainability and long-term memory without traditional stabilizers like residual connections.

**Method.** The study uses mean-field theory, stochastic path integrals, and the replica method in the large-N limit to analytically derive activation dynamics, complemented by empirical testing on tasks like Fashion-MNIST and variable-delay copy tasks.

**Model / system.** The models consist of deep feedforward and recurrent neural networks (RNNs, GRUs) with internal layers that are equivariant under continuous symmetry groups such as U(1) or O(k).

**Key observables.** Order parameter (c_l), Jacobian magnitude and its protected component, effective rank of representations, and vorticity/winding numbers in 2D convolutional models.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Weight variance (sigma_W), symmetry dimension (k), network depth (L), and recurrent delay length (T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the large-N limit for mean-field derivations and that the tasks being performed do not necessarily possess the underlying symmetry.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the potential energy landscape of symmetry breaking, the phase transition in activation variance, the stability of the Jacobian component in the SSB phase, and the emergence of topological vortices in 2D convolutional RNNs.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical analogy of Goldstone modes, provides an analytical mean-field derivation of the symmetry-breaking phase transition, presents empirical evidence for improved information propagation in deep and recurrent architectures, and concludes with observations of topological defects.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In physical systems, whenever a continuous symmetry is spontaneously broken, the system possesses excitations called Goldstone modes, which allow coherent information propagation over long distances and times. In this work, we study deep neural networks whose internal layers are equivariant under a continuous symmetry and may therefore support analogous Goldstone-like degrees of freedom. We demonstrate, both analytically and empirically, that these degrees of freedom enable coherent signal propagation across depth and recurrent iterations, providing a mechanism for stable information flow without relying on architectural stabilizers such as residual connections or normalization. In feedforward networks, this results in improved trainability and representational diversity across layers. In recurrent settings, we demonstrate the same mechanism is valuable for long-term memory by propagating information over recurrent iterations, thereby improving performance of RNNs and GRUs on long-sequence modeling tasks.

</details>

### [Worst-Case Sample Complexity Bounds for Distributed Inner Product Estimation with Local Randomized Measurements](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14256v1)

**Authors:** Zhenyuan Huang, Kun Wang, Ping Xu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14256v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **2/5**

For local Clifford sampling, the data match the theoretical picture from Appx. C: pure stabilizer states sit at the top boundary, and in particular the product state |+⟩⊗n realizes the worst-case growth in the displayed coefficients. The Bell-dimer and GHZ families stay below this envelope, while Haar-random and W states are further suppressed. For local Haar sampling, the decomposition reveals the microscopic origin of the competition effect emphasized in Sec. V. The Bell-dimer family can beat the pure product benchmark at the level of Bn,H, as shown in the main text. However,...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 2: Resolved state-dependent moment coefficients for the benchmark state families. The top row shows local Clifford sampling and the bottom row shows local Haar sampling. The first two columns show An and Cn, while the third column shows AnBn,E. The data confirm that local Clifford worst-case behavior is governed by pure stabilizer states, whereas local Haar sampling exhibits a genuine competition: Bell-dimer states enhance Bn,H but are penalized in An.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14256_figures/2605.14256_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 3: Variation with purity loss induced by local depolarization. Each curve corresponds to a fixed system size n, starting from the deterministic product state |+⟩⊗n and varying the local depolarizing parameter p. The states are not randomly generated; randomness enters only through the sampling of local measurement settings when Monte Carlo estimation is used. Both ensembles show decreasing fourth-moment contributions as the state moves away from the pure-state boundary.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14256_figures/2605.14256_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 4: Variation with entanglement structure within the chain graph-state family |ϕn,m⟩. Each curve corresponds to a fixed system size n, and the horizontal axis gives the number m of nearest-neighbor CZ edges. Local Clifford sampling is largely insensitive to this deformation within the stabilizer manifold, whereas local Haar sampling reacts strongly to the amount of entanglement across qubits.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** This paper provides new rigorous upper bounds on the number of measurements needed to estimate the overlap of two remote quantum states. It identifies shared randomness as a critical resource and shows that while entanglement can increase certain variance terms, the overall complexity is dominated by the scaling of the measurement ensemble.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Establishing rigorous worst-case sample complexity bounds for Distributed Inner Product Estimation (DIPE) using local randomized measurements.

**Main result.** The paper proves a worst-case sample complexity of O(sqrt(4.5^n)) for Clifford sampling and conjectures a sharper scaling of O(sqrt(3.6^n)) for Haar sampling, while showing that independent Pauli shadows scale worse at O(sqrt(7.5^n)).

**Method.** The authors use variance decomposition, symmetry reduction to Hamming-distance kernels, and mathematical tools like Clifford commutants and local twirling identities to bound the estimator's variance.

**Model / system.** A distributed quantum system where two remote parties, Alice and Bob, possess unknown n-qubit states and perform local randomized measurements (Clifford, Haar, or Pauli shadows) using shared or independent randomness.

**Key observables.** The Hilbert-Schmidt inner product Tr[rho sigma].

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits (n), number of unitary samples (N_U), number of shots per sample (N_M), and error/confidence parameters (epsilon, delta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the single-qubit Clifford group acts as an exact unitary 3-design and that the kernel optimization is covariant under qubit permutations and local bit-flips.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the rigidity of the Clifford ensemble versus the entanglement sensitivity of the Haar ensemble; Figure 2 and 3 illustrate how moment coefficients scale with entanglement and purity deformation.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with kernel optimization and uniqueness proofs, followed by variance decomposition and the derivation of Clifford and Haar complexity bounds, then explores the impact of entanglement and purity on these bounds, and concludes with numerical verification of conjectures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study distributed inner product estimation for $n$-qubit states using local randomized measurements, for which rigorous worst-case guarantees are less understood. We first reduce the minimax kernel optimization to Hamming-distance kernels. Within this class, unbiasedness fixes a unique kernel. For this kernel under local Clifford sampling, we prove a sharp fourth-moment bound using the single-qubit Clifford commutant. This yields worst-case sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{4.5^n})$, attained by identical pure product stabilizer states. For the same kernel under local Haar sampling, we prove a local twirling identity that compares its fourth moment with the Clifford fourth moment. This gives the same rigorous upper bound as in the Clifford case, but the comparison is lossy. This motivates the conjectured sharper Haar scaling $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{3.6^n})$ attained by product states, and verify it for several important classes of states. We also show that independent single-qubit Pauli shadows have worst-case scaling $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{7.5^n})$ for large $n$.

</details>

### [A microcanonical approach to criticality in the mean-field $φ^4$ model: evidence of intrinsic microcanonical structure before the thermodynamic limit](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14198v1)

**Authors:** Loris Di Cairano, Roberto Franzosi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14198v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2605.14198_figures/2605.14198_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Microcanonical entropy derivatives for the mean-field φ4 model. (a) Inverse temperature βN(ε) reconstructed from microcanonical simulations for several system sizes. (b) Entropy curvature γN(ε) = ∂2ε sN(ε). While βN(ε) varies smoothly across the critical region, the curvature γN(ε) develops a localized extremal structure that becomes progressively sharper with increasing N. This structure reflects a local restructuring of the entropy landscape associated with the onset of critical behavior. The position of this extremal feature defines the finite-size critical marker ε⋆(N) through microcanonical inflection-point analysis (MIPA).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14198_figures/2605.14198_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2 panel (a.1) shows the resulting values of ε⋆(N) ob- tained from the curvature signal γN(ε) for all simulated system sizes, while panel 2(a.2) represents the value of the maxima at the critical energy value, namely, M(N) := γN(ε⋆(N)). Rather than appearing as isolated pseudocritical estimates, these val- ues form a trajectory in the (N−1/2,ε) and (N−1/2,maxε γN) planes, respectively. This trajectory represents the finite- size evolution of the entropy-derivative structure identified in Sec. V. Each point corresponds to the energy at which the microcanonical entropy curvature signals the collective reor- ganization of the phase-space measure for that system size.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14198_figures/2605.14198_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 2. Finite-size critical trajectory extracted from the entropy cur- vature. (a) Energy values ε⋆(N) identified from the extremal struc- ture of γN(ε) for all simulated system sizes. (b) Convergence of ε⋆(N) toward the thermodynamic critical energy εc as a function of N−1/2. The dashed line indicates the exact critical energy. The data reveal a smooth and systematic finite-size flow toward εc, showing that the thermodynamic critical point emerges as the limit of a well- defined family of finite-size entropy structures.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14198_figures/2605.14198_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 3. Canonical finite-size signatures of the phase transition in the mean-field φ4 model.(a) Caloric curve ε(T) for several system sizes. (b) Specific heat CV (T) obtained from canonical simulations. The vertical dashed line indicates the exact critical temperature of the thermody- namic limit. As expected for a continuous transition, the specific heat develops a size-dependent peak that sharpens and shifts with increasing N. At finite system size the thermodynamic singularity is replaced by smooth anomalies whose position defines a pseudocritical temperature. These standard observables clearly signal the approach to criticality, yet they do so through broadened and size-dependent...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes that criticality is an intrinsic structural property of finite-size systems rather than just an asymptotic phenomenon. By analyzing the morphology of microcanonical entropy derivatives, the authors identify a predictable 'critical trajectory' that converges to the thermodynamic limit. This provides a new way to identify and characterize phase transitions in finite-system platforms.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether phase transitions and criticality can be defined as intrinsic structural properties of finite-size systems rather than just non-analyticities that emerge only in the thermodynamic limit.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that the microcanonical entropy landscape contains well-defined structural markers (inflection points and extrema) at finite N that form a 'critical trajectory' converging to the exact thermodynamic critical point.

**Method.** The study uses Microcanonical Inflection-Point Analysis (MIPA) applied to microcanonical simulations of the mean-field phi^4 model, comparing results against analytical thermodynamic-limit solutions and canonical observables.

**Model / system.** A 1D mean-field phi^4 model, which is a paradigmatic system for continuous phase transitions, characterized by a Hamiltonian with a potential term and a long-range coupling strength J.

**Key observables.** Microcanonical entropy derivatives (inverse temperature beta_N and entropy curvature gamma_N), magnetization, specific heat, and the critical energy marker epsilon_star(N).

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size N, energy density epsilon, temperature T, and the thermodynamic limit N approaching infinity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the structural reorganization of the phase-space measure is the fundamental physical driver of phase transitions, independent of the infinite-size limit.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the evolution of entropy derivatives (beta and gamma) and their convergence toward the thermodynamic limit; Figure 2 displays the scaling of the critical trajectory epsilon_star(N) against N^(-1/2); Figure 3 shows canonical signatures like the caloric curve and specific heat peaks.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of finite-size criticality, presents the MIPA framework, applies it to the mean-field phi^4 model as a benchmark, validates the results against analytical solutions, and demonstrates the convergence of the critical trajectory and its ability to organize other thermodynamic observables.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Collective critical behavior is often identified with thermodynamic nonanalyticities and divergences emerging only in the infinite-size limit. Here we adopt a complementary viewpoint: criticality is a structural property due to the rearrangement of the interactions among system's constituents that already exists at finite size and becomes singular only asymptotically. We show that the microcanonical entropy derivatives provide a natural finite-$N$ arena where such structure is encoded in intrinsic extremal/inflection morphologies, and that microcanonical inflection-point analysis (MIPA) turns these morphologies into a unique finite-size critical marker and a well-defined critical trajectory. Using the mean-field $φ^4$ model as a stringent benchmark, we reconstruct $β_N(\varepsilon)$ and $γ_N(\varepsilon)$ from microcanonical simulations, validate them against analytic results, and demonstrate that the MIPA trajectory converges to the exact thermodynamic critical point while simultaneously organizing the approach of other observables to their asymptotic behavior. Our results elevate finite-size criticality from a rounded remnant of the thermodynamic limit to a measurable and predictive object in its own right, with direct relevance to modern finite-system platforms and numerical studies.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [A Twist on Scattering from Defect Anomalies](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13961v1)

**Authors:** Andrea Antinucci, Christian Copetti, Giovanni Galati, Giovanni Rizi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13961v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

In both cases, the out–going state can be created by a non-genuine local operator attached to a topological line. The presence of a defect anomaly implies that some of the global charge is stored in the topological junction and localized on the defect.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13961_figures/2605.13961_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Upper figure: A symmetric defect D. A topological symmetry line Ua can intersect D topologically, but it cannot be absorbed by it. Lower figure: A symmetry-reflecting defect D. A topological symmetry line Ua can end topologically on D, and can also be absorbed by it.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13961_figures/2605.13961_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 4: (a) a state on the cylinder in the twisted Hilbert space Ha. (b) a twist field ϕa, which maps the untwisted to the twisted Hilbert space (time flows upwards).</figcaption>
</figure>
