<img src="2605.13961_figures/2605.13961_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 5: Going from the cylinder to the Strip Hilbert space by inserting a complete set of states. Left, the initial cylinder state in Ha, center, inserting a complete base of IR states in the large radius limit, right, decomposing into states on the Strip Hilbert space by fusing the topological line Ua.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13961_figures/2605.13961_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 6: Above: A topological line gL ⊂GL ending on a symmetry reflecting defect measures only the charge of the incoming radiation. Therefore, GL selection rules require ρL = 1 for this process to happen. Below: Because of the defect anomaly, a topological line gL ⊂GL ending on a symmetry reflecting defect can measure the charge of topological junctions of gR ⊂GR lines.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper explains how 'categorical scattering'—where particles transform into exotic twist operators upon hitting a defect—is driven by 't Hooft anomalies on the defect's worldvolume. By using algebraic tools like the Defect Strip algebra, the authors derive exact scattering solutions for several integrable 1D models and lattice spin chains. This provides a fundamental link between topological anomalies and the observable dynamics of particles interacting with impurities.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the mechanism behind 'categorical scattering,' where incoming particles scatter off extended defects into exotic outgoing states (twist operators) driven by localized 't Hooft anomalies.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that defect anomalies allow for the transmission of twist operators by creating charged topological junctions, permitting charge conservation even when selection rules appear violated. They provide explicit scattering amplitudes for new massive integrable theories and establish universality for lattice spin chains.

**Method.** The study utilizes a theoretical framework involving Tube and Strip algebras, the 'folding trick,' and the 'Defect Strip algebra.' Mathematical tools include the analysis of non-invertible symmetries (fusion categories), Ward identities, and exact S-matrix calculations using free-field realizations.

**Model / system.** The work focuses on 1+1-dimensional quantum field theories and lattice models, including massless chiral fermions, Ising field theory (with interfaces between ordered/disordered phases), Tricritical Ising CFT, and XYZ spin chains with defects.

**Key observables.** Scattering amplitudes (S-matrix entries), reflection and transmission coefficients, and the presence of poles in amplitudes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Rapidity of the incoming particle state, interface parameters, and deformation parameters (such as $\gamma$ and $\delta$ in the XX model).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is primarily restricted to 1+1-dimensional systems and assumes the S-matrix is well-defined and free from infrared divergences.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows particle scattering off boundaries/interfaces with twist operator creation; Figure 2 distinguishes between symmetric and symmetry-reflecting defects; Figure 3 illustrates defect anomalies via symmetry operator fusion; Figure 9 shows RG flow in the XX model.

**Paper structure.** The paper reviews symmetry and defect properties, develops the Defect Strip algebra, demonstrates the mechanism using massless models, applies it to new massive integrable models to derive S-matrices, and extends the findings to lattice-based impurities.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In the presence of extended defects, familiar incoming particles can scatter into exotic outgoing states created by twist operators. We show that one possible mechanism driving these "categorical scattering" processes is the presence of localized 't Hooft anomalies on the defect's worldvolume. Defect anomalies trap non-trivial charges at junctions between the symmetry lines and the interface, opening new transmission channels that would naively appear to violate selection rules. After outlining the general mechanism, we investigate several concrete examples with defects, interfaces, and boundaries. For models of massless chiral fermions already studied in the literature, we show that the emergence of twist operators can be understood as a consequence of defect anomalies. We then introduce new massive integrable theories in which a similar phenomenon occurs, and we explicitly solve the associated scattering problem, obtaining new integrable solutions. Finally, we construct lattice spin chains with defects where similar physics is expected to arise.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.15098"></a>
### [Accelerating State-Vector Quantum Simulation on Integrated GPUs via Cache Locality Optimization: A Cross-Architecture Evaluation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15098v1)

**Authors:** Gabriel Fernandes Thomaz, Jerusa Marchi, Eduarda Rodrigues Monteiro, Fernando Augusto Caletti de Barros, Evandro Chagas Ribeiro da Rosa  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15098v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The top row (a–d) shows the baseline, while the bottom row (e–h) displays the execution times using the optimal state partitioning. The y-axis represents the wall-clock time in seconds on a logarithmic scale, and the x-axis indicates the number of simulated qubits. Executions range from 4 to 28 qubits, except for the AMD system (c, g), which is limited to a maximum of 27 qubits. In the optimized plots (e–h), the numbers annotated below the data points indicate the optimal block size yielding the shortest execution time.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15098_figures/2605.15098_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5. Performance analysis of a 27-qubit QPE simulation on CPU and integrated GPU. The top row (a–d) displays the CPU execution, while the bottom row (e–h) shows the GPU execution. The plots illustrate execution time (left y-axis), cache miss percentage (red bars, right y-axis), and SWAP count (green bars, right y-axis) for block sizes ranging from 16 to 27. Cache miss data is not available GPU executions and Apple M1 Pro CPU execution (d).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a way to make quantum circuit simulation much faster on standard consumer laptops by optimizing how data is stored in the GPU cache. By reorganizing the quantum state into blocks that fit into the processor's cache, the authors prevent the massive slowdowns typically seen when simulating larger numbers of qubits. This makes high-performance quantum simulation more accessible to researchers without expensive data-center hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** State-vector quantum simulation on integrated GPUs (iGPUs) suffers from severe performance degradation at larger qubit scales due to memory bandwidth bottlenecks and poor spatial cache locality.

**Main result.** A state-partitioning optimization strategy successfully mitigates performance drops, significantly improving GPU speedup over CPU on architectures like Intel Core i5 (from 0.95x to 1.89x) and Apple M1 Pro (from 3.71x to 5.88x).

**Method.** The authors propose a vendor-agnostic approach using state-vector partitioning into blocks to maximize last-level cache (LLC) locality, implemented via the CubeCL framework and wgpu backend.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the classical simulation of quantum algorithms using the state-vector method, specifically benchmarking the Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) algorithm.

**Key observables.** Wall-clock execution time, GPU speedup relative to CPU, cache miss rate (as a proxy for GPU performance), and the number of global-local qubit SWAP operations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit scale (up to 28 qubits), block size (optimized to fit within L3/SLC cache capacities), and hardware architectures (Intel, AMD, and Apple).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The optimization is specifically tailored for integrated GPUs with shared memory architectures and may not directly translate to discrete GPUs.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows memory access patterns; Figure 2 illustrates state partitioning into blocks; Figure 3 shows the QPE circuit; Figure 4 compares execution times across architectures; Figure 5 shows the relationship between block size, cache misses, and SWAP counts; Figure 6 compares baseline vs. optimized GPU speedup.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the memory bottleneck problem, describes the state-partitioning optimization and implementation details, presents a cross-architecture experimental evaluation, and concludes with a discussion on hardware-specific findings and future work.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The classical simulation of quantum algorithms is a crucial tool for circuit development, testing, and validation. Although acceleration using GPUs significantly reduces simulation time, most high-performance simulators rely on vendor-specific frameworks that target data-center hardware. To broaden access to quantum simulation, this work proposes a vendor-agnostic approach targeting the integrated GPUs commonly found in consumer-grade laptops. A primary challenge in state-vector simulation is its inherently poor spatial locality, which creates a memory bandwidth bottleneck. Consequently, baseline implementations experience a severe degradation in relative GPU speedup as the number of simulated qubits increases. To address this limitation, we introduce a state partitioning optimization that reorganizes the quantum state vector to maximize the last-level cache locality and minimize costly main memory fetches. We evaluate this strategy using a Quantum Phase Estimation algorithm across diverse architectures from Intel, AMD, and Apple. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed optimization successfully mitigates performance degradation at larger qubit scales. In particular, for a 28-qubit simulation, the optimization reversed a performance deficit on an Intel Core i5, improving the GPU speedup over the CPU from 0.95x to 1.89x, and increased the Apple M1 Pro speedup from 3.71x to 5.88x. Overall, this approach yields consistent execution time improvements, demonstrating the viability of integrated GPUs for efficient quantum circuit simulation.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14637"></a>
### [Adaptive Window Decoding based on Spatiotemporal Complementary Gap](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14637v1)

**Authors:** Moeto Mishima, Riki Toshio, Kaito Kishi, Jun Fujisaki, Hirotaka Oshima, Shintaro Sato, Keisuke Fujii  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14637v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14637_figures/2605.14637_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic of the adaptive window decoding scheme. By default, decoding is performed with a small buffer size, while soft information g, which quantifies the decoder’s confi- dence, is computed simultaneously. If g is smaller than a pre- determined threshold gth (i.e., the decoding result is deemed unreliable), the buffer size is enlarged to d and decoding is re- done; otherwise, the result is committed as is. This adaptive switching reduces the average decoding time while preventing degradation of the logical error rate.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14637_figures/2605.14637_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 3. Schematic illustration of the boundary nodes used in the window. Separate boundary nodes are introduced for the two boundaries of the commit region (vcom,R and vcom,L), the two boundaries of the buffer region (vbuf,R and vbuf,L), and the virtual boundary (vvir). When performing decoding, the label ⊥is assigned to each of these five boundary nodes.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14637_figures/2605.14637_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 2. Schematic of sliding-window decoding. Each window consists of a commit region and a buffer region. After de- coding the window, the tentative error estimate in the buffer region is discarded, and only that in the commit region is finalized. Artificial defects are generated according to the fi- nalized error estimate in the commit region, and the window advances by rcom before the next decoding step.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14637_figures/2605.14637_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Examples of logical errors caused by a small buffer size. Red lines represent the actual error chains, and blue lines represent the estimated error chains. (a) Two defects in window i are incorrectly matched to the right commit boundary and the virtual boundary, leaving an artificial defect with no partner in window i + 1, which is then matched to the left commit boundary by MWPM. (b) Two defects that should have been matched to the boundaries are instead matched to each other in window i, leading to an unanticipated single defect in window i + 1, which is then matched to the left boundary through a pair of defects.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14637_figures/2605.14637_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Difficulties in computing the complementary error within a window. (a) Leaving the buffer boundary open only yields additional errors indicated by the red lines. (b) Clos- ing the buffer boundary for both the minimum-weight and complementary errors leads to a spuriously small gap in some cases. (c) Computing the minimum-weight error with the buffer boundary open and the complementary error with it closed also leads to a spuriously small gap in some cases.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes an adaptive window decoding method for surface codes that significantly reduces computational overhead. By using a new 'spatiotemporal complementary gap' to measure decoding confidence, the system can use small, fast buffers most of the time and only revert to larger, more expensive buffers when errors are likely, maintaining high accuracy with much lower average latency.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The bottleneck in real-time fault-tolerant quantum computing is the latency of decoding, where reducing window sizes in sliding-window decoding typically increases logical error rates due to insufficient buffer regions.

**Main result.** The proposed adaptive decoding scheme reduces the average buffer size by approximately 40% while maintaining the logical error rate by using a new confidence metric called the spatiotemporal complementary gap (STCG).

**Method.** The authors introduce an adaptive scheme that first attempts decoding with a small buffer and uses the STCG (and its variants like path-selected STCG) as soft information to decide whether to commit the result or re-decode with a larger buffer.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on surface codes and repetition codes under both phenomenological and circuit-level noise models, utilizing a 3D decoding graph structure.

**Key observables.** Logical error rate, decoding confidence (gap), average buffer size, and switching rate.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code distance (d), buffer size (r_buf), commit region (r_com), and physical error rate (p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the use of the Minimum-Weight Perfect Matching (MWPM) decoder and relies on an extrapolation procedure to predict performance in low-error-rate regimes.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the adaptive decoding workflow, the mechanics of sliding window decoding, the construction of the STCG and path-selected STCG, and numerical results showing the relationship between the gap, error rates, and buffer size efficiency.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the decoding latency problem, describes the sliding window framework, proposes the adaptive scheme and the new STCG metrics, provides numerical validation via Monte Carlo simulations, and demonstrates performance extrapolation to low error rates.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Real-time decoding plays a crucial role in practical fault-tolerant quantum computing. Window decoding, in which the decoding problem is divided into windows, is a promising approach. While reducing the window size is desirable for faster decoding, each window contains a buffer region whose size must typically be at least the code distance to avoid degrading the logical error rate, which limits how much the window can shrink. In this paper, we propose an adaptive decoding scheme in which window decoding is first performed with a small buffer size and a decoding confidence (soft information) is computed; if the confidence is low, the buffer size is enlarged and decoding is redone. This approach reduces the average decoding time, since most shots are decoded with a small buffer. A central challenge in realizing this scheme is that existing forms of soft information are not directly applicable to window decoding, especially with a small buffer. We address this challenge by introducing a new form of soft information, the spatiotemporal complementary gap, specifically designed for this setting. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed scheme reduces the average buffer size by approximately 40% while maintaining the logical error rate.

</details>

### [All-Electric Quantum State Transfer via Spin-Orbit Phase Matching](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13976v1)

**Authors:** Madhumita Sarkar, Roopayan Ghosh, Charles G. Smith, Maksym Myronov, Sougato Bose  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13976v1>

These results are for L = 4. In both cases, the fidelity is maximized when the spin-orbit axis aligns close to the z-direction.</figcaption>

For experimentally relevant fields (B = 50 MHz), the dynamics remain essentially identical to the zero-field case, while larger fields introduce visible deviations only at longer times. In both cases, however, the maximum fidelity remains unchanged, demonstrating that the axis-alignment protocol is robust against variations in magnetic- field strength. (c,d) Maximum fidelity within a fixed time window (t &lt; 10) as a function of θso for different orientations of nso, shown for (c) B = 50 MHz and (d) B = 1000 MHz. While the axis-alignment...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes an all-electric method to achieve high-fidelity quantum state transfer in germanium hole-spin qubit arrays. By precisely tuning the electric field to match spin-orbit phases or align the spin-orbit axis, the detrimental effects of anisotropic exchange can be neutralized. This provides a scalable pathway for quantum information transport in semiconductor-based quantum architectures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum networks as it provides a mechanism to use intrinsic spin-orbit coupling as a resource for robust quantum communication rather than a source of decoherence.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving high-fidelity, long-distance quantum state transfer in semiconductor hole-spin qubit arrays, which is typically hindered by spin-orbit induced anisotropic exchange.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that all-electric control via tuning the electric field strength (phase-matching) or direction (axis alignment) can restore near-perfect state transfer and suppress leakage processes.

**Method.** The study uses a Schrieffer-Wolff transformation to derive an effective spin Hamiltonian from a Fermi-Hubbard model and employs Van Vleck perturbation theory to analyze the low-energy dynamics and robustness against charge noise.

**Model / system.** A chain of semiconductor hole-spin qubits (specifically Ge hole-spin quantum dots) acting as a quantum bus, modeled by an anisotropic exchange Hamiltonian with spin-orbit rotation.

**Key observables.** State-transfer fidelity (F), effective tunneling amplitude (teff), and oscillation frequency.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spin-orbit rotation angle (thetas), spin-orbit axis orientation (nso), electric field strength (E), and magnetic field (B).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system operates in the strongly interacting regime (U >> t) and assumes quasi-static charge noise as the primary decoherence mechanism.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows state-transfer fidelity as a function of the spin-orbit angle, highlighting discrete high-fidelity peaks at commensurate points.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of SOC-induced anisotropy, presents an effective Hamiltonian model, proposes two electric control protocols (magnitude and axis tuning), evaluates the robustness of these protocols against magnetic fields and charge noise, and concludes with a scalable 2D architecture proposal.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Semiconductor hole-spin qubits offer a promising route to quantum computation due to their weak hyperfine interaction, and strong intrinsic spin-orbit coupling enabling electric control of qubits. Scalable architectures, however, require coherent long-distance quantum state transfer, which is hindered in these systems by spin-orbit induced anisotropic exchange. Here we show that this limitation can be overcome by using an all-electric control protocol. By tuning the electric field strength, we identify discrete spin-orbit phase-matching conditions that restore near-perfect state transfer, independent of the rotation axis. Complementarily, controlling the electric field direction aligns the spin-orbit axis, suppressing excitation non-conserving processes and enabling robust transfer without fine tuning. Our results establish that electrical control of spin-orbit phases through either magnitude tuning or axis alignment as a practical route for robust quantum information transport in hole-spin quantum dot arrays.

</details>

### [Analyzing the two-dimensional doped Hubbard model with the Worldvolume HMC method](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14965v1)

**Authors:** Masafumi Fukuma, Yusuke Namekawa  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14965v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Number density ⟨𝑛⟩and energy density ⟨𝑒⟩in the continuum limit 𝜖→0, compared with results using ALF at 𝜖= 0.01 (figure adapted from [25]).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents the application of the Worldvolume Hybrid Monte Carlo method to solve the sign problem in the doped 2D Hubbard model. It demonstrates that this approach can accurately calculate physical observables in regimes where standard determinant QMC methods fail. The results are validated by showing convergence to known results in the continuum limit.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The numerical sign problem prevents standard determinant quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) from simulating the 2D Hubbard model when it is doped away from half-filling.

**Main result.** The Worldvolume Hybrid Monte Carlo (WV-HMC) method successfully predicts physical observables with controlled statistical errors in the doped regime where standard methods fail.

**Method.** The Worldvolume Hybrid Monte Carlo (WV-HMC) method, which uses an anti-holomorphic flow to deform the integration surface into a continuum union of surfaces (the worldvolume) to mitigate the sign problem.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional Hubbard model on a periodic, bipartite square lattice, characterized by hopping amplitude (t), on-site repulsion (U), and chemical potential (mu).

**Key observables.** Number density operator <n> and energy density operator <e>.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lattice size 8x8, temperature T/t = 1/6.4, interaction strength U/t = 8.0, and the tuning parameter alpha for balancing sign suppression and ergodicity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Uses a symmetric Trotter decomposition up to O(epsilon^2) and assumes the validity of the continuum limit as the Trotter step epsilon approaches zero.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares number density against ALF results; Figure 2 shows finite-epsilon effects on number density; Figure 3 presents number density and energy density in the continuum limit.

**Paper structure.** The paper defines the sign problem, introduces the WV-HMC method, details the Hubbard model Hamiltonian and path integral implementation, presents simulation results for density and energy, and discusses the continuum limit and ergodicity.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We apply the Worldvolume Hybrid Monte Carlo (WV-HMC) method [arXiv:2012.08468] to the two-dimensional Hubbard model, which is known to suffer from a severe sign problem when the system is doped (away from half filling). We show that the method predicts physical observables with controlled statistical errors on an $8 \times 8$ lattice at temperature $T/t = 1/6.4 \approx 0.156$ and interaction strength $U/t = 8.0$ ($t$ is the hopping amplitude), for which the standard determinant quantum Monte Carlo fails.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Authors:** Dhanvi Bharadwaj, Siddharth Dangwal, Yuewen Hou, Gokul Subramanian Ravi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14042v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 2. Unlike traditional compilers that decouple program optimization from logical execution (Top), Qomet (Bottom) introduces a Co-Design Engine that fuses algorithmic properties with LS primitives. By reinterpreting sequential gate chains as simultaneous multi-target interactions, Qomet bypasses the artificial constraints of slice-based mapping. The resulting scheduled instructions account for realistic microarchitectural overheads (e.g., routing time), enabling high-density instruction execution and a marked reduction in program duration compared to serialized baselines.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14042_figures/2605.14042_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 3. (a) Logical surface code qubits (b) 2-D layout for 9 logical program qubits, where logical operations are real- ized by coupling program qubit boundaries to intermediary ancilla patches (𝑞0–𝑞8) using LS.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2605.14042_figures/2605.14042_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 5. C-Phase is decomposed since it is not a native gate, enabling execution on standard hardware gate sets.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14042_figures/2605.14042_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 6. Eliminating false dependencies via CP-aware compilation. (1) Example circuit with two CP gates. (2) Existing approach: despite spatial independence, the compiler enforces serialization. Slices (2a–c) show the CNOT–𝑅𝑧(𝜃)–CNOT decomposition of CP(0, 1), while (2d–f) show CP(2, 0) under the same constraints. Although oper- ations such as (2d) could overlap with (2a) and (2c), false dependencies force sequential execution. (3) Qomet: exposes parallelism via multi-target C-Phase execution. Slices (3a) and (3c) show concurrent CNOT(0, 1) and CNOT(0, 2), while (3b) shows simultaneous independent teleportation from magic-state patches.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14042_figures/2605.14042_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 7. Layouts proposed in prior work with the MS- starved density: (a) Compact [35], (b) 1/2-filling [23], (c) 2/3- filling floorplan [23], and (d) Square sparse [35].</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces Qomet, a compiler designed to optimize fault-tolerant quantum computing by treating the hardware layout as a continuous, dynamic resource. By exploiting the commutativity of C-Phase gates and using a cycle-accurate scheduler, it significantly reduces the time required to execute complex quantum algorithms like QAOA and QFT.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Existing compilers for fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) use rigid, slice-based abstractions that ignore microarchitectural details, leading to high latency and inefficient resource utilization due to routing contention and artificial dependencies.

**Main result.** The Qomet compiler achieves up to a 59.7x reduction in execution time by leveraging C-Phase commutativity for multi-target interactions and using a cycle-accurate, event-driven scheduler.

**Method.** A microarchitecture-aware co-design engine that uses multi-target grouping, routing-aware packing via Steiner trees, and a dynamic, event-driven scheduler to overlap operations and minimize qubit idling.

**Model / system.** A fault-tolerant quantum computing architecture based on the surface code, utilizing lattice surgery (LS) for logical operations (merge/split) and magic state distillation/cultivation for non-Clifford gates.

**Key observables.** Execution latency (clock cycles), instructions per cycle (IPC), resource utilization, and routing footprint.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code distance (d), magic state availability (MS-Abundant vs. MS-Starved), and the number of nearest candidates (k) for magic state selection.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The approach is specifically designed for surface-code-based lattice surgery architectures and assumes a regime where quantum and classical resources are significant constraints.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares existing work to Qomet's speedup; Figure 2 shows the Qomet Co-Design Engine architecture; Figure 3 depicts the surface code 2D layout; Figure 4 details the temporal steps of a CNOT via lattice surgery; Figures 5-6 illustrate gate decomposition and parallelism; Figures 7-13 show performance scaling across various layouts, qubit counts, and magic state regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of inefficient FTQC compilation, presents the Qomet compiler framework (grouping, packing, and rotation realization), details the event-driven scheduling and cost-aware magic state selection, and concludes with extensive benchmarks on QAOA and QFT workloads across different hardware regimes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Achieving practical quantum advantage on fault-tolerant quantum computers (FTQC) is fundamentally constrained by the substantial spatial and temporal overheads required to map logical operations onto physical hardware. Existing compilation approaches typically adopt coarse-grained, slice-based abstractions that overlook fine-grained microarchitectural effects, such as routing contention, leading to inefficient resource utilization and limited alignment between algorithm structure and hardware capabilities.   This work presents a microarchitecture-aware compilation approach that integrates algorithmic structure directly with lattice surgery (LS) execution. By leveraging the commutativity of C-Phase operations, the method transforms inherently sequential gate sequences into concurrent multi-target interactions, effectively removing artificial dependencies and exposing significant instruction-level parallelism. To enable this, we design a dynamic, event-driven scheduling strategy that accurately models spatial layout and routing constraints, allowing operations to overlap in time while minimizing contention. Through improved coordination of computation and communication, this approach substantially reduces idle resources and achieves up to a 59.7$\times$ reduction in execution time compared to standard baselines.

</details>

### [Carrier-density dependence of magnetotransport in correlated Dirac semimetal CaIrO$_3$](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14180v1)

**Authors:** Rinsuke Yamada, Jun Fujioka, Minoru Kawamura, Tatsuya Okawa, Yoshio Kaneko, Shiro Sakai, Motoaki Hirayama, Ryotaro Arita, Kiyohiro Adachi, Daisuke Hashizume, Yoshinori Tokura  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14180v1>

</figure>

**Summary.** This paper explores how changing the carrier density in the Dirac semimetal CaIrO3 affects its transport properties. It demonstrates that at low densities, the system enters a quantum limit where enhanced Coulomb interactions lead to non-linear magnetoresistance. The results confirm the presence of a robust Dirac dispersion characterized by a constant Fermi velocity.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the carrier-density dependence of magnetotransport properties, specifically the origin of giant magnetoresistance and the transition in MR scaling behavior in the correlated Dirac semimetal CaIrO3.

**Main result.** The study finds that as carrier density decreases, mobility and magnetoresistance increase, with the MR scaling transitioning from B-linear to B-nonlinear (alpha > 2) due to enhanced long-range Coulomb interactions in the quantum limit. The constant Fermi velocity across different carrier densities provides strong evidence for k-linear Dirac dispersion.

**Method.** Transport measurements including resistivity, Hall effect, and Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations were performed on single crystals at low temperatures and high magnetic fields, supplemented by DFT+DMFT modeling.

**Model / system.** The system is CaIrO3, an orthorhombic perovskite characterized as a correlated Dirac semimetal with a line node in its energy dispersion and 5d orbitals featuring a j_eff = 1/2 multiplet.

**Key observables.** Carrier density (nH), mobility, transverse magnetoresistance (MR) ratio, effective mass (mc), Fermi velocity (vF), and MR exponent (alpha).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low carrier density regime (nH ~ 2.2 x 10^16 cm^-3), quantum limit, magnetic fields up to 14 T, and temperatures down to 2 K.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Difficulty in precisely estimating the Thomas-Fermi screening length and the exact strength of the impurity potential.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the crystal structure, energy dispersion schematic, and temperature/density dependence of resistivity and MR; Fig 2 compares Hall resistivity and MR ratio across samples; Fig 3 verifies Kohler's rule.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the material and its electronic structure, presents transport data across varying carrier densities, analyzes quantum oscillations to characterize the Dirac dispersion, discusses the scaling of magnetoresistance, and concludes with a physical interpretation of the scattering mechanisms.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report the carrier density dependence of the magnetotransport property in the correlated Dirac semimetal CaIrO$_3$. In the dilute carrier density region ($n_{\rm H}$ $\sim 2.2 \times 10^{16} \,$$\rm{cm}^{-3}$) at $2 \, \mathrm{K}$, the mobility exceeds $1.0 \times 10^{5} \,$$\rm{cm}^{2}/\rm{Vs}$ at $2 \, \mathrm{K}$, and the transverse magnetoresistance (MR) reaches $2,000 \,$\% at $12 \, \mathrm{T}$. The analysis of quantum oscillations and Hall conductivity shows that the Fermi velocity is nearly independent of the cross-sectional area of the Fermi surface, or equivalently the carrier density, supporting a $k$-linear dispersion of the Dirac node. The field dependence of magnetoresistivity is nearly $B$-linear in the moderate carrier density region ($n_\mathrm{H} \geq 4 \times 10^{16}\,$cm$^{-3}$), but scales with $B^α$ ($α> 2$) in the lower carrier density region. The variation of magnetoresistivity is likely affected by the enhanced long-range Coulomb interaction in the quantum limit, where Dirac electrons are subject to the magnetic confinement.

</details>

### [Deforming the Trail: Baseline Quantum Circuitry for $\text{SU(2)}_k$ Lattice Gauge Theory](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15076v1)

**Authors:** Zoë Webb-Mack, Natalie Klco  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15076v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Links a, b, c and d are controls and remain unchanged. The matrix element for this transition is governed by Eq. (4) and (7). Active links of the plaquette on the original lattice (far left) are |ql⟩, |jt a⟩, |jb a⟩, |qr⟩. Links |jt l ⟩, |jb l ⟩, |jt r⟩, |jb r⟩are external but connected to the plaquette at its vertices. These links serve as controls in the reduced basis with projection degrees of freedom integrated out. Each step in the F-sequence turns the active link it acts on into...</figcaption>

An order-8 polynomial fit applied to |Hphys| estimates that the ratio converges to the dashed line at 0.2563(5) .</figcaption>

Circuit capable of implementing plaquette oper- ator time evolution (τ = t g2NT for NT Trotter steps) via F-sequence diagonalization, demonstrated for the qubit- truncated gauge field (k = 1). By rotating from the eigen- basis of the electric operator to that of the compressed plaquette operator, the time evolution is isolated to a single-qudit active space on the modified lattice (|jb a⟩). Each step in the diagonalization can be accomplished by a series of multi-controlled single qudit gates. Each con- trol sector corresponds to a set of external links that allow Hphys →H′ phys transitions, as indicated by the diagrams above UF1 components.</figcaption>
</figure>

For reference, the GCX scal- ing of the non-deformed theory as reported in Ref. [61] is shown in gray. The baseline(reduced) GCX scaling of the q-deformed Trotter step is shown in dark teal(light teal). For the qubit truncation k = 1, immediate improvement (light teal star) is available as discussed in Appendix C 2.</figcaption>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper proposes a method to simulate q-deformed SU(2) lattice gauge theories on quantum computers with significantly lower gate complexity. By using q-deformation and gauge-variant completions, the authors reduce the scaling of required gates from O(d^8) to O(d^5). This approach provides a more efficient and unitary-preserving way to truncate gauge fields for quantum simulation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and quantum computing as it provides a scalable method for simulating gauge theories, which are fundamental to understanding strongly interacting quantum fields.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of implementing the magnetic term of the SU(2) lattice gauge theory Hamiltonian on quantum hardware due to high gate complexity and the non-unitarity of truncated operators.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that using q-deformed SU(2)k theory and Gauge-Variant Completions (GVC) reduces the quantum resource scaling for generalized-controlled-X gates from O(d^8) to O(d^5).

**Method.** The study employs a diagrammatic approach using phased F-moves and G-moves, leveraging q-deformed 6j symbols and symmetry analysis (flux hierarchy inversion) to optimize circuit synthesis.

**Model / system.** A q-deformed SU(2)k Yang-Mills pure-gauge theory on a 1D lattice (plaquette chain) with periodic boundary conditions, utilizing a truncated Hilbert space with dimension d = k + 1.

**Key observables.** Quantum resource upper-bounds (GCX gate counts), circuit depth, and the ratio of the physical Hilbert space dimension.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Truncation level k, deformation parameter q, and the dimension of the local Hilbert space d.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on SU(2) pure gauge theory in 1D and assumes the ability to implement multi-controlled single-qudit unitaries on quantum hardware.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows F-move diagrams and matrix elements; Figure 2 plots the scaling of the physical Hilbert space and adjacency matrix symmetry; Figure 3 illustrates the circuit for k=1; Figure 4 compares gate scaling for different simulation strategies; Figure 6 shows the interleaved control sector strategy.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the SU(2) Hamiltonian and the need for truncation, introduces the q-deformed SU(2)k model, details the F-move diagonalization strategy and GVC technique, provides a resource scaling analysis, and concludes with optimized circuit implementations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantifying quantum resources for simulating the fundamental forces of Nature is sensitive to the mapping of gauge fields onto finite quantum computational architectures. When locally truncating lattice gauge theories in the irreducible representation basis, it has been proposed to further deform the theory via quantum groups. The purpose of this deformation is (1) to provide an infinite tower of finite-dimensional ($d = k+1$) groups systematically approximating the infinite-dimensional gauge links and (2) to restore the physical unitarity of a plaquette operator diagonalization procedure analytically derived from the field continuum by recontracting vertex pairs. For the SU(2)$_k$ Yang-Mills pure-gauge theory, we provide a constructive strategy of gauge-variant completions to extend this unitarity to the entire computational Hilbert space, leading to well-defined time evolution unitaries as targets for optimized circuit synthesis. Leveraging basic circuit decompositions and symmetries of the diagonalized plaquette operator, we report resource upper-bounds on the generalized-controlled-X two-qudit gates for arbitrary local truncation $d$, reducing estimates and scaling relative to the non-deformed theory by three polynomial powers from $O(d^8)$ to $O(d^5)$. Examining the stronger q-deformed gauge constraint, which softens the total flux at vertices, we show that the physical Hilbert space dimension of the deformed plaquette operator scales equivalently to its non-deformed counterpart with a constant factor $0.2563(5)$. Thus, despite affecting interactions at all scales as exemplified by the observed flux hierarchy inversion symmetry, q-deformation continues to pass scrutiny as a reliable truncation offering advantages in quantum circuit synthesis.

</details>

### [Discrete-phase-randomized mode-pairing quantum key distribution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14484v1)

**Authors:** Yuewei Xu, Zeyang Lu, Chan Li, Jian Long, Zhu Cao  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14484v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic of the discrete-phase-randomized mode-pairing MDI-QKD protocol. Alice and Bob prepare coherent pulses with random intensities from {0, µ} and phases φa i (φb i) uniformly drawn from D discrete values 2πn/D | n = 0, 1, . . . , D −1, each chosen with probability 1/D. Here, D represents the number of discrete phases, which is required to be an even integer. Pulses are sent to Charlie for measurement. Successfully detected pulses (solid lines) within a maximum pairing interval l are paired as leading (red) and trailing (blue) pulses, while pulses that are detected but fail to pair (grey) and pulses with failed detection (dashed lines) are discarded. The basis (Z or X) is...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14484_figures/2605.14484_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 3. The relation between the key rate and the transmis- sion distance for discrete phase with maximal pairing interval l = 102, 104 and 106. The dashed line is the key rate with discrete phase number D = 10 and the solid lines is the key rate with discrete phase number D = 12. The dot-dashed line is the PLOB bound.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14484_figures/2605.14484_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 2. The relation between the key rate and the trans- mission distance for continuously random phases and discrete phases with maximal pairing interval l = 106. The dashed line is the key rate for continuously random phases and the solid lines from left to right are for 8, 10, 12, and 14 discrete phases, respectively. The dot-dashed line is the PLOB bound.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a quantum key distribution protocol that uses discrete rather than continuous phase randomization. It proves that a small number of discrete phases (around 14) are sufficient to match the performance of the ideal case while significantly reducing the required randomness.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it addresses the practical implementation of quantum communication protocols by bridging the gap between idealized continuous-variable models and realistic discrete-variable hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The existing mode-pairing QKD protocol relies on an experimentally infeasible assumption of continuous phase randomization, which also requires an impractical, infinite supply of random bits.

**Main result.** The authors propose a discrete-phase-randomized (DPR) protocol that achieves security using only a few random bits and demonstrates that performance converges to the continuous case with as few as 14 discrete phases.

**Method.** The study uses a mathematical framework of pseudo k-photon states, fidelity-based error estimation, and a discrete version of the decoy-state method to bound phase error rates.

**Model / system.** A Measurement-Device-Independent (MDI) QKD setup involving two parties (Alice and Bob) and an untrusted third party (Charlie) using coherent state light sources with discretized phases.

**Key observables.** Key rate, Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), yield of pseudo single-photon states, and fidelity between bases.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of discrete phases (D), pairing interval (l), intensity (mu, nu), and transmission distance.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes an even number of discrete phases, uniform probability for phase selection, and a symmetrically attenuating channel.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the protocol schematic; Figure 2 plots the key rate versus distance for various D values compared to the PLOB bound.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the security vulnerability of continuous phase randomization, proposes the DPR-MP-QKD protocol, develops the mathematical framework for security analysis using fidelity and decoy states, and concludes with simulation results showing convergence and resource efficiency.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Mode-pairing quantum key distribution (MP-QKD) protocol achieves performance beyond the repeaterless rate-transmittance bound and exhibits excellent practicality by avoiding the requirement for difficult global phase locking. However, the source side of MP-QKD still relies on the assumption of continuous phase randomization, an experimentally infeasible requirement in practice. Therefore, the practical security of the protocol cannot be fully guaranteed. In this work, we propose a discrete-phase-randomized mode-pairing quantum key distribution (DPR-MP-QKD) protocol and analyze the basis-dependence of the source side. Then, we introduce a concrete discrete version of the decoy state method that ensures the security of the DPR-MP-QKD protocol. Finally, simulation results indicate that as the number of discrete phases increases, the key rate performance of DPR-MP-QKD progressively approaches that of the continuous case, with convergence achieved at approximately 14 discrete phases. Moreover, our approach drastically lowers the demand for randomness. While conventional continuous phase randomization demands an unlimited supply of random bits, we show that merely a few bits (e.g., 4) are adequate.

</details>

### [Failure-Guided Fuzzing for Hybrid Quantum-Classical Programs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14219v1)

**Authors:** Lei Zhang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14219v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14219_figures/2605.14219_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1: Illustration of the rare-event barrier. If a crash occurs only when a specific Nq-qubit bitstring is observed and the measurement distribution is close to uniform, then the ex- pected number of crashes under budget B is at most B ·2−Nq. Even large fixed budgets quickly fall below the expected crash as Nq grows.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14219_figures/2605.14219_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: Failure-guided fuzzing workflow: crash seeds are discovered through random, enumerative, or concolic exploration, then used for local circuit-parameter fuzzing under a fixed budget.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14219_figures/2605.14219_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 4: VQE: distribution of crash counts per trial for each strategy over T = 20 trials with budget B = 2,000. Boxes show interquartile ranges; whiskers and markers indicate spread and outliers.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14219_figures/2605.14219_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 6: QAOA: distribution of crash counts per trial for each strategy over T = 20 trials with budget B = 2,000.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14219_figures/2605.14219_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5: QAOA ansatz used in the case study: depth-P QAOA for MaxCut on a 4-node ring graph, starting from |+⟩⊗n and alternating cost layers with ZZ(γℓ) rotations on each edge and mixer layers with RX(2βℓ) rotations on all qubits.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new testing framework for hybrid quantum-classical algorithms that uses previously discovered failures to guide the search for new errors. By fuzzing quantum parameters around known non-convergent points, the method significantly improves the efficiency of finding bugs compared to random sampling.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Testing hybrid quantum-classical (HQC) algorithms like VQE and QAOA is difficult because failures (non-convergence) are rare events that are hard to find within realistic execution budgets.

**Main result.** Failure-guided local fuzzing, which perturbs quantum circuit parameters around identified non-convergent seeds, is the primary driver of improvement over random testing, though the benefit of concolic seed discovery depends on the specific workload.

**Method.** A two-phase fuzzing strategy consisting of (1) seed discovery using random, enumerative, or concolic methods, and (2) local fuzzing by mutating quantum circuit parameters around those seeds.

**Model / system.** The study uses two hybrid quantum-classical workloads implemented in Qiskit: a 2-qubit Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) and a 4-qubit Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) for the MaxCut problem on a ring graph.

**Key observables.** Crash count (number of non-convergence events) and crash rate (crashes per execution budget).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits (Nq), execution budget (B), classical optimizer hyperparameters (learning rate, iteration limit), and quantum circuit parameters (rotation angles).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes noiseless simulators and focuses on a small-scale, discrete classical hyperparameter space for the concolic exploration component.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the exponential decay of expected crashes as qubits increase; Figure 2 shows the failure-guided fuzzing workflow; Figure 3 depicts the QAOA diagram; Figure 6 shows the distribution of crash counts for QAOA.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the testing challenge of HQC algorithms, defines the hybrid input model, proposes a two-phase failure-guided fuzzing strategy, compares five different testing strategies via VQE and QAOA case studies, and concludes with a discussion on workload dependency and future directions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hybrid quantum-classical (HQC) algorithms, such as the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) and the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA), are central to near-term quantum computing but remain challenging to test. Sampling-based fuzzing can expose faulty or non-convergent configurations, but under realistic execution budgets, it may miss failure-prone regions in the joint space of classical optimizer settings and quantum circuit parameters.   This paper studies failure-guided fuzzing for HQC programs. It models a hybrid input as a pair of classical optimizer hyperparameters and quantum circuit parameters, and evaluates a two-phase strategy that first searches for non-convergent seeds and then locally fuzzes circuit parameters around those seeds. To understand where the gains come from, five budgeted strategies are compared: random hybrid testing, classical enumeration without fuzzing, random-seed local fuzzing, enumeration-seed local fuzzing, and concolic-seed local fuzzing. The study is implemented on a VQE instance and a QAOA MaxCut instance in Qiskit. The results show that failure-guided local fuzzing is the main driver of improvement over random testing, while concolic seed discovery provides additional benefits on VQE but is less stable on QAOA. These findings suggest that reusing failure information is a promising direction for HQC testing, but that the value of concolic seed discovery is workload-dependent.

</details>

### [Fermi Surface Geometry from Charge Fluctuations in Three-Dimensional Metals](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13951v1)

**Authors:** Pok Man Tam, Yarden Sheffer, Xiao-Chuan Wu, F. D. M. Haldane, Shinsei Ryu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13951v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the shape and quantum geometry of 3D Fermi surfaces are imprinted on the subleading logarithmic scaling of charge fluctuations. It provides a mathematical link between physical observables and topological invariants like the Euler characteristic and Chern number. This establishes a way to probe the complex geometry of multi-band metals through density-density correlations.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how the geometric and quantum geometric properties of 3D Fermi surfaces are encoded in the subleading logarithmic term of bipartite charge fluctuations.

**Main result.** The authors establish that the logarithmic coefficient of charge fluctuations can be decomposed into a geometric part (related to the Euler characteristic and Will and Willmore energy) and a quantum geometric part (related to the quantum metric and Chern number), and they derive a topological lower bound for this coefficient.

**Method.** The study uses analytical expansions of the structure factor, differential geometry (curvature tensors, Gauss-Bonnet theorem), and the application of Wick's theorem and RPA to evaluate momentum-space integrals.

**Model / system.** The system consists of three-dimensional non-interacting multi-band metals and Landau Fermi liquids, including specific models like the copper Fermi surface and Weyl metals.

**Key observables.** Bipartite charge fluctuations, structure factor S(q) (specifically the |q|^3 coefficient), and the logarithmic scaling coefficient Gamma.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fermi surface curvature, Euler characteristic, Chern number, quantum metric tensor, Willmore energy, and the Landau parameter F0.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes zero temperature, the thermodynamic limit, non-interacting or Landau Fermi liquid regimes, and that different Fermi surface components are separated and do not intersect.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 visualizes the structure factor overcounting and critical lines on the copper Fermi surface; Figure 3 shows the dependence of structure factor coefficients on the Landau parameter F0.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of encoding Fermi surface geometry in charge fluctuations, moves to the analytical derivation of the structure factor expansion, decomposes the logarithmic coefficient into geometric and quantum geometric parts, establishes topological bounds, and discusses implications for interacting Fermi liquids and Weyl systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

For three-dimensional non-interacting multi-band metals, we show that important information about the shape and the quantum geometry of Fermi surfaces is encoded in the subleading logarithmic term of bipartite charge fluctuations. This logarithmic term is related to the dimensionless $|\mathbf{q}|^3$-coefficient of the structure factor in momentum space, and both quantities can be expressed as Fermi surface integrals of the Fermi surface curvature tensor and the quantum metric tensor. When the real-space partition surface is a quadric (i.e., sphere or ellipsoid), the logarithmic coefficient satisfies a topological bound depending only on the Euler characteristic and the Chern number of the Fermi surface, illustrating a non-trivial interplay between topology and quantum topology in multi-band metals.

</details>

Mattis overlaps mµ for a 50/50 mixture of helix and Lissajous patterns, with pseudo-inverse couplings, simulated annealing (β : 0.5 →5.0), and sequential updates; columns correspond to the three layers (σ, τ, ϕ). Top row: thermal fluctuations break the initial symmetry and one com- ponent reaches m ≈1 consistently across layers. Bottom row: both components remain trapped at the spurious mixture fixed point. **Summary.** This paper develops a method to map continuous, real-world data into Ising spin systems for associative memory. It demonstrates that using pseudo-inverse couplings and specific update dynamics allows for robust pattern reconstruction and the ability to disentangle overlapping signals, even when applied to noisy biological EEG data.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Bridging the gap between high-dimensional continuous signals and discrete Ising spin frameworks for associative memory, specifically addressing basin collapse and cross-modal retrieval.

**Main result.** The authors establish a multi-layer hetero-associative framework that achieves robust cross-modal reconstruction and spontaneous symmetry breaking, with an asymptotic storage capacity of approximately 0.50.

**Method.** A pipeline of PCA whitening and SimHash random-hyperplane projection is used to encode continuous signals into Ising spins, combined with Kanter-Sompolinsky pseudo-inverse couplings and Monte Carlo dynamics.

**Model / system.** A tri-layer hetero-associative Ising spin system where layers are coupled pairwise using pseudo-inverse memory couplings to decorrelate patterns and stabilize retrieval.

**Key observables.** Overlap (intra-class and inter-class), separation gap (delta q), Basin Collapse Index (BCI), Mattis magnetization, and storage capacity (alpha_c).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Inverse temperature (beta), spin dimension (N), loading parameter (alpha), Tikhonov regularization (gamma), and bit-flip noise rate (p_flip).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes SimHash entries are i.i.d. Gaussian and that PCA whitening can be performed; assumes the ability to perform sign re-binarization.

**Figures summary.** Figures show magnetization dynamics, temperature-dependent phase diagrams, separation gaps between spin overlaps, and the reconstruction of sleep-state signatures from EEG/EOG data.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the interface problem, describes the multi-layer encoding and coupling architecture, presents analytical proofs for encoding fidelity and scaling, details numerical simulations on synthetic curves, and concludes with an experimental application to human sleep polysomnography.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Real-world physical signals are continuous and high-dimensional, yet the statistical-mechanics machinery of associative memory operates on discrete Ising spins. We bridge this divide through a multilayer Ising framework that couples a geometry-preserving continuous-to-Ising encoder (PCA whitening composed with SimHash random-hyperplane projection) to Kanter-Sompolinsky pseudo-inverse memory couplings, embedded directly into the local-field equations of a tri-layer hetero-associative system. The pseudo-inverse correction renders the equal-weight mixture state thermodynamically unstable, so that thermal fluctuations break the cross-modal symmetry and select a single global winner. We further establish a dynamical duality: parallel (Little) updates are structurally required to ignite the cross-modal signal avalanche from a single cued layer, whereas sequential (Glauber) sweeps resolve symmetric superpositions. The operational storage capacity obeys the Amit-Gutfreund-Sompolinsky finite-size correction $α_c(N)=α_c(\infty)-c\,N^{-1/2}$, extrapolating to an asymptotic operational limit $α_c(\infty)\approx 0.50$ under macroscopic-basin retrieval. Applied to multi-channel sleep polysomnography (PhysioNet Sleep-EDF), the architecture reconstructs the macroscopic sleep state on parietal EEG and EOG axes from a single noisy frontal-EEG cue, demonstrating cross-modal recall in the presence of quenched biological disorder.

</details>

### [From Hilbert's Tenth Problem to Quantum Speedup: Explicit Oracles for Bounded Diophantine Systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13980v1)

**Authors:** Gabriel Escrig, M. A. Martin-Delgado  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13980v1>

Resource scaling is evaluated across 1500 Diophantine problem instances to establish empirical bounds. (Left) Log-log scaling of the Toffoli gate count as a function of the total number of logical qubits q. Data points are generated by varying the number of variables n ∈[1, 7], the maximum polynomial degree d ∈[2, 7], and the search interval length N. The empirical scaling exponent of 1.77 validates the sub-quadratic growth of the gate complexity in the pre-asymptotic regime. <figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Amplitude amplification dynamics for a strongly coupled multivariate quadratic Diophantine system. The plot displays the probability of measuring the unique target state P(|x = 3, y = 2, z = 1⟩) as a function of the number of Grover iterations. The underlying quantum oracle evaluates the explicit non-linear system: 3x2 + 2y2 + 5z2 = 40, 2xy −4yz + 3xz = 13, and −x2 + 5y −7z = −6. Each of the n = 3 variables is discretized using a 3-qubit register, yielding a global search space of size |D| = 29 = 512. The coherent search successfully navigates the complex arithmetic landscape, with the success probability peaking at 99.9% exactly at the theoretically predicted optimal step topt ≈⌊π</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13980_figures/2605.13980_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Schematic representation of a single Grover iteration. The circuit explicitly constructs the composite operator G = DOf, highlighting the sequential application of the Diophantine oracle (left block), which coherently evaluates the system of m polynomial equations to mark valid solution states, followed by the diffusion operator (right block), which amplifies their amplitudes.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13980_figures/2605.13980_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Circuit schematic of the logical rewiring technique. The implementation is demonstrated for the evaluation of the linear function f(x, y) = 3x −2y + 7. By exploiting cost-free logical bit-shifts (rewiring), the need for full quantum multipliers is eliminated. Since the coefficient of x (cx = 3 ≡112) has a Hamming weight of wH = 2, its scalar multiplication is strictly decomposed into two sequential quantum additions. Conversely, to implement the negative coefficient cy = −2, the circuit utilizes two’s complement arithmetic: the input qubits of the y register are inverted via X gates and the carry-in bit (Cin) is set to |1⟩. The scalar factor of 2 is concurrently resolved through a...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13980_figures/2605.13980_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Signed quantum squaring circuit. This schematic illustrates the in-place evaluation of a quadratic term. To prevent algorithmic self-reference conflicts, an ancillary qubit is temporarily entangled with the control bit via a CNOT gate, mediating its input to the adder. The final step, controlled by the sign bit (MSB), triggers the two’s complement subtraction logic (X inversions and Cin = |1⟩) to properly weight the negative MSB component.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a concrete quantum architectural design for solving polynomial equations over integers. By explicitly synthesizing the arithmetic gates required for the oracle, the authors prove that the quantum advantage is preserved and that the computational overhead remains polynomially bounded. This provides a scalable blueprint for applying Grover-type searches to integer optimization and cryptographic problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The computational intractability of solving bounded Diophantine systems (polynomial equations over integers) and the need for an explicit, resource-efficient quantum oracle that avoids the 'black-box' assumption.

**Main result.** The authors provide a gate-level synthesis of a reversible quantum arithmetic oracle that achieves a quadratic speedup over classical search while maintaining polynomial overhead in terms of qubit and gate complexity.

**Method.** A 'compute-utilize-uncompute' architecture using Grover's algorithm, two's complement arithmetic, and a shift-and-add engine for polynomial evaluation.

**Model / system.** A digital quantum computing framework operating on a bounded integer domain D = [-N/2, N/2 - 1]^n, utilizing quantum registers for system variables, function accumulation, and result counting.

**Key observables.** Toffoli gate count, qubit requirements (spatial complexity), and amplitude amplification probability dynamics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of variables (n), maximum polynomial degree (d), interval length (N), and Hamming weight of coefficients (w_H).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The problem is restricted to bounded (decidable) Diophantine systems; the analysis focuses on the pre-asymptotic regime; the architecture assumes two's complement binary encoding.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows scaling of gate counts and qubits; Figure 2 displays amplitude amplification success probability; Figure 3 shows the Grover iteration schematic; Figure 4 and 5 illustrate specific arithmetic circuit implementations (rewiring and squaring); Figure 6 and 7 show gate complexity scaling with respect to qubits, equations, and variables.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the mathematical definition of Diophantine systems, moves to the construction of a reversible arithmetic engine, details the gate-level synthesis of linear and quadratic oracles, provides a complexity analysis proving the preservation of quantum speedup, and concludes with empirical validation via state-vector simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Solving non-linear Diophantine systems lies at the mathematical core of integer optimization and cryptography. While the general unbounded problem is undecidable, even over bounded integer domains it remains classically intractable in the worst case. In this work, we introduce a fully reversible quantum algorithmic framework tailored to solve arbitrary polynomial Diophantine equations over bounded integer domains. The core of our approach is the explicit, gate-level synthesis of an evaluation oracle for amplitude amplification. By coherently evaluating polynomial constraints via in-place two's complement arithmetic and routing operations into a single recycled accumulator, this garbage-free strategy achieves a compact and scalable synthesis of the underlying non-linear arithmetic. Through analytical derivations and empirical circuit simulations, we prove that the overall spatial complexity is bounded by $q = \mathcal{O}((n + d^2)\log_2 N)$ logical qubits for $n$ variables, maximum degree $d$, and interval length $N$. The non-Clifford Toffoli depth is upper-bounded by $\mathcal{O}(q^2)$. This structural scaling exponent remains invariant to the variable count, modulated linearly only by the coefficients' Hamming weights. By moving beyond abstract black-box assumptions, this explicit architectural synthesis guarantees that the necessary quantum arithmetic acts as a bounded polynomial overhead. This ensures a quadratic speedup over classical exhaustive search, whether retrieving a unique assignment or dynamically enumerating an unknown number of solutions.

</details>

### [Graphical Algebraic Geometry: From Ideals and Varieties to Quantum Calculi](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13993v1)

**Authors:** Dichuan Gao, Razin A. Shaikh, Aleks Kissinger  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13993v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** The paper introduces Graphical Algebraic Geometry (GAG), a new family of diagrammatic languages for reasoning about commutative algebras and affine varieties. It establishes a formal link between GAG and the ZH quantum calculus, showing that the latter is an extension of the former. The work also demonstrates that rewriting in GAG is #P-hard, as it can represent counting constraint satisfaction problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers working on quantum circuit simplification and the development of new diagrammatic calculi, as it provides a rigorous algebraic-geometric foundation for the ZH calculus and links quantum computation to classical polynomial constraint satisfaction.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the need for a graphical calculus capable of modeling the nonlinear classical backbone of quantum computation, specifically for the ZH calculus and polynomial constraint satisfaction problems.

**Main result.** The authors introduce Graphical Algebraic Geometry (GAG), proving it is a sound, universal, and complete diagrammatic language for commutative algebras and affine varieties, and establish that the ZH quantum calculus is an extension of GAG.

**Method.** The researchers use category theory (PROPs, Lawvere theories, and structured spans/cospans) to construct the language and employ structural induction and complexity analysis to prove completeness and #P-hardness.

**Model / system.** The framework models algebraic structures such as commutative algebras, ideals, and affine varieties, and specifically relates these to the ZH calculus, a diagrammatic language for qudit quantum computation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The order of the finite field q, and the dimension of the polynomial rings and varieties.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The construction relies on the Noetherian property of polynomial rings and assumes the field k is either algebraically closed or finite for certain categorical equivalences.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the recursive definition of diagrammatic terms; Figure 2 presents the generators and rewrite rules of the Lawvere theory; Figure 3 and 4 illustrate scalable notation for polynomial operations; Figure 5 displays additional rewrite rules for GCA_k.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the GAG framework, constructs the GCA, GAG_k, and GAG_q languages, proves their soundness and completeness using category theory, demonstrates the connection to #CSP complexity, and finally characterizes the ZH calculus as an extension of GAG.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce Graphical Algebraic Geometry (GAG), a family of diagrammatic languages extending the Graphical Linear Algebra programme. We construct several languages within this family and prove that they are universal and complete for the corresponding (co)span semantics of commutative algebras and affine varieties. This framework provides clear graphical representations of algebraic structures -- such as polynomials, ideals, and varieties -- enabling intuitive yet rigorous diagrammatic reasoning. We showcase two practical viewpoints on GAG. First, we show that instances of counting constraint satisfaction problem (#CSP) are recast as rewrite problems of closed diagrams in GAG. This means that deciding rewritability in GAG is #P-hard, and GAG can be viewed as a complete and compositional rewrite system for networks of polynomial constraints. Second, we characterize the qudit ZH calculus, a diagrammatic language for quantum computation, as an extension of Graphical Algebraic Geometry. This establishes the correspondence that Graphical Algebraic Geometry is to the ZH calculus what Graphical Linear Algebra is to the ZX calculus. Using this construction, we show that computing amplitudes in qudit ZH requires only a constant number of queries to a GAG oracle.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Non-Invertible Symmetries and Boundaries for Two-Dimensional Fermions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13952v1)

**Authors:** Guillermo Arias-Tamargo, Philip Boyle Smith, Rishi Mouland, Maxwell L. Velásquez Cotini Hutt  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13952v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
