**Summary.** This paper classifies the non-invertible symmetries and boundary conditions for 2D fermionic systems. It establishes a deep connection between number theory (Pythagorean triples) and the existence of topological defects and symmetric boundaries in conformal field theories.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the relationship between boundary conditions and non-invertible (categorical) symmetries in 2D fermionic Conformal Field Theories (CFTs). Specifically, it seeks to classify anomaly-free invertible symmetries and their associated non-invertible topological defects.

**Main result.** The authors identify all anomaly-free invertible symmetries of two free complex Weyl fermions as cyclic groups Z_k related to primitive Pythagorean triples, and show that the theory is self-dual under gauging these symmetries. They further demonstrate that all U(1)^2 symmetric conformal boundary conditions for two Dirac fermions can be constructed by dressing a Dirichlet boundary with these non-invertible lines.

**Method.** The study employs modular transformation analysis of partition functions, symmetry gauging, and number-theoretic classification using Gaussian integers and Pythagorean triples. Microscopic realizations are provided via fermion-rotor coupling and Abelian gauge theories implementing symmetric mass generation.

**Model / system.** The primary system is a 2D Conformal Field Theory consisting of two free complex Weyl fermions. The study also considers two Dirac fermions on a manifold with a boundary (half-line).

**Key observables.** Partition functions in different spin structures (NS-NS, R-NS, NS-R, R-R), central charge, and scaling dimensions of operators.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The integer k = p^2 + q^2 (from coprime integers p and q), the gauging parameter t, and the mass parameters for the fermions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The symmetries considered are assumed to be faithful, discrete, and invertible 0-form symmetries.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the relationship between boundary conditions and non-invertible lines; Figure 2 shows modular S and T transformations for different spin structures; Figure 11 and 14 depict interfaces and boundary conditions involving Majorana modes.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by classifying anomaly-free invertible symmetries, moves to demonstrating the self-duality of the theory under gauging these symmetries, then constructs non-invertible defects and uses them to define conformal boundary conditions, and concludes with microscopic UV realizations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the relation between boundary conditions and categorical symmetries of two-dimensional fermionic conformal field theories. We determine all anomaly-free invertible global symmetries of two free complex Weyl fermions, which take the form $\mathbb{Z}_k$ for each primitive Pythagorean triple $a^2 + b^2 = k^2$. The theory is self-dual under gauging any of these symmetries, and so to each there is associated a non-invertible topological defect. We study the properties of these lines, and show that any conformal boundary condition of two Dirac fermions that preserves a $U(1)^2$ symmetry can be found by dressing a trivial Dirichlet boundary with one of them. We discuss two microscopic descriptions of these defects: fermions coupled to a quantum-mechanical rotor degree of freedom; and an abelian gauge theory that realises symmetric mass generation in a half-space.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14969"></a>
### [On the Symmetries of Anisotropic Spin Interaction Models](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14969v1)

**Authors:** Arist Zhenyuan Yang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14969v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14969_figures/2605.14969_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Spin model with twisted collinear SSG symmetries eC4 and eT . Panel (a) illustrates the Hamiltonian and the AFM background field (teal), while panel (b) shows the site- dependent spin operations associated with C4 (red) and T (purple), respectively. Here, each θ ∈[0, 2π) in the panel (b) stands for a local spin rotation Rz(θ) ∈SO(2)z.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14969_figures/2605.14969_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. The Hamiltonian (12) realizing tSSGs with arbitrary S0. The upper layer represents the spin-exchange Hamilto- nian Hbond, while the lower layer represents the on-site single- ion anisotropy Hamiltonian Hsite.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new theoretical framework called twisted Spin-Space Groups to accurately describe the symmetries of anisotropic magnetic systems. It reveals that these 'twisted' symmetries can create exotic topological excitations on non-orientable manifolds like the Klein bottle.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the inadequacy of conventional spin-space group (SSG) frameworks in describing anisotropic spin interactions, which do not merely break symmetries but 'twist' them through cohomology invariants.

**Main result.** The authors develop a twisted Spin-Space Group (tSSG) theory and demonstrate that this leads to topological quadrupolar excitations on a spin Brillouin Klein-bottle, characterized by a Z2 invariant and Mobius boundary states.

**Method.** The study utilizes group extension theory and cohomology invariants to classify symmetries, combined with linear flavor wave theory and Bogoliubov-de Gennes formalism to analyze the bosonic spectrum.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional spin-1 model on a square lattice featuring anisotropic exchange interactions (including Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya and XY components) and single-ion anisotropy.

**Key observables.** Bosonic excitation branches, edge mode energies, decay factor, and a Z2 topological invariant.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Single-ion anisotropy (D), exchange anisotropy parameters (a, b, c, lambda, alpha, beta, gamma), and the large-D ferroquadrupolar limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the lattice symmetry group contains a C4 cyclic subgroup and focuses on the large-D limit for the ground state vacuum.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the Hamiltonian components, including the AFM background field, and visualizes the site-dependent spin operations associated with C4 rotation and time-reversal symmetry.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of symmetry breaking vs. twisting, develops the mathematical framework for tSSG using group cohomology, applies this to a specific spin-1 model, and concludes with the topological implications for excitations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that anisotropic spin interactions do not merely break spin-space group (SSG) symmetries, but instead twist them through cohomology invariants, yielding symmetry classes beyond subgroups of $O(3)\times \operatorname{Isom}(\mathbb{R}^3) $. This requires redefining the spin-only group $S_0$ in terms of proper spin rotations. Based on this unitary $S_0$, we formulate a twisted SSG (tSSG) theory that captures the complete set of spin-space symmetries. We then study a spin-1 model with tSSG symmetry using linear flavor wave theory and find topological quadrupolar excitations defined on a spin Brillouin Klein-bottle rather than the conventional torus. Specifically, the bosonic BdG Hamiltonian satisfies a glide reflection sewing relation, the ribbon spectrum exhibits Möbius boundary states. These topological excitations are classified by $ \mathbb{Z}_2 $, enforced by the nonorientability of the Klein-bottle.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.14148"></a>
### [Operator ordering as an emergent geometric background in Dirac systems with spatially varying mass](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14148v1)

**Authors:** C. A. S. Almeida  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14148v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14148_figures/2605.14148_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Spectral consequences of operator ordering for Dirac modes on a ring with angular mass modulation m(θ) = m0+δm cos θ. (a) Energy levels En as a function of the modulation amplitude δm/m0 for quantum numbers n = −2, . . . , 2. Dashed lines correspond to the unordered Hamiltonian H0; solid lines include the ordering-induced correction of Eq. (7). (b) Ordering-induced second-order spectral shift ∆E(2) n = (8E(0) n )−1(1/ √</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the requirement for probability-current conservation in relativistic systems uniquely fixes the operator ordering for spatially varying mass. This fixed ordering acts as an emergent geometric background that causes measurable shifts in the energy spectra of Dirac particles.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the ambiguity of operator ordering in the Dirac Hamiltonian when mass varies spatially, specifically investigating if the unique ordering required for probability-current conservation leads to observable spectral effects.

**Main result.** The study finds that the required Hermitian ordering introduces an effective geometric connection that induces a universal, second-order spectral shift, which becomes strongly enhanced near the mass-inversion threshold.

**Method.** The authors use analytical derivation based on Hermiticity and current conservation requirements, combined with second-order perturbation theory and explicit computation in a 1D ring geometry.

**Model / system.** A single-particle Dirac field coupled to a spatially varying scalar mass profile m(x), with specific analysis applied to a 1D ring with angular mass modulation.

**Key observables.** Probability current, spectral quantization condition, energy level shifts, and the effective geometric connection (holonomy).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Logarithmic gradient of the mass profile, mass-inversion threshold, modulation amplitude (epsilon), and the geometric scale (grad m / m).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The mass profile is assumed to be smooth, single-valued, and periodic; the derivation focuses on the single-particle Hamiltonian.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1(a) shows the evolution of energy levels (En) as a function of the mass modulation amplitude (delta m/m0).

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the problem of operator ordering ambiguity, derives the unique Hermitian Hamiltonian required for current conservation, compares the relativistic case to the non-relativistic case, and then provides analytical results for spectral shifts in a ring geometry.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the spectral consequences of the uniquely determined Hermitian ordering of the Dirac Hamiltonian with spatially varying mass. In contrast to the nonrelativistic case, where continuous families of admissible prescriptions exist, the relativistic Dirac operator admits a single consistent ordering compatible with probability-current conservation. This requirement generates an additional logarithmic-gradient term proportional to the spatial variation of the mass profile. We show that this contribution modifies the effective kinetic operator and induces a universal deformation of the spectral quantization condition. In compact geometry, an explicit analytic computation reveals a mode-dependent second-order spectral shift that becomes strongly enhanced near the mass-inversion threshold. These results demonstrate that the consistent relativistic ordering of the Dirac operator leads to observable modifications of discrete spectra in spatially inhomogeneous scalar backgrounds.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14640"></a>
### [Perfect transmission and parallel composition for quantum walks on graphs with two leads](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14640v1)

**Authors:** Allan John Gerrard, Ryo Asaka, Kazumitsu Sakai  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14640v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14640_figures/2605.14640_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Visual description of the operation of extending the graph G up the first lead.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14640_figures/2605.14640_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: The hyperbola (24) in the µ-ν plane. The points of the hyperbola are parametrised by the phase of transmission θ ∈(−π, 0) ∪(0, π).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14640_figures/2605.14640_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: A visual explanation of the perfect transmission property of a two-terminal graph consisting of parallel paths at momentum k = −π/4. Each path is represented as a vector in the µ-ν plane, and their parallel composition corresponds to the sum of these vectors, landing precisely on the perfect transmission hyperbola.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14640_figures/2605.14640_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Left: the graph obtained by combining graphs G(1) and G(2) in parallel. Right: a graphical explanation of perfect transmission exhibited by G at k = −π/6. The vector (µ, ν) for each graph is plotted as a blue line. The sum of the two vectors for G(1) and G(2) lands on the perfect transmission hyperbola, indicated by the blue star.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14640_figures/2605.14640_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Phase gate for operating momentum k = −π/4. The resulting phase angle is equal to ψ = arccos(−1/3) −π/2 ≈0.108173π.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper develops a method to design quantum graphs with specific scattering properties by decomposing them into simpler components. By mapping transmission conditions to a geometric vector-sum problem, the authors show how to construct complex networks that act as perfect momentum filters or phase gates.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a systematic, modular approach to designing quantum networks and gates by treating complex graph structures as combinations of simple, additive building blocks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of constructing finite graphs with prescribed scattering properties, such as perfect transmission or specific phase gates, for continuous-time quantum walks.

**Main result.** The authors introduce three additive quantities (mu1, mu2, and nu) that transform the search for specific transmission properties into a geometric vector-sum problem in a (mu, nu)-plane.

**Method.** The study uses the S-matrix formalism, Schur complements, and characteristic polynomials of graphs and their subgraphs, alongside a parallel composition rule for graph building.

**Model / system.** Continuous-time quantum walks on finite, edge-weighted graphs connected to two semi-infinite paths (leads), where the Hamiltonian is the adjacency matrix of the infinite graph.

**Key observables.** S-matrix elements (reflection and transmission amplitudes), transmission probability, transmission phase, and effective length.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Momentum (k), edge weights (omega), and the admittance-like parameters (mu1, mu2, nu).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The graph G is assumed to be connected; the edge weights are real-weighted for the geometric construction.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the process of extending a graph up a lead; Figure 2 illustrates graph extension; Figure 3 visualizes the vector sum of paths landing on a hyperbola; Figure 4 shows a specific vector sum achieving perfect transmission; Figure 5 illustrates a phase gate setup.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the scattering problem and S-matrix in terms of graph polynomials, introduces the additive mu-nu parameters for parallel composition, provides a geometric framework for finding perfect transmission, and concludes with practical examples of constructing phase gates and calculating effective lengths.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study scattering for continuous-time quantum walks on finite graphs with two attached leads. We derive explicit formulae for the two-terminal scattering matrix in terms of characteristic polynomials of the finite graph and its vertex-deleted subgraphs. For real-weighted two-terminal graphs, we then introduce three real quantities, $μ_1$, $μ_2$, and $ν$, which are each additive under parallel composition of graphs. In these variables, perfect transmission at fixed momentum is characterized by the condition $μ_1=μ_2$ together with a hyperbola in the corresponding $(μ,ν)$-plane, whose points determine the transmission phase. This turns the search for graphs with prescribed transmission properties into a geometric vector-sum problem for smaller building blocks.

</details>

### [QUACOD: Quantum Optimization via Coordinate Descent for Scalable Drone Scheduling](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14001v1)

**Authors:** Van-Quang-Huy Nguyen, Hoang-Quan Nguyen, Samee U. Khan, Ilya Safro, Khoa Luu  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14001v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2605.14001_figures/2605.14001_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">

(a) Three predetermined routes, A, B, and C, have completion times of 3.4, 2.8, and 4.4 hours, respectively. (b) An example schedule assigns routes A and B to the red drone (completion time: 3.4 + 1.15 + 2.8 = 7.35 hours) and route C to the green drone (completion time: 4.4 hours). The objective is to minimize the time until all drones have completed their routes, which equals 7.35 hours in this example.</figcaption>
</figure>

The quantum component prepares a parameterized state |ψ(θ)⟩and measures the expected value ⟨Hf⟩, which the classical optimizer uses to iteratively update θ until convergence.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14001_figures/2605.14001_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3. Heuristic for transformation from Cmax minimization to con- vergence toward T/q. (a)-(c) Route assignment minimizes Cmax toward the optimal solution. (d)-(e) Minimizing the sum of squared distances P j∈[q]  Tj −T</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4. A single-layer hardware-efficient ansatz for an m-qubit register, comprising two Ry rotation blocks with a linear CZ entanglement chain on consecutive qubit pairs in between. Each Ry gate has its own trainable parameter, giving 2m parameters θ0,1, . . . , θ0,m, θ1,1, . . . , θ1,m in total.</figcaption>
</figure>

Column (A) shows the objective function value per iteration; Column (B) shows the time (in hours) for all drones to complete all routes per iteration.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces QUACOD, a new hybrid quantum-classical algorithm designed to tackle large-scale optimization problems like drone scheduling. By using coordinate descent to break down complex problems into smaller, qubit-manageable subproblems, it achieves significantly better scalability and performance on NISQ-era hardware compared to existing methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of solving large-scale, NP-hard drone scheduling problems on NISQ-era hardware, which lacks the necessary qubit count and quality for direct optimization.

**Main result.** The QUACOD algorithm outperforms the previous state-of-the-art (QUADRO) in both solution quality and scalability, handling up to 5 times more drones and 35 times more routes.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical approach using Coordinate Descent to decompose large problems into smaller subproblems, which are then optimized using Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQE or QAOA) with hardware-efficient circuits.

**Model / system.** The problem is modeled as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problem, which is mathematically equivalent to finding the ground state of an Ising model Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Drone completion times (makespan) and the objective function value during the optimization process.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of drones (q), number of routes (n), recharging time (c), number of available qubits (m), and the penalty parameter (p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Drones are assumed to be identical, routes are pre-determined, and the model does not account for heterogeneous vehicle fleets or complex real-world logistics.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the drone scheduling problem; Fig 2 depicts the hybrid quantum-classical loop; Fig 3 illustrates the heuristic for minimizing completion time; Fig 4 shows the hardware-efficient ansatz; Fig 5 shows the convergence of the objective function.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the drone scheduling problem, presents the QUACOD algorithm based on coordinate descent, describes the QUBO reformulation and VQE implementation, details the experimental setup and benchmarks against QUADRO, and concludes with scalability results and limitations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum computing has demonstrated its potential to solve various optimization problems, including drone scheduling, which is important not only for drone delivery but also for logistics in general. However, one of the main obstacles is that practical drone scheduling settings typically require quantum resources that current hardware cannot provide. Therefore, in this work, we introduce a new Quantum Optimization via Coordinate Descent (QUACOD) approach to address this problem under the constraint of a limited number of available qubits. By leveraging coordinate descent, QUACOD decomposes the original high-complexity problem into multiple subproblems, which are then solved using quantum optimization. In our experiments, QUACOD outperforms the state-of-the-art (SOTA) quantum-based drone scheduling method not only in optimized drone completion times but also in scalability, handling up to 5 times more drones and 35 times more routes. In addition, QUACOD demonstrates that hardware-efficient circuits are effective for optimization problems. Together, these contributions advance quantum computing toward practical applications in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Quantum Criticality in Monolayer Amorphous Carbon](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14349v1)

**Authors:** Rejaul SK, Hanning Zhang, Artem K Grebenko, Arsen Herasymchuk, Ranjith Shivajirao, Hongji Zhang, Abee Nelson, Zheng Jue Tong, Gagandeep Singh, Naoto Kimiuchi, Yuta Sato, Kazutomo Suenaga, Chee Tat Toh, Rudolf A Romer, Shaffique Adam, Oleg V. Yazyev, Barbaros Ozyilmaz, Bent Weber

At higher energies...</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that monolayer amorphous carbon hosts scale-invariant, critical-like electronic states despite being a strictly 2D disordered system. This phenomenon is attributed to an emergent chiral symmetry protected by the topological disorder of the carbon network. The findings challenge the conventional view that all states in 2D are localized by weak disorder.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the nature of electronic localization in a strictly 2D amorphous system and whether topological disorder can prevent Anderson localization at the band center.

**Main result.** The authors discover that monolayer amorphous carbon (MAC) exhibits critical-like extended states near the band center, protected from localization by an emergent chiral symmetry described by a Wess-Zumino-Witten topological term.

**Method.** The researchers combined experimental Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM/STS) and High-Resolution TEM with atomistic tight-binding simulations and multifractal analysis.

**Model / system.** The system is Monolayer Amorphous Carbon (MAC), a 2D continuous random network of sp2-bonded carbon characterized by topological disorder (5-8 membered rings) and a lack of long-range translational symmetry.

**Key observables.** Local Density of States (LDOS) distributions, multifractal singularity spectrum f(alpha), anomalous scaling dimension Delta q, and localization length xi(E).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Energy near the band center (E ~ 0), critical exponent nu approx 0.90, and the scaling relation eta = -Delta2.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the disorder is primarily topological (bond-length/angle variations) rather than random on-site potentials, and proposes a WZW topological term in the effective field theory.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares LDOS maps and structural disorder in graphene vs MAC, showing non-periodic modulations and non-hexagonal rings. Figure 2 demonstrates the transition from Gaussian to log-normal LDOS distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of 2D localization, presents structural and electronic characterization of MAC via STM/TEM, details the multifractal and scaling analysis of wavefunctions, provides theoretical validation via tight-binding simulations, and concludes with a proposed topological mechanism for the observed criticality.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Amorphous solids represent the extreme limit of broken translational symmetry, in which the absence of long-range order removes well-defined crystal momenta and invalidates the Bloch description of electronic states. Monolayer amorphous carbon (MAC) has emerged as a unique realization of a strictly two-dimensional (2D) amorphous lattice defined by a structurally contiguous but topologically disordered $sp^2$-bonded random network devoid of any defined long-range crystal symmetry. From atomic-resolution measurements of multifractal wavefunctions, we show that disorder in MAC effectively localizes the low-energy part of the electronic spectrum but retains an extended critical-like state near the band centre ($E\sim 0$). We conjecture that this state is protected from topological disorder by remnant chiral symmetry surviving within the continuous random network, described by a Wess-Zumino-Witten (WZW) topological term. Near criticality, we verify the multifractal scaling relation $η= -Δ_2$, providing quantitative agreement between independently measured spatial correlation decay and multifractal scaling exponents. Our results are confirmed by atomistic tight-binding calculations that closely mirror the multifractal scaling near $E\sim 0$. Our results establish MAC as the first strictly 2D amorphous electronic system to exhibit Anderson criticality driven purely by topological disorder

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Momentum-resolved spectral functions on both sides of the pseudogap QCP at p∗. (a,b) Non-interpolated DCA spectra Ak(ω) along antinodal and nodal cuts [indicated in (d)], respectively. Columns show dopings below and above p∗≃0.14; the black vertical separator marks p∗. For the antinodal cut (a), the low-energy spectral weight present on the FL side (p &gt; p∗) is strongly depleted on the PG side (p &lt; p∗), whereas for the nodal cut (b) the low-energy spectrum evolves smoothly. (c1,c2) L-interpolated zero-frequency spectral function Ak(0) in the FL at p = 0.155 &gt; p∗and in the PG+ regime at p = 0.125 &lt; p∗, respectively. In (c1), the curved dashed line denotes the locus of maximal Ak(0);...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.15059_figures/2605.15059_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Angular distribution of the zero-frequency spectral weight along the Fermi surface in the PG+ regime at p = 0.125. For each ray ℓθ emanating from (π, π) at a specified angle θ as indicated in the inset, we plot maxk∈ℓθ Ak(ω = 0), normalized by its maximum value. Blue: the direct DCA cluster result, with abrupt steps at the patch boundaries; red: Liouvillian (L) interpolation smooths out the steps.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the transition between the pseudogap metal and the Fermi liquid in the 2D Hubbard model is a continuous quantum phase transition. It shows that this transition is momentum-selective, leading to the formation of Fermi arcs as the antinodal spectral weight is suppressed. The findings provide a theoretical basis for understanding the non-Fermi-liquid behavior seen in cuprate superconductors.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the nature of the normal-state phase diagram in the 2D Hubbard model, specifically whether a quantum critical point (QCP) separates the pseudogap metal from the overdoped Fermi liquid.

**Main result.** The study identifies a continuous quantum phase transition at a critical doping p* = 0.14, characterized by the collapse of the Fermi-liquid scale and the emergence of momentum-selective Fermi arcs.

**Method.** The researchers used the Dynamical Cluster Approximation (DCA) combined with the Numerical Renormalability Group (NRG) as a cluster solver to access real-frequency dynamics at zero temperature.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional square-lattice Hubbard model with parameters U=7t and t'=-0.3t, representing a regime relevant to high-Tc cuprates.

**Key observables.** Dynamical susceptibilities (charge, spin, and d-wave pairing), spectral functions, quasiparticle weights (Z_K), and the Fermi-liquid scale (T_FL).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Doping (p), Hubbard interaction strength (U=7t), next-nearest-neighbor hopping (t'=-0.3t), and critical doping (p*=0.14).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Calculations are restricted to the normal state (excluding symmetry-broken solutions) and use a four-patch DCA cluster configuration.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the zero-temperature phase diagram and static susceptibility peaks; Figure 2 illustrates frequency-dependent susceptibility plateaus; Figure 3 displays cluster spectral functions and vanishing quasiparticle weights; Figure 5 quantifies spectral weight distribution along the Fermi surface.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific problem of the pseudogap transition, details the DCA+NRG computational framework, presents the identified phase diagram and quantum criticality, analyzes momentum-dependent spectral evolution and Fermi arc formation, and concludes with discussion on the robustness of the results.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the normal-state, doping-driven phase diagram of the square-lattice Hubbard model using the dynamical cluster approximation combined with the numerical renormalization group as a cluster solver, which gives direct access to real-frequency dynamics at essentially zero temperature. In a parameter regime relevant for cuprates, $U=7t$ and $t'=-0.3t$, we find a critical doping $p^{\ast}$ that marks a continuous quantum phase transition between a pseudogap metal and a normal Fermi liquid. The transition is identified by a continuous collapse, from both sides, of the Fermi-liquid scale extracted from charge, spin, and $d_{x^2-y^2}$-wave pairing susceptibilities. This collapse produces a non-Fermi-liquid regime at intermediate energy scales, which appears to extend to arbitrarily low scales at $p^{\ast}$. As $p^{\ast}$ is crossed from the normal Fermi liquid at $p>p^{\ast}$ into the pseudogap metal at $p<p^{\ast}$, the coherent low-energy spectral weight in the antinodal region is lost and replaced by a narrow, metallic pseudogap, while the nodal region evolves smoothly and remains comparatively coherent. This gives rise to Fermi arcs in the pseudogap metal at $p<p^{\ast}$, since the zero-frequency spectral weight remains large in the nodal region but is strongly suppressed in the antinodal region.

</details>

**Authors:** Anna Zakharova  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15121v1>

**Summary.** This paper provides a detailed theoretical calculation of the rovibrational spectrum and electric dipole moments of the AcOCH3+ ion. By using relativistic coupled cluster methods and a coupled channel technique, the study identifies key molecular properties essential for future experiments searching for parity and time-reversal symmetry violation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to AMO physics as it provides the necessary molecular spectroscopic data (dipole moments and energy levels) required to implement laser cooling and precision spectroscopy for testing fundamental symmetries.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to investigate the rovibrational structure and electric dipole moments of the AcOCH3+ ion to support searches for fundamental parity (P) and time-reversal (T) symmetry violation.

**Main result.** The researchers computed vibrational frequencies and longitudinal electric dipole moments for various rovibrational states, finding that the longitudinal dipole moment behaves nearly linearly with the internuclear distance.

**Method.** Electronic structure was calculated using the relativistic coupled cluster method [CCSD(T)] via the DIRAC 19 program, while nuclear motion was treated using a coupled channel technique to account for all rovibrational effects and anharmonicities.

**Model / system.** The system is the AcOCH3+ molecular ion, modeled as a symmetric top molecule consisting of a linear rotor (Actinium atom and the center of mass of the OCH3 ligand) coupled to a rigid symmetric top representing the ligand.

**Key observables.** Vibrational frequencies, longitudinal electric dipole moments (d_parallel), and energy levels (E - E0).

**Important parameters / regimes.** l-doublets, K-doublets, equilibrium distance (Re), vibrational frequency (omega), and dissociation energy (De).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The variation of the potential with the angle phi is treated as a perturbation for most properties, and the radial coordinate is discretized using a harmonic approximation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the molecular geometry; Figure 2 shows the adiabatic potential dependence on R; Figure 3 shows the phi-averaged adiabatic potential dependence on theta; Figure 4 shows the azimuthal dependence of the potential and wavefunction extent.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the motivation for P,T-violation searches, describes the molecular model and Hamiltonian, details the relativistic electronic structure and coupled channel methods, presents the calculated energy levels and dipole moments, and concludes with the results.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The possibility of laser cooling and the presence of closely spaced rovibrational doublets make polyatomic molecules an attractive platform for the $\mathcal{P}$, $\mathcal{T}$-violation searches. We study the spectrum of the lowest rovibrational state of the AcOCH$_3+$ symmetric top molecule. The electronic structure full-electron computation was performed within a relativistic coupled cluster method with double and perturbative triple excitations. The rovibrational wavefunctions are obtained using a coupled channel technique, taking into account all rovibrational effects and anharmonicities of the potential. As a result, the vibrational frequencies, as well as the values of the electric dipole moments for the rovibrational states, were computed.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates why some altermagnetic candidates show suppressed spin-band splitting while others show pronounced features. It demonstrates that the competition between electron localization (driven by Hubbard U) and orbital hybridization (driven by ligand p-orbitals) is the decisive factor. These findings provide a roadmap for the rational design of new altermagnetic materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the electronic origins of altermagnetism and determining how strong electron correlations and ligand hybridization influence the emergence or suppression of spin-band splitting.

**Main result.** The study finds that while strong correlations can suppress spin-band splitting by localizing electrons (as in MnF2), robust ligand hybridization can enable significant splitting (as in MnTe), establishing a design principle for new altermagnets.

**Method.** The authors use advanced many-body frameworks, primarily fully self-consistent GW plus extended dynamical mean-field theory (FGW+EDMFT), supplemented by DFT+DMFT and cRPA.

**Model / system.** The study analyzes three prototypical altermagnetic candidates: MnF2, MnTe, and RuO2, utilizing the single-band Hubbard model to describe the interplay between Coulomb repulsion (U) and hopping (t).

**Key observables.** Spin-band splitting, spin-resolved bandwidth, local magnetic moments, spectral functions, and Hubbard bands.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hubbard interaction energy (U), hopping amplitude (t), U/t ratio, and hybridization function (Delta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the validity of the GW+EDMFT framework for capturing nonlocal screening and many-body effects in these specific transition metal oxides and fluorides.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a schematic of correlation and hybridization effects; Figure 2 shows the electronic structure and spectral functions of MnF2; Figure 3 presents spin-resolved spectral functions and local susceptibility for the three materials; Figure 4 visualizes ligand hybridization and orbital-projected DOS.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of altermagnetic detection inconsistencies, presents a theoretical framework (FGW+EDMFT), analyzes three specific material cases (MnF2, MnTe, RuO2) to isolate the effects of correlation and hybridization, and concludes with design principles for future materials.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Spin-band splitting is a hallmark of altermagnetism, intrinsically linked to magnetic ordering driven by electron correlations. However, recent inconsistencies in the detection of altermagnetism in strongly correlated altermagnet candidates have cast doubt on the robustness of this phenomenon and its dependence on many-body effects. Here, using state-of-the-art quantum many-body frameworks, we dissect the electronic origins of altermagnetism in three prototypical candidates: MnF$_2$, MnTe, and RuO$_2$. In MnF$_2$, we identify pronounced local electron correlations within Mn-3$d$ states and uncover a distinct Mott gap in the visible range, rooted in nonlocal screening effects. The strong correlations markedly localize the Mn-3$d$ electrons, leading to a narrowing of the spin-resolved bandwidth and, consequently, a suppression of spin-band splitting. By contrast, MnTe provides an ideal platform for altermagnetism, exhibiting substantial local Mn-3$d$ magnetic moments due to the strong correlations and pronounced spin-band splitting, enabled by robust Mn 3$d$--Te-5$p$ orbital hybridization. RuO$_2$ manifests as a Pauli paramagnet with vanishing local moments, even in its antiferromagnetic phase. Nonetheless, it exhibits significant spin-band splitting, indicative of itinerant altermagnetic behavior. Our results reveal that both strong local electron correlations and judicious ligand selection to promote orbital hybridization are key prerequisites to realizing altermagnetism in strongly correlated systems. These insights pave the way for the rational design and discovery of novel altermagnetic materials.

</details>

**Authors:** M. A. Habitzreuter, Willdauany C. de Freitas da Silva, Rodrigo A. Fontenele, Natanael C. Costa, Thereza Paiva  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14225v1>

**Summary.** This paper explores how various types of electronic interactions and electron-phonon coupling influence the Seebeck coefficient in 2D systems. It demonstrates that these interactions can trigger anomalous sign changes in thermopower by driving phase transitions and restructuring the Fermi surface. The findings provide a deeper understanding of the thermoelectric properties of complex materials like cuprates and transition-metal dichalcogenides.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how electronic interactions beyond the on-site Hubbard model, such as nearest-neighbor interactions, sublattice potentials, and electron-phonon coupling, affect the thermopower (Seebeck coefficient) and lead to anomalous behavior.

**Main result.** Additional interaction scales can enhance the Seebeck coefficient and cause multiple anomalous sign changes linked to gap opening and Fermi surface reconstruction. Electron-phonon coupling can also induce Seebeck anomalies even without on-site repulsion.

**Method.** The study uses Determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) for thermodynamic and transport properties, supplemented by Exact Diagonalization (ED) and Cluster Perturbation Theory (CPT).

**Model / system.** The research focuses on 2D lattice models including the Hubbard, Extended Hubbard, Holstein-Hubbard, and Ionic Hubbard models, incorporating kinetic energy, on-site (U) and nearest-neighbor (V) interactions, and electron-phonon coupling (g).

**Key observables.** Seebeck coefficient (Kelvin formula), entropy, electronic density, charge structure factor, and spectral functions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** On-site interaction (U), nearest-scale interaction (V), electron-phonon coupling (g), staggered potential (delta), temperature (T), and electron density (n).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Trotter decomposition introduces a small discretization error of O(delta-tau^2), which is mitigated by keeping delta-tau <= 0.1.

**Figures summary.** Figures show density and entropy as functions of chemical potential, Seebeck coefficient anomalies across different interaction regimes, phase boundaries for CDW and SDW orders, and spectral functions illustrating Fermi surface reconstruction.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the importance of thermopower in correlated systems, details the various Hubbard-like models and DQMC methodology, presents results for different interaction scales (U, V, g, delta), connects these to gap opening and Fermi surface topology, and concludes with implications for materials like cuprates.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The quest for efficient devices has fueled research in thermoelectric materials. In these materials, the goal is to maximize the Figure of Merit $ZT$. One of the components of this quantity is the Seebeck coefficient, which measures the voltage generated in response to a temperature gradient. Recent studies have revealed that strong electronic correlations can enhance the Seebeck coefficient, leading to anomalous behavior near half-filling. However, the impact of interactions beyond the on-site Hubbard remains mostly unexplored. In this work, we investigate the Seebeck coefficient considering attractive interactions, nearest-neighbor interactions, sublattice potentials and electron-phonon coupling. We find that additional interaction scales can enhance the Seebeck coefficient, while also leading to multiple anomalous changes of sign as a function of doping. We also show that the anomalous behavior is connected to a gap opening in the ground state. Moreover, electron-phonon coupling also lead to a Seebeck anomaly, even without on-site repulsion. We connect these changes of sign in the Seebeck coefficient with a restructuring of the Fermi surface and a change in its topology, an effect commonly seen in cuprates.

</details>

### [Two Protons, Two Positrons, and Four Electrons: Covalent Bond with van der Waals Characteristics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15099v1)

**Authors:** Jorge Charry, Alexandre Tkatchenko  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.15099v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 4 shows the above decomposition for the local variations of the positronic (a) and elec-</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper investigates the bonding nature of the positronium hydride dimer using advanced Quantum Monte Carlo simulations. It reveals that the bond is mediated by a delocalized positron cloud that behaves like a covalent bond but possesses van der Waals-like strength. This discovery suggests a new class of quantum-mediated interactions between particles and antiparticles.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides deep insight into matter-antimatter interactions and the emergence of 'proto-bonds' through quantum correlations, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the fundamental classification of chemical interactions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to resolve the enigmatic nature of the chemical bond in the positronium hydride (PsH) dimer, specifically determining whether the interaction is ionic, covalent, or van der Waals-like.

**Main result.** The researchers find that the bond is characterized by a delocalized molecular orbital of two positrons that envelopes two hydrogen anions, acting as a collective dipole with covalent-like features but a bond strength comparable to van der Waals interactions.

**Method.** The study employs high-precision Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) methods, specifically Variational and Diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC), using an advanced many-body wavefunction ansatz with an extended dynamical Jastrow factor.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the (PsH)2 dimer, composed of two protons, two positrons, and four electrons, modeled within the Born-Oppenheimer approximation using a fermionic Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Binding energy, dipole polarizability (including electronic and positronic components), dispersion coefficients (C6, C8), and electron-positron contact density (annihilation rate).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Internuclear distance (R), equilibrium distance (Req approx 6.0 Bohr), and external electric field strength (F).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the Fixed-Node approximation to handle the fermion sign problem and the assumption that the positronic wavefunction is independent of nuclear positions to reduce computational complexity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares four bonding mechanisms; Figure 2 shows bonding densities across different molecules; Figure 3 plots interaction energies and polarizability vs. distance; Figure 4 illustrates dipole response decomposition and local polarizability; Supplemental figures show Jastrow factor construction and convergence tests.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the bonding puzzle, describes the (PsH)2 system, details the QMC methodology and Jastrow wavefunction construction, presents results on energy and polarizability, and concludes with implications for broader quantum systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Classifying interactions is key in the physical sciences, and bonding mechanisms in matter-antimatter systems remain particularly enigmatic. Here we focus on a paradigmatic example of positronium hydride (PsH) dimer composed of two protons, two positrons, and four electrons, whose bonding nature has been previously described as either ionic, covalent, or van der Waals-like. Accurate quantum Monte Carlo calculations show that the two positrons occupy a delocalized molecular orbital that envelopes the two hydrogen anions and responds as a collective dipole to an applied electric field. This positronic bonding stems from quantum correlations that resemble a single covalent bond formed between negatively charged pseudo-nuclei, but with a bond strength commensurate with the traditional van der Waals interaction. Our findings suggest that the ability to form delocalized proto-bonds is a more general property of quantum systems, and could be present in a broader class of particles, antiparticles, and quasi-particles interacting with matter.

</details>

**Authors:** Natsuto Isogai, Hayata Yamasaki, Sho Sonoda, Mio Murao  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13979v1>

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13979_figures/2605.13979_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">

The experiments use N ∈{8, 16, . . . , 4096}. Curves show the mean over 50 executions, while error bars are the standard error of the mean.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13979_figures/2605.13979_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 1 shows that for all tested dimensions, our sampler closely follows the exact sampler from the optimized distribution. This implies that the proposed rejection sampling preserves the useful bias of the optimized distribution. In contrast, sampling from the non-optimized uniform distribution</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13979_figures/2605.13979_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">

Each curve shows the mean over 20 executions, while error bars are 95% confidence intervals. Fitted curves are scaling guides.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.13979_figures/2605.13979_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">

</figure>

<img src="2605.13979_figures/2605.13979_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a classical algorithm that performs a task previously thought to require quantum advantage: efficiently sampling optimal hidden nodes for sparse neural networks. By exploiting the mathematical structure of the ridgelet transform, the authors achieve polynomial scaling, providing a powerful classical alternative to quantum machine learning approaches for subnetwork selection.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Efficiently identifying sparse, high-performing subnetworks (winning lottery tickets) within large neural networks by overcoming the exponential computational bottleneck of sampling from an optimized probability distribution.

**Main result.** The authors developed a 'dequantized' classical algorithm that achieves polynomial-time scaling in the data dimension D, effectively replacing a previously proposed quantum algorithm without exponential overhead.

**Method.** The method uses a randomized classical rejection sampling algorithm that exploits the isometry structure of the discrete ridgelet transform to avoid dense matrix inversion.

**Model / system.** A discretized single-hidden-layer neural network using a ridgelet-based architecture over a finite field Z_P^D, where the learning task is supervised regression.

**Key observables.** Empirical risk, runtime scaling with dimension D, and total variation distance between sampled and target distributions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Data dimension D, number of training samples M, regularization parameter lambda, smoothing parameter Delta, and precision parameter P.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The efficiency relies on the specific ridgelet structure rather than general sparsity; it assumes parameters are polynomially bounded and uses a discretized domain.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares empirical risk across different dimensions, showing the proposed method tracks exact sampling; Figure 2 demonstrates the reduction from exponential to polynomial runtime scaling.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of sparse subnetwork selection, defines the ridgelet-based mathematical framework, presents the new classical dequantized algorithm and an improved quantum version, provides theoretical proofs for complexity and correctness, and concludes with numerical simulations validating the efficiency and performance.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum machine learning (QML) aims to accelerate machine learning tasks by exploiting quantum computation. Previous work studied a QML algorithm for selecting sparse subnetworks from large shallow neural networks. Instead of directly solving an optimization problem over a large-scale network, this algorithm constructs a sparse subnetwork by sampling hidden nodes from an optimized probability distribution defined using the ridgelet transform. The quantum algorithm performs this sampling in time $O(D)$ in the data dimension $D$, whereas a naive classical implementation relies on handling exponentially many candidate nodes and hence takes $\exp[O(D)]$ time. In this work, we construct and analyze a quantum-inspired fully classical algorithm for the same sampling task. We show that our algorithm runs in time $O(\operatorname{poly}(D))$, thereby removing the exponential dependence on $D$ from the previous classical approach. Numerical simulations show that the proposed sampler achieves empirical risk comparable to exact sampling from the optimized distribution and substantially lower than sampling from the non-optimized uniform distribution, while also exhibiting exponentially improved runtime scaling compared with the conventional classical implementation. These successful dequantization results show that sparse subnetwork selection via optimized sampling can be achieved classically with polynomial data-dimension scaling on conventional computers without quantum hardware, providing an alternative to the existing quantum algorithm.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14767"></a>
### [Optimizing strong light-matter coupling of plasmonic lattices and monolayer semiconductors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14767v1)

**Authors:** Lukas Krelle, Lukas Husel, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Ismail Bilgin, Alexander Högele, Farsane Tabataba-Vakili  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14767v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5** · `Tavis-Cummings & cavity-many-emitter` **3/5** · `correlated cavity matter` **2/5** · `interference shaping light` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Fabrication sequence: (a) mechanical exfoliation of an hBN flake on a SiO2/Si substrate, (b) hBN flake covered with a PMMA film, (c) electron-beam lithography, (d) ICP-RIE process of the lattice, (e) gold deposition and (f) removal of the PMMA film. (g) Schematics of the two sample geometries. (h) Optical micrograph of sample A.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14767_figures/2605.14767_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Monolayer characteristics. (a) Representative PL and (b) Kramers-Kronig phase-corrected DR spectra of sample A with the linewidth indicated by black arrows. In inset in (a) indicates the sample geometry. (c) Distribution of the exciton linewidth and (d) resonance energy in an isolated MoSe2 region on sample A with dashed lines indicating a Gaussian distribution of the resonance energy and arrows marking the standard deviation. (e)-(h) Same as (a)-(d) but for sample B.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2605.14767_figures/2605.14767_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Exciton-plasmon coupling characteristics. (a) Representative momentum-resolved polariton dispersion of sample A in DR with solid lines highlighting the fitted polariton branches and dashed lines indicating the exciton and SLR resonance energies. (b) Distribution of the resonance energy of the A1s exciton and (c) the SLR at k = 0 in sample A with dashed lines indicating a Gaussian distribution and the standard deviation marked by arrows. (d) Distribution of the exciton-plasmon coupling strength in sample A. (e)-(h) Same as (a)-(d) but for sample B.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** The study demonstrates that the spatial arrangement of plasmonic nanodisks relative to a 2D semiconductor layer is critical for maintaining high-quality excitons. By placing the lattice on top of the semiconductor, the researchers reduced strain and disorder, leading to a 25% increase in light-matter coupling strength. This approach enables the creation of more homogeneous and robust polaritonic platforms.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and cavity QED as it demonstrates a practical method to maximize light-matter interaction strength and maintain high-quality resonances in hybrid plasmonic-semiconductor architectures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Optimizing the strength of light-matter coupling in plasmonic lattices integrated with monolayer semiconductors by addressing strain and dielectric disorder caused by fabrication processes.

**Main result.** Placing the plasmonic lattice on top of the semiconductor stack (Sample A) achieves a 25% increase in coupling strength and significantly improved exciton quality compared to a bottom-layer configuration.

**Method.** The researchers used electron-beam lithography, plasma etching, and gold evaporation to embed gold nanodisk arrays into hBN/MoSe2 heterostructures, followed by momentum-resolved differential reflectance and photoluminescence spectroscopy.

**Model / system.** The system consists of gold nanodisk arrays embedded in hexagonal Boron Nitride (hBN) layers coupled to MoSe2 monolayers, modeled using a coupled oscillator Hamiltonian to describe exciton-polariton formation.

**Key observables.** Exciton linewidth, exciton energy, spatial homogeneity (energy and linewidth), coupling strength (g), Rabi splitting, and Surface Lattice Resonance (SLR) Q-factors.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exciton energy (~1.64 eV), coupling strength (23.5 meV in optimized sample), and exciton linewidth (3.0 meV in optimized sample).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the primary degradation in the control sample is due to fabrication-induced strain and surface contamination/doping.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the fabrication sequence and a schematic comparison of the two sample geometries (top-lattice vs. bottom-lattice).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of light-matter coupling in 2D materials, describes the fabrication of two different layer-order heterostructures, presents spectroscopic characterization of exciton quality and coupling strength, and concludes with the implications for large-area polariton lattices.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Exciton-polaritons provide a versatile platform for the study of a wide range of phenomena, including polariton lasers, topological polaritons, and bosonic condensation. Transition metal dichalcogenide monolayers host excitons with large oscillator strength and binding energies constituting a robust matter constituent that forms polaritons from cryogenic to room temperature when embedded in optical microcavities. Plasmonic nanoparticles arranged in lattice geometries offer strong field-confinement and high quality factors. However, the high sensitivity of monolayer excitons to strain and dielectric disorder necessitates encapsulation in atomically flat hBN to ensure a high optical quality, rendering plasmonics more challenging. Here, we employ our recently developed fabrication method for embedding gold nanodisk arrays into van der Waals heterostructures and compare two samples with opposite layer order. We observe that strain and etching-induced surface contamination can reduce the exciton quality and thus the light-matter interaction strength significantly. Our fabrication approach reduces interfacial irregularities and enables homogeneous large-area polariton lattices for a wide range of applications, such as polarization-control or topological polaritonics.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper reports the development of a high-brightness, tunable source of polarization-entangled photons at telecom wavelengths using twisted NbOBr2 van der Waals crystals. By using a 90-degree twisted stack, the researchers achieved entanglement fidelities over 95% and significantly higher brightness than previous 2D material-based sources. This provides a promising new platform for integrated quantum photonic communication networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it presents a new material platform for generating high-fidelity, high-brightness entangled photons at telecom wavelengths using van der Waals engineering.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for scalable, high-brightness, and tunable telecom-wavelength entangled photon sources that are compatible with existing optical fiber infrastructure while overcoming the noise and brightness limitations of current 2D materials like TMDs.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrated a high-brightness, tunable polarization-entangled photon source using twisted NbOBr2 crystals, achieving entanglement fidelities exceeding 95% and a brightness an order of magnitude higher than TMD-based sources.

**Method.** The team used spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) in a 90-degree twisted stack of NbOBr2 crystals, employing below-bandgap excitation and quantum state tomography to characterize the generated Bell states.

**Model / system.** The experimental platform consists of a 90-degree twisted stack of two mechanically exfoliated NbOBr2 van der Waals crystals (approximately 600 nm thick each) on a quartz substrate, utilizing the material's second-order nonlinearity (chi-2) for photon pair generation.

**Key observables.** Entanglement fidelity, coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR), entanglement visibility, photon bunching (g2(0)), and brightness (Hz/mW).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pump wavelength of 725 nm, telecom-range photon generation (~1450 nm), 90-degree twist angle, and a 150 nm detection bandwidth.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The approach assumes that the 90-degree twist is precise enough to induce necessary quantum interference and that below-bandgap excitation effectively suppresses photocarrier-induced noise.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the NbOBr2 crystal structure, the 90-degree twisted stack setup, and the experimental configuration for SPDC; Figure 2 displays SPDC coincidence counts as a function of pump polarization.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of telecom quantum light sources, describes the NbOBr2 material and twisted stacking platform, details the experimental setup for SPDC and characterization, presents the results for polarization selection rules and entanglement fidelity, and concludes with a comparison to existing 2D material technologies.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nanoscale quantum light sources are essential building blocks for integrated quantum photonic systems. Here, we report a wavelength-scale entangled-photon source based on van der Waals-engineered NbOBr$_2$, and benchmark its performance for telecom-wavelength quantum light generation. By exploiting the material's second-order nonlinearity, we generate quantum-correlated photon pairs via spontaneous parametric down-conversion. We then use a 90$^{\circ}$ twisted stacking to induce quantum interference in photon-pair generation, yielding polarization-entangled photons. This approach enables tunability of the quantum optical state via control of the excitation laser polarization. We experimentally obtain entanglement fidelities exceeding 95% for Bell states, along with a high coincidence-to-accidental ratio of $\sim$335, and a brightness approximately one order of magnitude higher than recently reported telecom sources based on transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) 2D materials. These results establish twisted van der Waals engineering as a powerful platform for highly tunable, high-brightness quantum light sources at telecom wavelengths.

</details>

**Summary.** Researchers have developed a method to grow high-quality, ultrathin InGaAs quantum dots in GaAs nanowires by using Antimony as a surfactant to suppress structural defects. This technique enables the creation of reliable single-photon emitters suitable for integration into silicon-based quantum photonic circuits.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a scalable material platform for single-photon sources, which are fundamental building blocks for photonic quantum computing and quantum networks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving precise thickness control and abrupt interfaces for ultrathin axial InGaAs quantum dots in GaAs-based nanowires is hindered by facet-dependent growth dynamics and rotational twin defects.

**Main result.** The use of dilute Antimony (Sb) as a surfactant suppresses rotational twins and promotes axial growth on the (111)B facet, enabling high-quality single-photon emission with g2(0) < 0.4.

**Method.** Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) using catalyst-free selective-area epitaxy, combined with structural analysis (STEM, EDXS) and optical characterization (micro-PL, TRPL, and HBT interferometry).

**Model / system.** Monolithic axial InGaAs(Sb) quantum dot emitters embedded within GaAs-based nanowires grown on Si(111) substrates.

**Key observables.** Second-order photon correlation g2(0), radiative lifetimes, emission wavelength, twin density, and elemental distribution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Sb incorporation of 3-4%, radiative lifetimes as short as 0.51 ns, and twin density reduction from 1.5 nm^-1 to 0.5 nm^-1.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of a rectangular quantum box model with an infinite potential well and effective-mass approximation to estimate the confined bandgap.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Schematic of the QD in a nanowire and the growth concept via Sb-mediated facet engineering. Fig 2: STEM and EDXS analysis showing twin-free structure and In/Sb distribution. Fig 3: CL intensity maps and spectra showing localized emission. Fig 4: Photon statistics and correlation measurements.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the growth challenge, describes the Sb-mediated engineering approach, presents structural verification via microscopy, demonstrates optical single-photon performance, and discusses the implications for scalable quantum photonics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

GaAs-based nanowires hosting active quantum heterostructures provide a promising route toward monolithic integration of single-photon sources on silicon, a key requirement for scalable quantum photonics. However, ultrathin axial quantum-emitter formation is often hindered by facet-dependent growth dynamics and rotational twins, which induce lateral overgrowth and compromise interface abruptness. Here, we develop InGaAs-based quantum emitters by tailoring facet evolution via dilute Sb incorporation, which efficiently suppresses twins and promotes confined axial insertion at the growth-front facet. This approach significantly enhances the probability of obtaining abrupt, few-nanometer-thin quantum dots at the nanowire tip. Single-nanowire optical spectroscopy reveals intense, spatially localized emission from the active region with lifetimes as short as (0.51 $\pm$ 0.02) ns, and second-order photon-correlation measurements consistently exhibit pronounced antibunching with $g^{(2)}(0)<0.4$, confirming single-photon emission. These results establish a strong correlation between twin density and axial heterostructure formation, identifying defect control as a key factor in realizing monolithically integrated nanowire single-photon sources.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.14934"></a>
### [Resonant optical cooling of nuclear spins in case of strong Knight field of photoexcited electrons](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14934v1)

**Authors:** Kirill Kavokin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.14934v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14934_figures/2605.14934_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. A sketch of effective fields and spin components in the two rotating frames: a) the frame rotating along with the Larmor precession of nuclear spins and b) the frame rotating opposite to the Larmor precession. The generated average spin is projected onto the total field in the rotating frame, and then onto the constant effective field in the laboratory frame.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.14934_figures/2605.14934_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Examples of Hanle curves under resonant cooling of nuclear spins for several modulation frequencies correspond- ing to resonance fields Bres for the following parameters of the electron-nuclear spin system: be = 200G, bN T1 T1e = 2000G, S0 = 0.1, B1/2 = 100G. a) negative g-factor of charge carri- ers, b) positive g-factor of charge carriers (the nuclear mag- netic moment is positive for both cases)</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper theoretically explores how a strong, oscillating Knight field from photoexcited electrons can resonantly cool nuclear spins in semiconductors. It demonstrates that this cooling process creates an Overhauser field that alters the Hanle effect, providing a way to identify the sign of the carrier g-factor.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores the non-equilibrium dynamics of spin systems under periodic driving and the back-action of a polarized reservoir (nuclear spins) on the driving species (electrons), which is relevant to open quantum systems and driven many-body dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the theoretical description of resonant cooling of nuclear spins in semiconductors when the Knight field of photoexcited electrons is strong enough to exceed local nuclear dipole-dipole interaction fields.

**Main result.** It is shown that the resulting Overhauser field can significantly modify the shape of Hanle effect curves, and the specific shape of these curves can be used to determine the sign of the charge carrier g-factor.

**Method.** A modified rotating frame method is used to decompose the oscillating Knight field into two components, followed by solving a nonlinear balance equation between dynamic nuclear polarization and spin-lattice relaxation.

**Model / system.** The system consists of a semiconductor nuclear spin system interacting with spin-polarized charge carriers under illumination with helicity-modulated (alternating circular polarization) light.

**Key observables.** Hanle curves (magnetic depolarization of photoluminescence), Overhauser field (BN), and electron spin polarization.

**Important parameters / regimes.** External magnetic field (B), Knight field amplitude (Be) and frequency (omega), nuclear spin-lattice relaxation time (T1), and charge carrier g-factor.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The frequency of the Knight field modulation is assumed to be far from the frequency range of the nuclear spin correlator, and the Knight field is assumed to be large compared to local nuclear fields.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of effective fields in two different rotating frames; Figure 2 displays numerical examples of Hanle curves at different modulation frequencies demonstrating different shapes for positive vs. negative g-factors.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of resonant cooling, describes the physical model and the rotating frame method, presents the mathematical framework for calculating DNP rates, provides numerical results for Hanle curves, and concludes with the implications for determining the g-factor.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Resonant cooling of the nuclear spin system of a semiconductor by spin-polarized charge carriers under pumping with helicity-modulated polarized light is considered theoretically. It is shown that in the case of strong Knight field of charge carriers, exceeding local fields of the dipole-dipole interaction of nuclear spins, the Overhauser field arising as a result of resonant cooling can considerably modify the overall shape of magnetic-field dependences of charge carrier spin polarization, experimentally observed as the Hanle effect.

</details>
